If the constitutional conception of equal protection means anything, it must at the very least mean that a bare congressional desire to harm a politically unpopular group cannot constitute a legitimate governmental interest. The legislative history reveals that the classification was motivated by a desire to exclude hippie communes — a purpose that is not a legitimate government interest.
cases.notes: Moreno is a foundational rational basis with bite case — establishing that bare animus against an unpopular group is not a legitimate government interest. The case established that even under rational basis review, a classification motivated purely by hostility to a politically disfavored group cannot survive equal protection scrutiny. Moreno, Cleburne, and Romer form the trilogy of rational basis with bite decisions.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Colorado's Amendment 2, which prohibited state and local governments from enacting any law protecting gay, lesbian, or bisexual persons from discrimination, violates the Equal Protection Clause under rational basis review. Amendment 2 classifies gay, lesbian, and bisexual persons not to further a proper legislative end but to make them unequal to everyone else — a bare desire to harm a politically unpopular group does not constitute a legitimate government interest. A law declaring that in general a certain class is unequal seems inexplicable by anything but animus.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia argued the majority's analysis was logically incoherent — Colorado's law reflected legitimate moral disapproval of homosexual conduct, and the majority had invented an animus rationale to strike a democratically enacted law it found distasteful.

cases.notes: Romer v. Evans applied Moreno's animus principle to sexual orientation, holding that bare desire to harm gay and lesbian persons is not a legitimate government interest. The decision completed the Moreno-Cleburne-Romer rational basis with bite trilogy and became the foundational case for constitutional protection of gay rights, leading directly to Lawrence v. Texas (2003) and Obergefell v. Hodges (2015). Kennedy's majority declined to identify sexual orientation as a suspect or quasi-suspect classification.

cases.short_name: Harper v. Virginia Board of Elections
cases.full_name: Harper v. Virginia State Board of Elections
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Virginia's poll tax as a condition of voting in state elections violates the Equal Protection Clause. The right to vote is a fundamental political right, preservative of all other rights, and wealth or fee-paying has no relation to voter qualifications. Once the franchise is granted to the electorate, lines may not be drawn that are inconsistent with equal protection.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black argued the Court's invalidation of the poll tax under equal protection was judicial legislation — the original Constitution explicitly permitted property qualifications for voting and the Twenty-Fourth Amendment addressed only federal elections, demonstrating the Framers' intent to leave state voting qualifications to the states.

cases.notes: Harper v. Virginia Board of Elections established voting as a fundamental right whose unequal distribution triggers strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause. The decision struck poll taxes in state elections (the Twenty-Fourth Amendment had already prohibited them in federal elections). Harper is the foundational case for the fundamental rights strand of equal protection doctrine, applied later in Reynolds v. Sims and Shapiro v. Thompson.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Equal Protection Clause requires that the seats in both houses of a bicameral state legislature be apportioned substantially on a population basis. The right to vote is too important in our free, democratic society to be stripped of judicial protection. Legislators represent people, not trees or acres. A citizen, a qualified voter, is no more nor no less so because he lives in the city or on the farm. The dilution of a citizen's vote in one legislative district compared to another is an unconstitutional denial of equal protection.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Harlan argued the Equal Protection Clause was never intended to apply to political apportionment and that the Court's decision was a flagrant violation of the principle that courts should not decide political questions.

cases.notes: Reynolds v. Sims established the one person, one vote principle for state legislative apportionment. The decision required both chambers of state legislatures to be apportioned by population, rejecting the federal analogy that would have allowed one chamber to represent geographic units. Warren later called Reynolds the most important decision of his tenure. The case triggered a massive reapportionment of state legislatures across the country.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: State statutes denying welfare assistance to residents who have not resided within the state for at least one year violate the Equal Protection Clause. The right to travel interstate is a fundamental right protected by the Constitution. A state law that conditions receipt of welfare benefits on prior residence penalizes the exercise of the constitutional right to travel by creating two classes of residents. Such a classification requires and cannot survive strict scrutiny unless shown to promote a compelling governmental interest.

opinions.author: Warren
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Warren argued that the constitutionally protected right to travel should not be derived from equal protection but from the Privileges and Immunities Clause, and that the majority's strict scrutiny analysis was too broad.

cases.notes: Shapiro v. Thompson established that welfare residency requirements that penalize the exercise of the right to travel interstate are subject to strict scrutiny and violate the Equal Protection Clause. The case is the foundation of the fundamental right to travel doctrine and the principle that conditioning government benefits on the relinquishment of a constitutional right triggers heightened scrutiny.

cases.full_name: San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Texas's system of financing public education through local property taxes, which results in wide disparities in per-pupil expenditures across school districts, does not violate the Equal Protection Clause. Education is not a fundamental right under the Constitution. Poverty is not a suspect classification. The Texas system bears a rational relationship to the legitimate state purpose of local control over public schools. The Court defers to legislative judgment on complex social and economic policy questions that do not involve suspect classifications or fundamental rights.

opinions.author: Marshall
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Marshall argued for a sliding scale approach to equal protection scrutiny calibrated to the importance of the interest and the degree of discrimination, and that the Court's refusal to recognize education as a fundamental right ignored its connection to the exercise of explicitly guaranteed constitutional rights like voting and free expression.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brennan argued that wealth discrimination in access to education required at least intermediate scrutiny and that the Texas system failed even that standard.

cases.notes: San Antonio ISD v. Rodriguez is the major limit on the fundamental rights strand of equal protection — holding that education is not a fundamental right under the federal Constitution and that wealth is not a suspect classification. The decision foreclosed federal equal protection challenges to school funding disparities and confined the fundamental rights doctrine to rights explicitly or implicitly guaranteed by the Constitution. Marshall's dissent arguing for a sliding scale scrutiny approach has been influential in academic literature but never adopted by the Court.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourteenth Amendment requires states to license marriages between two people of the same sex and to recognize same-sex marriages validly performed out of state. The right to marry is a fundamental liberty protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, and the Equal Protection Clause reinforces this conclusion. The four principles identified as underlying the right to marry — individual autonomy, a unique two-person union, the safeguards afforded families and children, and the connection to the nation's social order — apply with equal force to same-sex couples.

opinions.author: Roberts
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Roberts argued that the Constitution does not resolve the marriage question and that the majority's decision was an act of judicial will rather than legal judgment — removing the issue from democratic deliberation without constitutional warrant.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia argued the majority's decision represented a threat to American democracy by removing a hotly debated social policy question from democratic resolution and substituting nine lawyers' judgment for that of the people.

cases.notes: Obergefell v. Hodges held that the fundamental right to marry extends to same-sex couples, grounding its holding in both the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment. Kennedy's opinion synthesized the liberty and equality strands of constitutional doctrine. The decision extended the Romer-Lawrence line of gay rights cases and relied heavily on Loving v. Virginia's recognition of marriage as a fundamental right. Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (2022) cast some uncertainty on Obergefell's continued vitality, though Dobbs expressly disclaimed any effect on Obergefell.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Texas statute making it a crime for two persons of the same sex to engage in certain intimate sexual conduct violates the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The liberty protected by the Constitution allows homosexual persons the right to make personal choices regarding their private sexual conduct without government intervention. Bowers v. Hardwick is overruled. The petitioners' right to liberty under the Due Process Clause gives them the full right to engage in their conduct without intervention of the government.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: O'Connor concurred on equal protection grounds rather than due process — the Texas statute applied only to same-sex couples, making it a discriminatory law that could not survive even rational basis review under Romer v. Evans. O'Connor would not have overruled Bowers.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia argued the majority had signed onto the homosexual agenda, had effectively overruled morality-based legislation, and that the majority's logic would invalidate sodomy laws across the country and compel recognition of same-sex marriage — a prediction that proved accurate.

cases.notes: Lawrence v. Texas overruled Bowers v. Hardwick (1986) and held that intimate consensual sexual conduct between adults of the same sex is protected by the liberty guarantee of the Due Process Clause. The decision was grounded in substantive due process rather than equal protection, though O'Connor's concurrence suggested an equal protection path. Lawrence is the bridge case between Romer v. Evans (1996) and Obergefell v. Hodges (2015) in the constitutional protection of gay rights. SCHEMA NOTE: Lawrence explicitly overruled Bowers v. Hardwick (1986) — add bowers_v_hardwick_1986 node and OVERRULES edge when Bowers is added.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Hawaii constitutional provision limiting the right to vote for trustees of the Office of Hawaiian Affairs to persons of Hawaiian ancestry violates the Fifteenth Amendment's prohibition on denying or abridging the right to vote on account of race. The ancestry classification in this context is a proxy for race and functions as a racial classification subject to strict constitutional scrutiny. The Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments apply to all persons regardless of ancestry.
cases.notes: Rice v. Cayetano applied the Fifteenth Amendment to strike a voting restriction based on Hawaiian ancestry, holding that ancestry is a proxy for race in this context. The decision complicated Hawaiian sovereignty arguments and the status of the Office of Hawaiian Affairs. The case is significant for the broader principle that ancestry-based classifications that function as racial proxies are subject to the same constitutional scrutiny as explicit racial classifications.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Equal protection challenges to partisan political gerrymandering are justiciable. The political question doctrine does not bar judicial review of claims that a legislative apportionment plan discriminates against an identifiable political group. However, the Indiana legislative reapportionment plan at issue does not constitute an unconstitutional partisan gerrymander — to succeed, plaintiffs must show both intentional discrimination against an identifiable political group and an actual discriminatory effect on that group's ability to influence the political process as a whole.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: O'Connor argued that partisan gerrymandering claims are non-justiciable political questions and that the plurality's standard for unconstitutional gerrymandering was unworkable.

cases.notes: Davis v. Bandemer held partisan gerrymandering claims justiciable but set a standard so demanding that no plaintiff ever succeeded under it. Vieth v. Jubelirer (2004) produced a plurality that would have overruled Bandemer, and Rucho v. Common Cause (2019) definitively held that federal courts lack jurisdiction over partisan gerrymandering claims, effectively overruling Bandemer's justiciability holding.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A four-justice plurality would hold that partisan gerrymandering claims present a non-justiciable political question because there are no judicially discoverable and manageable standards for adjudicating them. Justice Kennedy concurred in the judgment on the ground that no workable standard had been identified but declined to foreclose the possibility that such a standard might be developed in the future. Davis v. Bandemer's holding that partisan gerrymandering is justiciable was not overruled because Kennedy's concurrence declined to join the plurality's categorical holding.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Kennedy's decisive concurrence left open the possibility that a workable standard for partisan gerrymandering might be developed, preventing the plurality from overruling Bandemer while signaling deep skepticism about justiciability.

cases.notes: Vieth v. Jubelirer produced a deeply fractured Court on partisan gerrymandering. Because Kennedy declined to join Scalia's plurality, Bandemer was not formally overruled. Rucho v. Common Cause (2019) ultimately held that partisan gerrymandering claims present non-justiciable political questions, ending the search for a workable standard that Kennedy had kept open.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Florida Supreme Court's order mandating manual recounts of undervotes in the 2000 presidential election violated the Equal Protection Clause because it lacked uniform standards for evaluating ballots. When a state grants the right to vote on equal terms, it may not value one person's vote over that of another through arbitrary and disparate treatment in the tabulation process. Because there was insufficient time to establish uniform standards before the federal safe harbor deadline, the recount could not continue. The per curiam opinion expressly limited its holding: 'Our consideration is limited to the present circumstances, for the problem of equal protection in election processes generally presents many complexities.'

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Stevens argued the majority's equal protection analysis was unprecedented, that the Court had no basis for stopping the recount, and that the decision threatened public confidence in the judiciary.

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Souter agreed there was an equal protection problem but argued the remedy should be to remand for establishment of uniform standards rather than ending the recount entirely.

cases.notes: Bush v. Gore is one of the most controversial Supreme Court decisions in modern history. The per curiam opinion expressly stated it was 'limited to the present circumstances,' which courts have widely read as a signal that the decision should not be treated as precedent beyond its specific facts. The decision effectively ended the Florida recount and decided the 2000 presidential election. Its equal protection holding — that standardless manual recounts violate equal protection — has rarely been applied by lower courts.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not condition the right to appeal a criminal conviction on the defendant's ability to pay for a trial transcript. Destitute defendants must be afforded as adequate appellate review as defendants who can afford to pay for the trial transcript. There can be no equal justice where the kind of trial a man gets depends on the amount of money he has. The Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses require that criminal procedure be fair to all defendants regardless of wealth.
cases.notes: Griffin v. Illinois is the foundational case for the access-to-courts strand of equal protection doctrine, establishing that states cannot condition meaningful access to criminal appellate review on a defendant's ability to pay. The decision drew on both equal protection and due process, often in combination — a fusion that became characteristic of fundamental rights cases. Douglas v. California (1963) extended Griffin to the right to appointed counsel on first appeal.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state procedure under which appointed counsel is denied to an indigent defendant on a first appeal as of right violates the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment. Where the merits of the one and only appeal an indigent has as of right are decided without benefit of counsel, there is inequality before the law in that an indigent, though entitled to appeal, does not have the right to have a lawyer argue on his behalf. The right to counsel on first appeal is constitutionally required.
cases.notes: Douglas v. California extended Griffin v. Illinois to the right to appointed counsel on first appeal as of right, holding that indigent defendants are entitled to counsel even on appeal. The decision is limited to first appeals as of right — Ross v. Moffitt (1974) held there is no constitutional right to appointed counsel for discretionary appeals or certiorari petitions.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Maryland's maximum grant regulation, which limits welfare benefits regardless of family size, does not violate the Equal Protection Clause. The regulation bears a rational basis to Maryland's legitimate interest in encouraging employment and in allocating limited resources. In the area of economics and social welfare, a state does not violate the Equal Protection Clause merely because the classifications made by its laws are imperfect. The Constitution does not require the state to choose between attacking every aspect of a problem or not attacking the problem at all.

opinions.author: Marshall
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Marshall argued that welfare benefits are essential to survival and that the majority's rational basis analysis was insufficiently rigorous — the case should have received heightened scrutiny because the classification burdened the poor's ability to meet subsistence needs.

cases.notes: Dandridge v. Williams is the major equal protection precedent for the proposition that welfare benefit classifications receive only rational basis review — establishing that economic and social welfare legislation is subject to minimal scrutiny even when it affects the poor. The decision rejected the argument that poverty or welfare receipt should trigger heightened scrutiny, limiting the fundamental rights strand of equal protection. San Antonio ISD v. Rodriguez (1973) relied on Dandridge in rejecting education as a fundamental right.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A California law limiting new residents for one year to the welfare benefits they would have received in their prior state of residence violates the right to travel protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Privileges or Immunities Clause. The right to travel has three components: the right to enter and leave another state, the right to be treated as a welcome visitor in other states, and the right of a new resident to be treated like other citizens of the new state. The third component — the right of the newly arrived citizen — is protected by the Privileges or Immunities Clause, not merely by equal protection.

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Rehnquist argued the majority was wrong to revive the Privileges or Immunities Clause, which had been dormant since the Slaughterhouse Cases (1873), and that the right to travel was adequately protected by equal protection doctrine.

cases.notes: Saenz v. Roe is constitutionally significant for reviving the Privileges or Immunities Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment as an independent source of constitutional rights — the first significant application of that clause since the Slaughterhouse Cases (1873) gutted it. The decision grounded the right to travel in the Privileges or Immunities Clause rather than equal protection, representing a major doctrinal shift in how the right to travel is conceptualized.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Constitution does not confer a fundamental right upon homosexuals to engage in sodomy. The Court declined to announce a fundamental right to engage in homosexual sodomy in the face of longstanding criminal proscription of such conduct. Hardwick's asserted fundamental right — to engage in consensual homosexual sodomy — is facially different from the fundamental rights recognized in Meyer, Pierce, and Griswold. The issue is whether the Constitution confers a fundamental right to engage in consensual homosexual sodomy, and the answer is no.

opinions.author: Blackmun
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Blackmun argued the case was about the fundamental right to be left alone in the most intimate aspects of one's life — not about a 'right to engage in homosexual sodomy' — and that the majority's framing was deliberately degrading.

cases.notes: Bowers v. Hardwick held that the Constitution does not protect consensual homosexual sodomy. The decision was explicitly overruled by Lawrence v. Texas (2003), which held that Bowers was wrong when decided. Bowers is essential for understanding Lawrence — Lawrence's holding is the direct repudiation of Bowers's reasoning and framing. Justice Powell, who provided the fifth vote in Bowers, later said it was his biggest mistake.

cases.short_name: Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization
cases.full_name: Dobbs, State Health Officer of the Mississippi Department of Health, et al. v. Jackson Women's Health Organization et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Constitution does not confer a right to abortion; Roe v. Wade and Planned Parenthood v. Casey are overruled; and the authority to regulate abortion is returned to the people and their elected representatives. Rational-basis review is the appropriate standard for state abortion regulations.
cases.notes: Majority opinion joined by Thomas, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh, and Barrett. Roberts concurred in the judgment but would have taken a narrower approach. Breyer, Sotomayor, and Kagan dissented. The decision explicitly overrules Roe v. Wade (1973) and Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Constitution protects a woman's right to obtain an abortion prior to fetal viability, subject to state regulation that does not substantially burden that right.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by dobbs_v_jackson_womens_health_organization_2022. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.full_name: Planned Parenthood of Southeastern Pennsylvania v. Casey
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The essential holding of Roe v. Wade is reaffirmed: a woman has a right to obtain an abortion prior to fetal viability without excessive government restriction, subject to an undue burden standard.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by dobbs_v_jackson_womens_health_organization_2022. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The exclusion of same-sex couples from marriage does not present a substantial federal question.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by obergefell_v_hodges_2015. One-line summary decision.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute forbidding the use of contraceptives violates the constitutional right of marital privacy, which is protected within the penumbra of specific guarantees of the Bill of Rights.

opinions.author: Goldberg
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Joined by Warren and Brennan; grounded right in Ninth Amendment

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred on due process grounds

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in the judgment

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black and Stewart dissented

opinions.author: Stewart
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black and Stewart dissented

cases.notes: Majority opinion joined by Clark, Harlan, Brennan, White, Goldberg, and Warren. Multiple concurrences reflect different constitutional grounds for the right to marital privacy.

cases.full_name: Cruzan v. Director, Missouri Department of Health
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The United States Constitution does not forbid a state from requiring that evidence of an incompetent person's wishes as to the withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment be proved by clear and convincing evidence, and a state may constitutionally apply such a standard as a procedural safeguard to protect the liberty interest in refusing unwanted medical treatment.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Grounded right to refuse treatment in liberty rather than privacy; emphasized importance of advance directives

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued no constitutional right to refuse treatment exists; matter for states to decide

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued Missouri's clear and convincing standard was unconstitutional as applied; right to refuse treatment is fundamental

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued Missouri's interest in preserving life could not override individual liberty interest

cases.notes: The Court assumed for purposes of this case that a competent person has a constitutionally protected liberty interest under the Due Process Clause in refusing lifesaving hydration and nutrition, but held that an incompetent person does not necessarily possess the same right. The Court rejected the argument that close family members have a constitutional right to exercise substituted judgment for an incompetent patient absent substantial proof that their views reflect the patient's wishes.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute that prohibits the teaching of modern foreign languages in schools to children who have not passed the eighth grade violates the liberty protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause because it is arbitrary and without reasonable relation to any legitimate state purpose, and infringes fundamental rights of parents to control their children's education and of teachers to pursue their occupation.

opinions.author: Holmes
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Holmes concurred in the judgment but expressed reservations about the breadth of substantive due process protection for economic liberty.

cases.notes: The opinion discusses the scope of liberty protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause, including the right of parents to control their children's education and the right of individuals to pursue lawful occupations. The Court rejected the state's police power justification based on promoting civic unity and preventing foreign influence.

cases.full_name: Pierce v. Society of Sisters of the Holy Names of Jesus and Mary
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute that compels all children to attend public schools and effectively prohibits private school education unreasonably interferes with the liberty of parents and guardians to direct the upbringing and education of children under their control, and violates the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: The Court affirmed preliminary injunctions restraining enforcement of Oregon's Compulsory Education Act of 1922. The decision protects parental liberty to direct children's education and the right of private schools to operate. The Court noted that states retain power to reasonably regulate schools, require attendance, set teacher qualifications, and mandate instruction in subjects essential to citizenship.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law limiting the hours of labor in bakeries violates the liberty of contract protected by the Fourteenth Amendment because the regulation lacks a fair, reasonable, and direct relation to public health or the health of bakery employees.
cases.notes: The Court struck down a New York labor law limiting bakery workers to 10 hours per day and 60 hours per week. The opinion establishes that while states possess police powers to regulate health, safety, morals, and welfare, those powers have limits when they interfere with liberty of contract without a substantial and direct relation to a legitimate public health purpose. Lochner Era effectively ended with West Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish (1937) which adopted rational basis review for economic legislation, though Lochner was never formally overruled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute permitting the state to appeal in criminal cases and retry a defendant does not violate the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment when the retrial is conducted free from substantial legal error, because the right to be free from double jeopardy is not a fundamental principle of liberty and justice implicit in the concept of ordered liberty that is binding on the states.

opinions.author: Butler
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's conclusion that state-initiated retrial does not violate fundamental due process protections.

cases.notes: Cardozo articulated the 'ordered liberty' or 'fundamental fairness' test for determining which Bill of Rights protections apply to the states via the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court distinguished between rights that are fundamental to ordered liberty (which bind the states) and those that are merely procedural or not essential to justice (which do not). This case established the selective incorporation doctrine framework, though the specific holding rejected incorporation of the Fifth Amendment's double jeopardy protection. Note: Palko's specific holding that double jeopardy is not incorporated was overruled by Benton v. Maryland (1969). The ordered liberty framework articulated in Palko survived and remains influential.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law that imposes sterilization as punishment for habitual criminals violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment when it creates arbitrary classifications between crimes of intrinsically the same quality, such as distinguishing between larceny and embezzlement, with no rational basis in eugenics or criminal inheritability.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the Oklahoma Supreme Court's 5-4 decision upholding the Habitual Criminal Sterilization Act. The opinion emphasizes that strict scrutiny applies to classifications affecting fundamental rights such as procreation and marriage. The Court distinguished Buck v. Bell on the ground that the Virginia statute in that case had a 'saving feature' (enabling confined individuals to be released), whereas Oklahoma's statute created arbitrary distinctions with no eugenic justification.

cases.short_name: Moore v. City of East Cleveland
cases.full_name: Moore v. City of East Cleveland
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city ordinance that restricts occupancy of a dwelling unit to members of a single family, defined narrowly to exclude certain blood relatives living together, violates substantive due process when applied to prevent a grandmother from living with her son and grandsons who are first cousins.

opinions.author: Powell
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Established that family living arrangements are protected under substantive due process; distinguished Village of Belle Terre on the basis that Belle Terre involved unrelated persons while Moore involved blood relatives.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in the judgment of reversal.

cases.notes: Plurality decision. Powell's opinion (pp. 498-506) announced the judgment; Stevens concurred in the judgment (pp. 513-521). The decision is fractured and does not command a clear majority on the full doctrinal scope, but the reversal itself was the judgment of the Court.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A parent has a fundamental right under the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause to make decisions concerning the care, custody, and control of their children, and a state statute that permits any person to petition for visitation rights at any time based solely on the child's best interest, without according deference to a fit parent's determination and without requiring a showing of harm, violates that fundamental right.

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in the judgment on narrower grounds, finding the statute sweeps too broadly in authorizing any person at any time to request visitation, without deciding whether harm is required or the precise scope of parental rights.

cases.notes: Plurality opinion. Justice O'Connor's opinion joined by Chief Justice Rehnquist, Justice Ginsburg, and Justice Breyer. Justice Souter concurred in the judgment on narrower grounds. The holding reflects what the plurality concluded; Souter's concurrence did not join the full reasoning but agreed the statute was unconstitutional as applied. Thomas, Kennedy, and Scalia also wrote separately concurring in the judgment. Stevens, Kennedy, Scalia, and Thomas each had distinct rationales.

cases.full_name: United States v. Carolene Products Co.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress has constitutional power under the Commerce Clause to prohibit the shipment in interstate commerce of filled milk (skimmed milk compounded with vegetable oil in imitation of milk or cream), and such prohibition does not violate the Fifth Amendment when supported by a rational basis and legislative findings regarding public health.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in result and all of opinion except the Third section regarding statutory characterization and presumption of constitutionality.

opinions.author: McReynolds
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Would have affirmed the lower court judgment.

opinions.author: Butler
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in result but emphasized that facts alleged are not conclusive of guilt and that defendant may introduce evidence at trial to show the statutory declarations lack substantial foundation.

cases.notes: Justice Cardozo and Justice Reed took no part in consideration or decision. The opinion contains the famous Footnote 4, which suggests narrower scope for presumption of constitutionality when legislation appears to violate specific constitutional prohibitions (particularly First Amendment and equal protection) or restricts political processes or targets discrete and insular minorities.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Double Jeopardy Clause of the Fifth Amendment is applicable to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment, and a defendant acquitted of a charge in a first trial cannot be retried on that charge following a successful appeal of a conviction on a different count.
cases.notes: The Court overruled Palko v. Connecticut (1937) and held that the Double Jeopardy Clause applies to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court vacated and remanded for consideration of whether the burglary conviction was affected by the double jeopardy violation.

cases.short_name: West Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish
cases.full_name: West Coast Hotel Co. v. Parrish
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law establishing minimum wages for women is constitutional as a reasonable regulation of liberty of contract adopted for the protection of the community against evils menacing health, safety, morals, and welfare, and the state has a special interest in protecting women against exploitative employment contracts.
cases.notes: Overruled Adkins v. Children's Hospital, 261 U.S. 525. Distinguished Morehead v. New York ex rel. Tipaldo, 298 U.S. 587. Affirmed judgment of Supreme Court of Washington (185 Wash. 581; 55 P.2d 1083).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A minimum wage law for women violates the liberty of contract protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by west_coast_hotel_co_v_parrish_1937. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Evidence obtained by federal officers in violation of the Fourth Amendment through an unreasonable search and seizure of a defendant's home and private papers without a warrant must be excluded from trial, and a defendant has the right to petition for the return of such illegally seized materials.
cases.notes: Establishes the exclusionary rule for federal prosecutions. The Court held that the Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches and seizures by federal officers and that illegally obtained evidence must be excluded. The Court distinguished this case from Adams v. New York on the ground that Adams involved incidental seizure in execution of a lawful warrant, whereas here the seizure was wholly unlawful and warrantless.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourteenth Amendment incorporates the Fourth Amendment's protection against arbitrary intrusion by police as a fundamental right implicit in the concept of ordered liberty, but the Fourteenth Amendment does not require states to exclude evidence obtained by unreasonable search and seizure as the remedy for Fourth Amendment violations.
cases.notes: The Court held that the Fourth Amendment's core protection against arbitrary police intrusion is fundamental and incorporated into the Fourteenth Amendment, but declined to impose the exclusionary rule on the states. The Court emphasized that states may employ alternative remedies such as private actions for damages, internal police discipline, and public opinion.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Evidence obtained by searches and seizures in violation of the Fourth Amendment is inadmissible in state court proceedings, as the exclusionary rule is an essential ingredient of the Fourth Amendment right enforceable against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued against applying the exclusionary rule to the states

cases.notes: Overrules Wolf v. Colorado (1949) on the question of whether the exclusionary rule applies to state prosecutions. The Court held that the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule is constitutionally mandated and must be applied to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to counsel in criminal prosecutions, and states must provide counsel to defendants who cannot afford to retain one.
cases.notes: Landmark decision establishing the constitutional right to appointed counsel for indigent criminal defendants.

cases.full_name: Danny Escobedo v. State of Illinois
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Where the investigation has focused on a particular suspect in police custody, the suspect has requested and been denied an opportunity to consult with his lawyer, and the police have not effectively warned him of his absolute constitutional right to remain silent, the accused has been denied the Assistance of Counsel in violation of the Sixth Amendment as made obligatory upon the States by the Fourteenth Amendment, and no statement elicited by the police during interrogation may be used against him at trial.
cases.notes: The Court held that the right to counsel attaches at the investigatory stage when the focus shifts from a general investigation to an accusatory interrogation of a particular suspect, prior to formal indictment. The opinion emphasizes that the critical stage for legal advice is during police interrogation, not merely at trial.

cases.full_name: Ernesto Miranda v. State of Arizona
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Law enforcement officers must inform suspects of their Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination and Sixth Amendment right to counsel prior to custodial interrogation, and any statements obtained without such warnings are inadmissible in criminal proceedings.

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the majority's decision imposed unnecessary restrictions on law enforcement and that the Constitution did not require warnings before custodial interrogation.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Criticized the majority's approach as going beyond constitutional requirements and imposing prophylactic rules not mandated by the Fifth and Sixth Amendments.

cases.notes: This case established the requirement for Miranda warnings before custodial interrogation. The decision was 5-4, with Chief Justice Warren writing for the majority. Justices White, Harlan, and Stewart dissented.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourth Amendment protects people, not places. Electronic surveillance of a person's words in a telephone booth, where he has a reasonable expectation of privacy, constitutes a search and seizure under the Fourth Amendment, and such surveillance without prior judicial authorization based on probable cause is per se unreasonable.
cases.notes: Overrules the trespass doctrine of Olmstead v. United States and Goldman v. United States. Establishes the reasonable expectation of privacy test for Fourth Amendment protection.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Surveillance without physical trespass and without seizure of material objects falls outside the scope of Fourth Amendment protection because the Fourth Amendment limits only searches and seizures of tangible property.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by katz_v_united_states_1967. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Surveillance conducted without physical penetration of a protected area does not constitute a search or seizure under the Fourth Amendment.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by katz_v_united_states_1967. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A police officer may conduct a limited search of the outer clothing of a person for weapons when the officer has reasonable suspicion, based on articulable facts, that the person may be armed and dangerous, even without probable cause for arrest.
cases.notes: Chief Justice Warren delivered the opinion of the Court. The case establishes the constitutional framework for stop-and-frisk searches under the Fourth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Miranda v. Arizona announced a constitutional rule governing the admissibility of statements made during custodial interrogation in both state and federal courts, and Congress may not supersede this constitutional holding through legislation such as 18 U.S.C. § 3501.
cases.notes: The Court held that Miranda is a constitutional decision that cannot be overruled by statute. The Court declined to overrule Miranda itself, applying stare decisis principles.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The rigid two-pronged test from Aguilar and Spinelli for determining whether an informant's tip establishes probable cause is abandoned in favor of a flexible totality of the circumstances approach, under which a magistrate must make a practical, common-sense decision whether there is a fair probability that contraband or evidence of a crime will be found in a particular place.
cases.notes: The Court declined to address the exclusionary rule modification question under the 'not pressed or passed on below' doctrine. The decision explicitly overrules the rigid application of the Aguilar-Spinelli two-pronged test.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state criminal defendant does not have an absolute right to appointed counsel in all felony cases; the right to counsel depends on the circumstances of the particular case.
cases.notes: Petition for writ of certiorari granted. Court requested counsel to discuss jurisdiction and exhaustion of state remedies. This case was overruled by Gideon v. Wainwright, 372 U.S. 335 (1963).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A defendant's own incriminating statements obtained by federal agents after indictment and in the absence of counsel cannot constitutionally be used as evidence against him at trial, even if obtained through surreptitious means without the defendant's knowledge.
cases.notes: The Court held that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies to post-indictment interrogation by government agents, whether direct or surreptitious. The Court distinguished between the government's right to continue investigation and its right to use the defendant's own statements obtained without counsel present.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to effective assistance of counsel. A defendant claiming ineffective assistance must demonstrate that counsel's performance was deficient and that this deficiency prejudiced the defense such that there is a reasonable probability the result would have been different, applying a standard of reasonably effective assistance with strong deference to counsel's strategic choices.
cases.notes: Established the two-prong test for ineffective assistance of counsel claims: deficient performance and prejudice. Applies to both trial and capital sentencing proceedings.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A warrantless search incident to arrest is limited in scope to the person of the arrestee and the area within his immediate control, and may not extend to other areas of the premises.
cases.notes: Established the constitutional scope of searches incident to arrest under the Fourth Amendment. The Court rejected the broader scope permitted under earlier decisions including Harris v. United States and United States v. Rabinowitz.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule does not bar the use in the prosecution's case in chief of evidence obtained by officers acting in reasonable reliance on a search warrant issued by a detached and neutral magistrate but ultimately found to be invalid.
cases.notes: Established the good-faith exception to the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule. The Court reversed the Court of Appeals' judgment and held that evidence obtained pursuant to a facially valid warrant is admissible even if the warrant is later found to be invalid, provided the officer's reliance on the warrant was objectively reasonable.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A warrantless search and seizure of an automobile may be conducted without a warrant if the seizing officer has probable cause to believe the vehicle contains contraband goods subject to forfeiture, because the mobility of vehicles and the impracticability of obtaining a warrant in such circumstances distinguish them from searches of fixed structures like dwellings.

opinions.author: McKenna
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in the majority opinion

cases.notes: Chief Justice Taft delivered the opinion of the Court. Justice McKenna concurred before his retirement. The decision establishes the automobile exception to the warrant requirement under the Fourth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Where the Government uses a device not in general public use to explore details of a private home that would previously have been unknowable without physical intrusion, the surveillance constitutes a Fourth Amendment search and is presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.
cases.notes: Reversed Ninth Circuit decision affirming thermal imaging search. Established that use of sense-enhancing technology to obtain information about home interior not otherwise obtainable without physical intrusion constitutes a search under the Fourth Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Access to historical cell-site records revealing the location and movements of a mobile phone user constitutes a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant supported by probable cause.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the third-party doctrine should apply to cell-site records and that the majority's approach improperly expanded Fourth Amendment protections.

cases.notes: The Court held that digital location information derived from cell-site records is not subject to the third-party doctrine and requires Fourth Amendment protection. This represents a significant expansion of privacy rights in the digital age.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The temporary detention of a motorist upon probable cause to believe that he has violated the traffic laws does not violate the Fourth Amendment's prohibition against unreasonable seizures, even if a reasonable officer would not have stopped the motorist absent some additional law enforcement objective.
cases.notes: The Court rejected the argument that subjective intent or pretext can invalidate police conduct justified by probable cause in traffic stops. The Court also rejected a balancing test that would vary Fourth Amendment protections based on local law enforcement practices or the minor nature of the traffic violation.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Police generally may not, without a warrant, search digital information on a cell phone seized from an individual who has been arrested, because the privacy interests implicated by cell phone searches substantially exceed those addressed by the search incident to arrest exception, and the government interests identified in Chimel are not furthered by warrantless cell phone searches.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in part and in the judgment; did not join the full majority opinion

cases.notes: Consolidated with United States v. Wurie, No. 13-212. Chief Justice Roberts delivered the opinion of the Court, joined by Justices Scalia, Kennedy, Thomas, Ginsburg, Breyer, Sotomayor, and Kagan. Justice Alito filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. The holding applies to both the Riley and Wurie cases.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A suspect's incriminating statements obtained in violation of the right to counsel, even absent interrogation, are inadmissible in a criminal prosecution.
cases.notes: Habeas corpus proceeding. Federal District Court and Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit ruled in favor of respondent Williams. The case addresses the admissibility of statements obtained in violation of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A post-indictment lineup conducted without notice to and in the absence of the accused's appointed counsel violates the Sixth Amendment right to the assistance of counsel, and courtroom identifications resulting from such a lineup must be excluded from evidence unless the government can demonstrate the identifications were based on sources independent of the tainted lineup.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the Fifth Circuit's judgment and remanded for entry of a new judgment vacating the conviction. The opinion addresses whether courtroom identifications must be excluded when a post-indictment lineup was conducted without counsel present. The case was set for oral argument with Gilbert v. California and Stovall v. Denno, which present similar questions.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Police may not search a vehicle incident to arrest after the arrestee has been secured and cannot access the interior of the vehicle, unless circumstances unique to the automobile context justify the search because it is reasonable to believe that evidence of the offense of arrest might be found in the vehicle.
cases.notes: Affirmed the Arizona Supreme Court's holding that the search-incident-to-arrest exception did not justify the search. Modified the scope of New York v. Belton by limiting vehicle searches incident to arrest. Narrowed New York v. Belton (1981) by holding vehicle searches incident to arrest are not categorically permitted when the arrestee is secured and cannot access the vehicle.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Police officers may approach individuals on buses during scheduled stops to request consent to search luggage, provided a reasonable person would understand that he or she could refuse to cooperate, applying the same standard that governs police encounters in airport lobbies and other public places.
cases.notes: The Court held that the Fourth Amendment permits police encounters on buses under the same reasonable-person standard applicable to airport and train station encounters. The opinion notes that officers advised Bostick of his right to refuse consent and did not threaten him with a weapon.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Police officers conducting a warrantless protective sweep incident to an arrest in a home may do so if the searching officer possesses a reasonable belief based on specific and articulable facts which, taken together with rational inferences from those facts, reasonably warrant the officer in believing that the area swept harbored an individual posing a danger to the officer or others.
cases.notes: Establishes the standard for warrantless protective sweeps incident to arrest in a home under the Fourth and Fourteenth Amendments.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: An anonymous tip that a person is carrying a gun is not, without more, sufficient to justify a police officer's stop and frisk of that person.
cases.notes: The Florida Supreme Court held the search invalid under the Fourth Amendment. The case addresses the reliability requirements for anonymous tips in the context of reasonable suspicion for investigatory stops and frisks.

cases.full_name: Berkemer, Sheriff of Franklin County, Ohio v. McCarty
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Miranda warnings are not required for roadside questioning of a motorist detained pursuant to a traffic stop, as such questioning does not constitute custodial interrogation within the meaning of Miranda v. Arizona.
cases.notes: The Court addressed two questions: (1) whether Miranda applies to custodial interrogation for misdemeanor traffic offenses, and (2) whether roadside questioning during a traffic stop constitutes custodial interrogation. The holding addresses the second question, establishing that routine traffic stops do not trigger Miranda requirements.

cases.full_name: Charles C. Apprendi, Jr. v. State of New Jersey
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment requires that any fact, other than a prior conviction, that increases the maximum prison sentence for an offense must be submitted to a jury and proved beyond a reasonable doubt.
cases.notes: The case addresses the constitutional requirement for judicial fact-finding that increases criminal sentences beyond statutory maximums. The source text is incomplete but clearly establishes the core holding regarding jury trial rights and proof standards in sentencing.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A suspect who is not in custody and not given Miranda warnings must expressly invoke the Fifth Amendment privilege against self-incrimination at the time of questioning to prevent the prosecution from using his silence as evidence of guilt; silence alone, without express invocation, does not invoke the privilege.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Established that express invocation of the Fifth Amendment privilege is required in precustodial interrogation; silence alone does not invoke the privilege.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that Griffin v. California should not extend to precustodial silence and that the prosecutor's comments did not compel self-incriminating testimony.

cases.notes: Fractured decision. Alito's plurality (joined by Roberts and Kennedy) announced the judgment and the express invocation requirement. Thomas (joined by Scalia) concurred in the judgment but on different grounds, arguing Griffin v. California should not apply to precustodial silence. No single rationale commanded a majority of the Court.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When an occupant of an automobile is subjected to a lawful custodial arrest, the constitutionally permissible scope of a search incident to that arrest includes the passenger compartment of the automobile in which the arrestee was riding.
cases.notes: The opinion addresses the scope of search incident to arrest in the context of automobile searches. The text provided is incomplete (ends mid-sentence in the Appellate Division reasoning), so the full holding and any concurrences or dissents cannot be fully determined from this excerpt.

cases.full_name: Ralph Howard Blakely, Jr. v. State of Washington
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A defendant's Sixth Amendment right to trial by jury is violated when a judge imposes a sentence exceeding the statutory maximum based on facts not admitted by the defendant or found beyond a reasonable doubt by a jury.
cases.notes: The case addresses judicial fact-finding at sentencing and its interaction with the Sixth Amendment jury trial right. Petitioner pleaded guilty to second-degree kidnaping with firearm and domestic violence allegations. The trial court imposed an exceptional sentence of 90 months (above the 49-53 month standard range) based on a judicial finding of deliberate cruelty, a fact not admitted in the plea.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Federal Sentencing Guidelines as written violate the Sixth Amendment under Blakely v. Washington because they require judges to find facts that increase sentences beyond the maximum permitted by the jury verdict alone; as a remedy, the mandatory provisions of the Guidelines are severed and excised, making the Guidelines advisory.
cases.notes: Fractured decision with two majority opinions: Stevens (merits, 5-4) holding the Guidelines violate the Sixth Amendment, and Breyer (remedy, 5-4) making the Guidelines advisory rather than striking them entirely.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not use peremptory challenges to strike prospective jurors solely on account of their race, as such practice violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: The source text provided appears to be from a different case (FTC v. Indiana Dentists) but is labeled as Batson v. Kentucky. The holding extracted reflects the actual Batson v. Kentucky decision regarding peremptory challenges and equal protection, as this is the case identified by the case ID and citation.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A criminal defendant has standing under the Equal Protection Clause to object to race-based exclusions of jurors through peremptory challenges, even when the defendant and the excluded jurors are of different races.
cases.notes: Extended Batson v. Kentucky to allow white defendants to raise Batson challenges on behalf of Black jurors excluded by peremptory challenges.

cases.full_name: J.E.B. v. Alabama ex rel. T.B.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Equal Protection Clause prohibits the use of peremptory challenges to exclude jurors on the basis of sex, just as it prohibits race-based peremptory challenges under Batson v. Kentucky.
cases.notes: Extended Batson v. Kentucky to prohibit gender-based peremptory challenges under the Equal Protection Clause.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The prosecution's suppression of evidence favorable to the accused that is material to guilt or punishment violates the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: Opinion written by Justice Douglas and announced by Justice Brennan. The Court held that suppression of exculpatory evidence by the prosecution violates due process. The case established the foundational rule that prosecutors must disclose material exculpatory evidence to the defense.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The prosecution's failure to disclose a promise of leniency made to a key government witness violates due process under Brady v. Maryland because such evidence is material to the credibility of the witness and therefore to the defendant's guilt or punishment.
cases.notes: Extended Brady v. Maryland to require disclosure of impeachment evidence, including promises of leniency made to government witnesses. Established that materiality of Brady evidence includes evidence affecting witness credibility.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The admission of eyewitness identification evidence obtained under unnecessarily suggestive circumstances does not violate due process if the identification is reliable under the totality of circumstances, assessed by weighing the corrupting effect of the suggestive procedure against indicia of reliability.
cases.notes: Source text is limited to procedural order and does not contain substantive opinion content. Full opinion details unavailable from provided excerpt.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: An eyewitness identification obtained through a suggestive show-up procedure does not violate due process when, under the totality of the circumstances, there is no substantial likelihood of misidentification.
cases.notes: Establishes the totality of circumstances test for eyewitness identification. Justice Marshall did not participate. Manson v. Brathwaite (1977) later refined the Neil factors into the explicit five-factor reliability test.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The right to a speedy trial under the Sixth Amendment requires courts to apply a balancing test considering the length of delay, the reason for delay, the defendant's assertion of the right, and prejudice to the defendant, rather than a fixed time standard.
cases.notes: The Court established criteria for evaluating speedy trial claims, addressing a gap left by prior cases like Klopfer v. North Carolina (1967), which had established the right as fundamental but had not articulated the standard of review.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A criminal defendant's exercise of peremptory challenges in a racially discriminatory manner violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Chief Justice Rehnquist filed a concurring opinion.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Justice Thomas filed an opinion concurring in the judgment.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Justice O'Connor filed a dissenting opinion.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Justice Scalia filed a dissenting opinion.

cases.notes: Majority comprised Justices Blackmun, Rehnquist, White, Stevens, Kennedy, and Souter (6 justices). The Court extended the prohibition on race-based peremptory challenges from civil litigants (Edmonson v. Leesville Concrete Co.) to criminal defendants.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A police officer's coercive interrogation without Miranda warnings does not violate the Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination unless the statements are actually used in a criminal prosecution against the suspect.
cases.notes: Plurality decision. Justice Thomas announced the judgment. The case involved a § 1983 suit challenging interrogation practices. Martinez was never charged and his statements were never used in criminal prosecution. The Court held that absent use of statements in a criminal case, no Fifth Amendment violation occurred.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Courts of appeals must review all sentences—whether inside, just outside, or significantly outside the Federal Sentencing Guidelines range—under a deferential abuse-of-discretion standard, and the extent of difference from the Guidelines range is relevant but does not trigger a proportionality requirement.
cases.notes: Decided same day as Rita v. United States, 551 U.S. 338 (2007). Addressed the standard for appellate review of sentences below the Federal Sentencing Guidelines range, a question left open when Claiborne v. United States was mooted by the defendant's death.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The plain view doctrine may not be invoked when police have less than probable cause to believe that an item is evidence of a crime or contraband; a warrantless seizure of an item observed during a lawful search requires probable cause, not mere suspicion.
cases.notes: The Court addressed the scope of the plain view doctrine established in Coolidge v. New Hampshire (1971), holding that probable cause is a prerequisite to seizure under that doctrine.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourth Amendment does not permit the seizure of contraband detected through a police officer's sense of touch during a protective patdown search when the officer's manipulation of the object goes beyond what is necessary to determine whether it is a weapon.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate concurring opinion

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part

cases.notes: Justice White delivered the opinion of the Court for Parts I and II (unanimous) and Parts III and IV (joined by Stevens, O'Connor, Scalia, Kennedy, and Souter). Chief Justice Rehnquist filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part, joined by Blackmun and Thomas. Justice Scalia filed a separate concurring opinion.

cases.full_name: Goldberg, Commissioner of Social Services of the City of New York v. Kelly et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state must provide an evidentiary hearing prior to terminating public assistance payments to a recipient, as required by the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: The case addresses procedural due process requirements for termination of public assistance benefits under AFDC and New York State's general Home Relief program.

cases.full_name: Board of Regents of State Universities v. Roth
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A nontenured public employee has no constitutionally protected property interest in continued employment absent a legitimate claim of entitlement to reemployment created by state law, statute, or mutually explicit understandings, and therefore is not entitled to procedural due process protections before nonrenewal of a fixed-term contract.
cases.notes: The case addresses the threshold question of whether a property interest exists before procedural due process protections apply. The Court distinguishes between liberty interests (which may be implicated by damage to reputation) and property interests (which require a legitimate claim of entitlement).

cases.full_name: Mathews, Secretary of Health, Education, and Welfare v. Eldridge
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment requires that prior to termination of Social Security disability benefits, the recipient must be afforded an opportunity for an evidentiary hearing.
cases.notes: Case addresses procedural due process requirements for termination of Social Security disability benefits. The opinion establishes a framework for analyzing what process is due in administrative proceedings.

cases.short_name: Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill
cases.full_name: Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public employee who can be discharged only for cause is entitled to notice of the charges and an opportunity to respond before termination, even if a full post-termination hearing is provided later.
cases.notes: The case addresses the pretermination process required by the Due Process Clause for public employees with property interests in continued employment.

cases.full_name: Hamdi et al. v. Rumsfeld, Secretary of Defense, et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Although Congress authorized the detention of combatants in the narrow circumstances alleged, due process demands that a citizen held in the United States as an enemy combatant be given a meaningful opportunity to contest the factual basis for that detention before a neutral decisionmaker.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Announced judgment of the Court; established the due process requirement for enemy combatant detention review

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in part, dissented in part, and concurred in the judgment; argued detention was unauthorized but joined plurality on remand procedures

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissented from the plurality's approach to enemy combatant detention

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissented from the plurality's approach to enemy combatant detention

cases.notes: Fractured decision. Plurality opinion by O'Connor (joined by Rehnquist, Kennedy, Breyer) announced the judgment. Souter and Ginsburg concurred in part, dissented in part, and concurred in the judgment. Scalia and Stevens dissented. Thomas dissented separately. The holding reflects what the plurality and the Souter-Ginsburg group agreed upon regarding the meaningful opportunity to contest detention.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress possesses the implied power to incorporate a national bank under the Necessary and Proper Clause, and a state may not tax an instrumentality of the federal government.
cases.notes: Chief Justice Marshall delivered the opinion of the Court. The case addresses the scope of federal enumerated powers and the doctrine of implied powers under the Necessary and Proper Clause.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: State laws granting exclusive navigation rights for steamboats are repugnant to the Commerce Clause insofar as they prohibit vessels licensed under federal law for the coasting trade from navigating state waters.
cases.notes: The source text is a syllabus and procedural summary. The majority author is not identified in the provided excerpt. The case establishes the supremacy of federal commerce power over state regulation of interstate commerce.

cases.full_name: Wickard, Secretary of Agriculture, et al. v. Filburn
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress has authority under the Commerce Clause to regulate wheat production for home consumption when such production, in the aggregate, substantially affects interstate commerce.
cases.notes: Landmark Commerce Clause case establishing that intrastate activity affecting interstate commerce may be regulated by Congress. Appellee challenged wheat marketing quota provisions as beyond Commerce Clause authority and violative of Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause.

cases.full_name: Katzenbach, Acting Attorney General, et al. v. McClung et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 is constitutional as applied to restaurants, and the District Court's injunction restraining enforcement of the Act against the restaurant was improper.
cases.notes: Decided same date as Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States (No. 515), which upheld Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 as applied to hotels and motels. This case extends that holding to restaurants.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Gun-Free School Zones Act of 1990 exceeds the authority of Congress under the Commerce Clause because it neither regulates a commercial activity nor contains a requirement that the possession be connected in any way to interstate commerce.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Joined by O'Connor; provided additional reasoning on Commerce Clause limits

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate concurrence on Commerce Clause interpretation

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with Commerce Clause analysis

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with Commerce Clause analysis

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with Commerce Clause analysis

cases.notes: Majority opinion by Chief Justice Rehnquist joined by O'Connor, Scalia, Kennedy, and Thomas. Kennedy and Thomas filed separate concurrences. Stevens, Souter, and Breyer filed dissents, with Breyer's dissent joined by Stevens, Souter, and Ginsburg.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress lacked constitutional authority under the Commerce Clause and Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment to enact 42 U.S.C. § 13981, which provided a federal civil remedy for victims of gender-motivated violence.
cases.notes: The Court affirmed the Fourth Circuit's decision striking down § 13981. The opinion indicates the case is controlled by United States v. Lopez (1995), United States v. Harris (1883), and the Civil Rights Cases (1883).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress may encourage States to provide for disposal of radioactive waste generated within their borders through conditional spending and other incentives, but the Constitution does not permit Congress to compel States to enact or administer a federal regulatory program, and a provision requiring States to take title to radioactive waste violates the Tenth Amendment.
cases.notes: The Court addressed the constitutionality of three provisions of the Low-Level Radioactive Waste Policy Amendments Act of 1985. The opinion establishes the principle that Congress cannot commandeer state legislatures or executive officials to implement federal regulatory programs.

cases.full_name: Printz, Sheriff/Coroner, Ravalli County, Montana v. United States
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Brady Act's interim provisions commanding state and local law enforcement officers to conduct background checks on prospective handgun purchasers violate the Constitution by commandeering state officials to administer a federal regulatory program.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurring in the judgment

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurring in the judgment

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's anti-commandeering holding

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate dissent on the commandeering issue

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate dissent on the commandeering issue

cases.notes: The case involved the Brady Handgun Violence Prevention Act's interim provisions requiring state and local law enforcement to conduct background checks on handgun purchasers. The Court held that Congress cannot commandeer state officials to administer federal regulatory programs.

cases.full_name: Shelby County, Alabama v. Eric H. Holder, Jr., Attorney General
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Section 4 of the Voting Rights Act of 1965, which provides the coverage formula determining which jurisdictions are subject to the preclearance requirement of Section 5, is unconstitutional because it is based on outdated data and fails to account for the significant progress in eliminating racial discrimination in voting.
