cases.notes: The text provided is incomplete (cuts off mid-sentence in the syllabus). Vote count and complete opinion information not available in the provided excerpt.

cases.full_name: South Dakota v. Dole, Secretary of Transportation
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress may condition the receipt of federal highway funds on a state's adoption of a minimum drinking age of 21 years, even though the Twenty-first Amendment reserves broad powers to the states over alcoholic beverages, because the spending power is not limited by the Twenty-first Amendment and the condition is reasonably related to the federal interest in highway safety.
cases.notes: Chief Justice Rehnquist delivered the opinion of the Court. The case addresses the scope of Congress's spending power and its relationship to the Twenty-first Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When police conduct a search incident to an arrest based on a reasonable but mistaken belief in an outstanding warrant caused by isolated negligence in police recordkeeping, the exclusionary rule does not automatically apply; instead, courts must consider the culpability of the police and the potential deterrent effect of exclusion.
cases.notes: The Court held that suppression is not an automatic consequence of a Fourth Amendment violation, but rather depends on police culpability and deterrent effect.

cases.full_name: Booker Hudson v. State of Michigan
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Violation of the knock-and-announce rule does not require suppression of all evidence found in the search.
cases.notes: Justice Scalia delivered the opinion of the Court except as to Part IV. The case addresses whether violation of the knock-and-announce rule, a Fourth Amendment protection established in Wilson v. Arkansas, requires suppression of evidence.

cases.full_name: Utah v. Edward Joseph Strieff, Jr.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When an officer makes an unconstitutional investigatory stop but discovers during that stop that the suspect is subject to a valid, pre-existing, and untainted arrest warrant, the discovery of that warrant attenuates the connection between the unlawful stop and evidence seized incident to a lawful arrest based on the warrant, making the evidence admissible under the attenuation doctrine.

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argues that the attenuation doctrine should not apply when an officer exploits an illegal stop to discover a warrant, and that the exclusionary rule should bar admission of evidence obtained through such exploitation.

opinions.author: Kagan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissents from the majority's application of the attenuation doctrine.

cases.notes: Majority opinion joined by Roberts, Kennedy, Breyer, and Alito. Sotomayor's dissent joined by Ginsburg as to Parts I, II, and III. Kagan's dissent joined by Ginsburg.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Government's attachment of a GPS device to a vehicle and use of that device to monitor the vehicle's movements constitutes a search under the Fourth Amendment when the Government physically intrudes on an effect for the purpose of obtaining information.
cases.notes: The Court held that the Fourth Amendment protects against physical intrusions on enumerated effects, consistent with common-law trespass principles. The opinion notes that later cases have applied Justice Harlan's reasonable expectation of privacy analysis from Katz v. United States, but the Court did not need to address the Government's contention regarding that framework in this case.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The natural dissipation of alcohol in the bloodstream does not constitute an exigency in every case sufficient to justify conducting a warrantless blood test in drunk-driving investigations; a warrantless search of the person is reasonable only if it falls within a recognized exception to the warrant requirement.
cases.notes: Justice Sotomayor delivered the opinion of the Court with respect to Parts I, II-A, II-B, and IV. The opinion form indicates a signed majority opinion, though the text suggests potential fractured reasoning on some issues.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When the registered owner of a vehicle has a revoked license, a police officer who lacks information negating the inference that the owner is the driver has reasonable suspicion to conduct an investigatory traffic stop.
cases.notes: Clarified the reasonable suspicion standard for traffic stops based on vehicle registration information.

cases.short_name: Penn Central v. New York City
cases.full_name: Penn Central Transportation Co. v. New York City
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city may restrict development of individual historic landmarks as part of a comprehensive historic preservation program without effecting a taking requiring just compensation under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments.
cases.notes: Appeal from the Court of Appeals of New York. The case addresses whether application of New York City's Landmarks Preservation Law to Grand Central Terminal constitutes a taking under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments.

cases.short_name: Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council
cases.full_name: David H. Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When a state regulation deprives a property owner of all economically beneficial use of land, it constitutes a taking of private property requiring just compensation under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments, unless the proscribed use interests were not part of the owner's title to begin with.
cases.notes: The case addresses the takings clause and establishes that total deprivation of economic value triggers heightened scrutiny, though the state may defend by showing the proscribed use was not part of the owner's title to begin with.

cases.short_name: Kelo v. City of New London
cases.full_name: Susette Kelo v. City of New London
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fifth Amendment's takings clause permits a city to exercise eminent domain to seize private property for transfer to private developers as part of an economic development plan, because such taking qualifies as a 'public use' or 'public purpose' even though the property will not be used by the general public.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's broad interpretation of 'public use' in the takings clause.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The majority rejected a requirement of 'literal' public use, adopting instead a broader interpretation of 'public purpose' that defers to legislative judgments about economic development.

cases.short_name: Loretto v. Teleprompter Manhattan CATV Corp.
cases.full_name: Jean Loretto v. Teleprompter Corp. and Teleprompter Manhattan CATV Corp.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A minor but permanent physical occupation of an owner's property authorized by government constitutes a taking of property for which just compensation is due under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments.
cases.notes: The case addresses whether a state-authorized permanent physical occupation of private property constitutes a taking requiring just compensation. New York law required landlords to permit cable television companies to install facilities on their property.

cases.short_name: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer
cases.full_name: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. et al. v. Sawyer
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The President lacks constitutional authority to seize private property to avert an economic emergency absent express congressional authorization, as such seizure constitutes an exercise of legislative power reserved to Congress.
cases.notes: The case addresses the scope of presidential power during national emergencies and the separation of powers doctrine. The source text is incomplete but establishes the core holding regarding presidential authority limits.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The appointment of an independent counsel under the Ethics in Government Act does not violate the Appointments Clause, the allocation of powers to the special court does not violate Article III, and the Act does not impermissibly interfere with Executive Branch functions under the separation of powers doctrine.
cases.notes: The Court upheld the constitutionality of the independent counsel statute on multiple grounds: Appointments Clause, Article III, and separation of powers.

cases.full_name: Immigration and Naturalization Service v. Chadha
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The one-house legislative veto provision of the Immigration and Nationality Act is unconstitutional because it violates the bicameralism and presentment requirements of Article I — Congress may not bypass the President's veto power by authorizing one house to overturn executive action.
cases.notes: Invalidated the legislative veto device used in over 200 federal statutes. The decision reshaped the relationship between Congress and the executive branch by requiring full bicameralism and presentment for any action with the force of law.

cases.short_name: Clinton v. City of New York
cases.full_name: Clinton v. City of New York
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Line Item Veto Act is unconstitutional because it grants the President the power to unilaterally cancel individual items in enacted legislation, in violation of the Presentment Clause of Article I, which requires legislation to be passed by both houses and signed in full by the President.
cases.notes: Struck down the Line Item Veto Act of 1996. The decision reaffirmed that the Presentment Clause requires the President to either sign or veto legislation in its entirety.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law that imposes a substantial burden on interstate commerce is unconstitutional unless the burden is clearly excessive in relation to the putative local benefits.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the district court's injunction against the Arizona Fruit and Vegetable Standardization Act provision requiring cantaloupes to be packed in closed standard containers. The case involved a dormant Commerce Clause challenge to a state law affecting interstate commerce in agricultural products.

cases.full_name: City of Philadelphia et al. v. New Jersey et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law that prohibits the importation of most solid or liquid waste originating outside the state violates the Commerce Clause when it discriminates against interstate commerce, even if justified by health and environmental concerns.
cases.notes: The New Jersey Supreme Court had reversed the trial court's judgment, finding the statute constitutional. The U.S. Supreme Court reversed the state court decision, holding the statute unconstitutional under the Commerce Clause.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: State laws that discriminate against out-of-state wineries by restricting direct shipment while allowing in-state wineries to ship directly to consumers violate the dormant Commerce Clause and are not saved by the 21st Amendment, which does not authorize economic discrimination among states.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's interpretation of the 21st Amendment's scope to authorize state alcohol regulation.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The Court reversed both the Sixth Circuit (which had ruled for the challenger in the Michigan case) and the Second Circuit (which had ruled for New York). The holding establishes that the 21st Amendment does not provide blanket authority for states to discriminate economically against out-of-state commerce in alcohol.

cases.full_name: Seminole Tribe of Florida v. Florida
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress lacks power under the Indian Commerce Clause to abrogate state sovereign immunity, and the Eleventh Amendment shields states from suit by Indian tribes in federal court for violations of the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act absent state consent.
cases.notes: The Court held that Congress did not have the power under the Indian Commerce Clause to abrogate state sovereign immunity under the IGRA. The decision also addressed the limits of Ex Parte Young in the context of IGRA provisions.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress may not use its Article I powers to abrogate the states' sovereign immunity in their own courts; states retain sovereign immunity from private suits in state court despite the supremacy of federal law when the national government exercises its enumerated powers.

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's extension of sovereign immunity to state courts and its interpretation of the Eleventh Amendment and constitutional structure.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The majority held that states retain sovereign immunity in their own courts based on the Eleventh Amendment, the original understanding of constitutional structure, and the Tenth Amendment. This case extended sovereign immunity doctrine beyond federal court (as established in Seminole Tribe v. Florida) to state courts.

cases.short_name: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp.
cases.full_name: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A plaintiff challenging a zoning decision under the Equal Protection Clause must prove that the decision was motivated by a discriminatory purpose, not merely that it had a discriminatory effect; discriminatory intent is a necessary element of an equal protection violation in the land use context.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the Court of Appeals' finding that the discriminatory effect of the rezoning denial violated the Fourteenth Amendment, establishing that discriminatory purpose is required.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress has authority under the Fourteenth Amendment to abrogate state sovereign immunity through Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act as applied to access to courts and the exercise of fundamental rights protected by the Due Process Clause, where Congress has sufficiently demonstrated the problems faced by disabled persons and the remedies are reasonable prophylactic measures reasonably targeted to a legitimate end.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's application of congruence and proportionality analysis to Title II's access provisions

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. Affirmed Sixth Circuit. Distinguished Alabama v. Garrett (2001) on the basis that Title II addresses Due Process violations rather than equal protection violations.

cases.short_name: Nevada Department of Human Resources v. Hibbs
cases.full_name: Nevada Department of Human Resources v. Hibbs
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: State employees may recover money damages in federal court for a state's failure to comply with the Family and Medical Leave Act's family-care provision because Congress clearly stated its intention to abrogate the States' Eleventh Amendment immunity and acted within its authority under Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment by enacting prophylactic legislation addressing gender-based discrimination in the administration of leave benefits.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's Section 5 analysis and Eleventh Amendment abrogation rationale

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's Section 5 analysis and Eleventh Amendment abrogation rationale

cases.notes: The Court held that Congress validly abrogated state sovereign immunity under the Fourteenth Amendment Section 5 by enacting the FMLA's family-care provision as prophylactic legislation addressing documented gender-based discrimination in leave administration.

cases.full_name: Menachem Binyamin Zivotofsky v. John F. Kerry
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The President possesses exclusive constitutional power to recognize foreign nations, derived from the Reception Clause and other Article II powers, and Congress cannot enact legislation that contradicts the President's recognition decisions, including by mandating passport designations that conflict with Executive branch policy on foreign sovereignty.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Breyer concurred in the judgment, noting the case presented a political question but joined the majority because precedent precluded political resolution.

opinions.author: Roberts
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Roberts argued the President must be exercising a power that the Constitution conclusively and preclusively granted to the Executive branch to ignore Congress; history and precedent on recognition power are conflicting, and an optional passport designation does not constitute official recognition.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia argued that even if the President has recognition power, it is not exclusive because Congress has authority to regulate commerce with foreign nations; the statute merely directs geographic description accommodation, not recognition.

cases.notes: The Court split 6-3 on the passport issue but 5-4 on the consular reports issue. Justice Thomas joined the dissenters on the consular reports question, arguing the statute unconstitutionally usurped recognition power as to passports but not consular reports. The holding reflects the 6-3 majority on the primary passport question.

cases.full_name: Williamson, Attorney General of Oklahoma, et al. v. Lee Optical of Oklahoma, Inc., et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may regulate the practice of optometry and the fitting of lenses through licensing requirements and restrictions on who may perform such services, and such economic and social legislation is subject to rational basis review under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: The District Court held certain provisions of an Oklahoma law unconstitutional under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment. The case involved challenges to state licensing and practice restrictions for optometrists and optical services.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A federal statute criminalizing the commercial creation, sale, or possession of depictions of animal cruelty is substantially overbroad and therefore facially invalid under the First Amendment; the government cannot establish a new category of unprotected speech based solely on a cost-benefit analysis of the speech's value.
cases.notes: Struck down 18 U.S.C. § 48. The Court rejected the government's argument that courts could balance the value of speech against its societal costs to create new categories of unprotected speech.

cases.short_name: First English Evangelical Lutheran Church v. County of Los Angeles
cases.full_name: First English Evangelical Lutheran Church v. County of Los Angeles
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When a land-use regulation is determined to constitute a taking of property under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments, the government must provide compensation for the period during which the regulation was in effect before such determination, not merely prospective relief.
cases.notes: The Court rejected the California Court of Appeal's holding that damages for the period before final determination of a taking are unavailable. The opinion establishes that compensation must be available for the interim period when a regulation is later found to be a taking.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A government may condition development permits on exactions only where the exaction substantially advances a legitimate government interest that would justify denial of the permit entirely; an exaction that does not substantially advance such an interest constitutes a regulatory taking requiring just compensation under the Fifth Amendment.
cases.notes: The Court held that the California Coastal Commission's condition requiring public beach access as a condition of a building permit was an uncompensated taking because it did not substantially advance the government's stated interest in overcoming psychological barriers to beach access.

cases.full_name: Florence Dolan v. City of Tigard
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city may not condition approval of a building permit on the dedication of property for public use unless there is an essential nexus between the legitimate state interest and the permit condition, and the degree of the exaction is roughly proportional to the impact of the proposed development.
cases.notes: Resolves the question left open by Nollan v. California Coastal Comm'n regarding the required degree of connection between exactions imposed by a city and the projected impacts of proposed development.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state regulation granting labor organizations a right to take access to an agricultural employer's property to solicit unionization constitutes a per se physical taking under the Fifth Amendment's Takings Clause, requiring just compensation, because it appropriates the property owner's right to exclude third parties from their land.
cases.notes: Majority opinion by Chief Justice Roberts (6-3). Justice Kavanaugh concurred, offering an alternative rationale. Justice Breyer dissented, joined by Justices Sotomayor and Kagan, arguing the regulation temporarily regulates the right to exclude rather than physically appropriating property and should be subject to Penn Central balancing test.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: In regulatory takings analysis, two legally distinct but commonly owned contiguous parcels should be evaluated as a single unit; courts must determine the relevant property parcel by considering state and local law treatment, physical characteristics, and prospective value, applying an objective inquiry into the landowner's reasonable expectations about whether the property would be treated as one parcel or separate ones.
cases.notes: Plurality opinion. Justice Kennedy delivered the opinion for the 5-3 majority. Chief Justice Roberts wrote a dissent joined by Justices Thomas and Alito. Justice Thomas wrote a separate dissent. Justice Gorsuch did not participate.

cases.full_name: Seila Law LLC v. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau's structure, which vests substantial executive power in a single Director removable only for cause, violates the separation of powers and is not saved by existing exceptions for multimember bodies or inferior officers with limited duties.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.summary: Concurred with the separation of powers violation but dissented on severability, arguing the first exception should be repudiated entirely and severability doctrine is unfounded.

opinions.author: Kagan
opinions.summary: Concurred with severability but dissented on the separation of powers violation, arguing for-cause removal restrictions serve legitimate independence interests and are supported by text, history, precedent, and governance needs.

cases.notes: Plurality opinion. Chief Justice Roberts authored the plurality opinion joined by Alito and Kavanaugh on the merits and severability. Justice Thomas concurred in the judgment on the merits but dissented on severability. Justice Kagan dissented on the merits but concurred on severability. The holding reflects what commanded a majority: that the CFPB's structure violates separation of powers (7 justices) and that the removal restriction is severable (5 justices: Roberts, Alito, Kavanaugh, Kagan, Ginsburg, Breyer, Sotomayor).

cases.full_name: Free Enterprise Fund v. Public Company Accounting Oversight Board
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A statutory restriction limiting the President's removal power over officers of an executive agency to removal only for cause violates the separation of powers by depriving the President of adequate control over executive officers and their accountability.
cases.notes: The Court held that the for-cause removal restriction on PCAOB members was unconstitutional, but severed only that provision rather than invalidating the entire Board. The Court upheld the Board's appointment as consistent with the Appointments Clause, finding Board members to be inferior officers whose appointment may be vested in the head of a department (the SEC).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The structure of the Housing and Economic Recovery Act of 2008, which restricts the President's power to remove the Federal Housing Finance Agency Director, violates the separation of powers principle; the anti-injunction provision of the Recovery Act bars shareholders' statutory claims when the FHFA acts within its conservatorship authority.
cases.notes: Unanimous on the constitutional holding regarding removal restrictions. Justice Thomas concurred in full with separate opinion on retrospective relief. Justice Gorsuch concurred except on retrospective relief. Justice Kagan concurred in part and concurred in judgment (joined by Breyer and Sotomayor). Justice Sotomayor concurred in part and dissented in part (joined by Breyer), arguing Seila Law should not control because FHFA lacks regulatory/enforcement authority comparable to CFPB.

cases.short_name: Hunt v. Washington State Apple Advertising Commission
cases.full_name: Hunt v. Washington State Apple Advertising Commission
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state regulation that is facially neutral but has a discriminatory impact on interstate commerce by removing the competitive advantage of out-of-state producers and shielding local producers from the same burden places an unreasonable burden on interstate commerce and violates the dormant Commerce Clause.
cases.notes: Unanimous decision. The Court rejected North Carolina's police power justification (uniformity and fraud prevention) as insufficient to justify the discriminatory burden on interstate commerce.

cases.short_name: C&A Carbone Inc. v. Town of Clarkstown
cases.full_name: C&A Carbone Inc. v. Town of Clarkstown
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A municipal flow control ordinance that requires all solid waste to be processed at a designated transfer station before leaving the municipality violates the Commerce Clause when it achieves its purpose of retaining processing fees by depriving competitors, including out-of-state firms, of access to a local market.
cases.notes: Justice Kennedy delivered the opinion of the Court, joined by Stevens, Scalia, Thomas, and Ginsburg. O'Connor filed a concurrence in the judgment. Souter filed a dissent, joined by Rehnquist and Blackmun. The case involves a flow control ordinance requiring all nonhazardous solid waste within the town to be deposited at a designated transfer station.

cases.short_name: Tennessee Wine and Spirits Retailers Association v. Thomas
cases.full_name: Tennessee Wine and Spirits Retailers Association v. Thomas
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The dormant Commerce Clause forbids a state from regulating liquor sales by granting licenses only to individuals or entities that have met state residency requirements, notwithstanding the Twenty-First Amendment, because such durational-residency requirements discriminate against nonresidents and protectionism is not a legitimate local purpose under the nondiscrimination principle that applied when the Twenty-First Amendment was ratified.

opinions.author: Gorsuch
opinions.summary: The original meaning of the Twenty-First Amendment was to allow states broad authority to regulate alcohol within their borders, which encompassed the authority to impose residency requirements on those seeking to sell alcohol.

cases.notes: 7-2 decision. Justice Gorsuch filed a dissenting opinion joined by Justice Clarence Thomas.

cases.short_name: Department of Revenue of Kentucky v. Davis
cases.full_name: Department of Revenue of Kentucky v. Davis
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state's differential tax scheme that exempts interest on in-state bonds while taxing interest on out-of-state bonds does not violate the dormant Commerce Clause when the bonds are a quintessentially public function and the tax is motivated by legitimate state objectives other than economic protectionism.
cases.notes: Fractured decision — no single opinion commanded a majority. The Court held 7-2 that Kentucky's tax policy was constitutional, but the reasoning was not unified across the seven justices in the majority.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public employee's speech on matters of internal office affairs, such as office transfer policy and internal grievances, is not protected by the First Amendment when the employee's primary motivation is personal grievance rather than public concern, and the government employer may discharge the employee for such speech without violating the First Amendment.
cases.notes: The Court applied the Pickering balancing test and held that Myers' questionnaire did not address matters of public concern because it focused on internal office grievances and transfer policy rather than matters of public interest. The Court reversed the lower courts' findings and held that Connick's termination of Myers did not violate the First Amendment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A prohibition on the registration of trademarks that are immoral or scandalous under Section 2(a) of the Lanham Act violates the First Amendment because it constitutes viewpoint-based discrimination.
cases.notes: The Court applied the viewpoint discrimination doctrine established in Matal v. Tam (2017). Justice Alito's concurrence emphasized the principle against viewpoint discrimination. Chief Justice Roberts and Justice Breyer filed opinions concurring in part and dissenting in part, disagreeing on whether the 'scandalous' portion of the statute could be narrowly construed. Justice Sotomayor, joined by Justice Breyer, dissented, arguing for a narrowing construction of 'scandalous' to obscenity, vulgarity, and profanity.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A warrant-based search of a third party's newsroom does not violate the Fourth Amendment when the magistrate has been satisfactorily demonstrated that fruits, instrumentalities, or evidence of crime is located on the premises, and the Framers of the Constitution did not forbid warrants where the press was involved.
cases.notes: Decided together with Bergna v. Stanford Daily. The source text does not identify the majority author by name.

cases.short_name: Minneapolis Star and Tribune Co. v. Minnesota Commissioner of Revenue
cases.full_name: Minneapolis Star and Tribune Co. v. Minnesota Commissioner of Revenue
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not impose a special use tax on ink and paper consumed in newspaper production that, through its exemption structure, effectively taxes only a small number of newspapers, as this violates the freedom of the press guarantee of the First Amendment.
cases.notes: The opinion is identified as 'opinion of the Court' but the author is not named in the provided text. The case involves a special tax on press materials (ink and paper) that disproportionately burdened a few large newspapers, raising First Amendment concerns.

cases.short_name: Boy Scouts of America v. Dale
cases.full_name: Boy Scouts of America v. James Dale
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Applying a state public accommodations law to require a private organization to admit a member violates the organization's First Amendment right of expressive association when the member's presence would compel the organization to express a message inconsistent with its stated values.
cases.notes: 5-4 decision. Chief Justice Rehnquist delivered the majority opinion. The Court reversed the New Jersey Supreme Court's decision that application of New Jersey's public accommodations law did not violate the Boy Scouts' First Amendment right of expressive association.

cases.full_name: Rumsfeld v. Forum for Academic and Institutional Rights
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Solomon Amendment, which conditions federal funding on law schools providing military recruiters equal access to students, does not violate the First Amendment. Congress may use its spending power to require universities to provide military recruiters access without triggering First Amendment scrutiny because the compelled speech doctrine does not apply to hosting a speaker whose message the school disagrees with.
cases.notes: Unanimous. Roberts' first major First Amendment opinion as Chief Justice.

cases.full_name: Personnel Administrator of Massachusetts v. Feeney
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A facially neutral law that grants hiring preference to honorably discharged veterans does not violate the Equal Protection Clause even if it has a disparate impact on women, because the law was enacted to serve legitimate purposes and the classification is between veterans and nonveterans, not between men and women.
cases.notes: The Court rejected the argument that the law violated equal protection despite its disparate impact on women. The decision established that discriminatory intent, not merely discriminatory effect, is required to trigger heightened scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause.

cases.full_name: Tuan Anh Nguyen v. Immigration and Naturalization Service
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A gender-based classification in 8 U.S.C. § 1409(a) governing citizenship acquisition by children born abroad out of wedlock to a citizen father and noncitizen mother comports with equal protection because it serves important governmental objectives and the discriminatory means are substantially related to achieving those objectives.
cases.notes: 5-4 decision. Dissent argued the majority failed to carefully apply equal protection jurisprudence concerning sex-based classifications.

cases.short_name: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center
cases.full_name: City of Cleburne, Texas v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city's denial of a special use permit to operate a home for the mentally retarded, based on irrational prejudice against the mentally retarded, violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment even under rational basis review.
cases.notes: Unanimous decision. The Court applied rational basis review but found the city's action failed even that deferential standard because it was based on irrational prejudice rather than legitimate governmental interests. The Court declined to elevate mental disability to quasi-suspect class status.

cases.full_name: City of Boerne v. Flores, Archbishop of San Antonio
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Religious Freedom Restoration Act of 1993 exceeds Congress' power under the Fourteenth Amendment and is unconstitutional as applied to state and local governments.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Joined majority opinion

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in part, disagreed with Part III-A-1 of majority opinion

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with invalidation of RFRA

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with invalidation of RFRA

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with invalidation of RFRA

cases.notes: Majority consisted of Kennedy, Rehnquist, Stevens, Thomas, and Ginsburg, with Scalia joining all but Part III-A-1. Vote was 6-3 on the core holding that RFRA exceeds Congress' power.

cases.short_name: Church of the Lukumi Babalu Aye v. City of Hialeah
cases.full_name: Church of the Lukumi Babalu Aye, Inc. v. City of Hialeah
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A government may not enact laws that suppress religious belief or practice, and laws that target religious conduct while exempting secular conduct with the same harmful effects violate the Free Exercise Clause even if facially neutral, because they lack a permissible object and fail the principle of general applicability.
cases.notes: Justice Kennedy delivered the opinion of the Court except as to Part II-A-2. The Court invalidated challenged municipal ordinances targeting animal sacrifice in Santería religious practices.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Legislative prayer at town board meetings does not violate the Establishment Clause when the prayer is offered as part of a longstanding tradition of legislative prayer, even if the prayer is sectarian in content, because such prayer is directed at the legislative body rather than the public and does not coerce religious observance.
cases.notes: Justice Scalia and Justice Thomas did not join the portion of Kennedy's majority opinion distinguishing between offense and coercion. Justice Alito wrote a concurrence. Justice Thomas wrote a separate concurrence in part and concurrence in the judgment. Justices Breyer and Kagan each wrote separate dissents, joined by Ginsburg, Sotomayor, and Breyer.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The application of physical force to the body of a person with intent to restrain constitutes a seizure under the Fourth Amendment even if the person does not submit and successfully flees.
cases.notes: Justice Barrett did not participate. Gorsuch dissented joined by Thomas and Alito.

cases.full_name: Marvin Pickering v. Board of Education of Township High School District 205
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public school teacher's dismissal for writing a letter to the editor criticizing the school board's fiscal and educational policies violates the First Amendment when the statements are not knowingly or recklessly false.

opinions.author: Douglas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Took a broader view of protected free speech than the majority

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Agreed letter may be protected speech but preferred remand to determine whether statements were knowingly or recklessly false

cases.notes: The majority opinion established that public employee speech on matters of public concern receives First Amendment protection unless the statements are knowingly or recklessly false.

cases.full_name: Gil Garcetti, et al. v. Richard Ceballos
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Speech by a public official is not protected by the First Amendment when it is expressed as part of the official's public duties, rather than as a private citizen.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's restriction of First Amendment protection for public employee speech on matters of public concern.

cases.notes: The Ninth Circuit had reversed the district court's grant of qualified immunity to the defendants, holding that Ceballos's speech addressing matters of public concern was protected by the First Amendment. The Supreme Court reversed the Ninth Circuit, holding that public employee speech made pursuant to official duties receives no First Amendment protection.

cases.full_name: Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Students do not lose their First Amendment right to freedom of speech when on school property, and school officials may suppress student speech only if they can demonstrate that the speech would materially and substantially interfere with the operation of the school.

opinions.author: Stewart
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that children are not necessarily guaranteed the full extent of First Amendment rights.

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Noted the majority's reliance on a distinction between communication through words and communication through action.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the First Amendment does not provide the right to express any opinion at any time, and that the armbands distracted students and detracted from school officials' ability to perform their duties.

opinions.author: Harlan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that school officials should be afforded wide authority to maintain order unless their actions stem from motivation other than a legitimate school interest.

cases.notes: The Court held that the armbands constituted pure speech entirely separate from conduct. The majority rejected the school district's justification based on fear of possible disruption, requiring instead proof of material and substantial interference.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not constitutionally require citizens to display an ideological message on their vehicle license plates, as such a requirement violates the First Amendment right to refuse to foster or display ideas one finds morally objectionable.
cases.notes: The Court recognized a First Amendment right to refuse to display or endorse ideological messages. The case involved a Jehovah's Witness who objected to New Hampshire's mandatory state motto 'Live Free or Die' on license plates on religious and political grounds.

cases.short_name: Hurley v. Irish-American Gay Group of Boston
cases.full_name: Hurley v. Irish-American Gay Group of Boston
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not require private citizens who organize a parade to include among the marchers a group imparting a message the organizers do not wish to convey, as such a mandate violates the First Amendment.
cases.notes: The case addresses compelled speech in the context of private parade organization. The Court held that Massachusetts' public accommodations law, as applied to require inclusion of GLIB in the St. Patrick's Day parade, violated the First Amendment right not to speak.

cases.short_name: Timmons v. Twin Cities Area New Party
cases.full_name: Timmons v. Twin Cities Area New Party
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may prohibit candidates from appearing on more than one party's ballot without violating the freedom of association, as such restrictions do not overly burden association rights when political parties remain free to endorse another party's candidate, and the state's interests in ballot integrity and political stability justify the restriction.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's balancing of association rights against state interests in ballot integrity

cases.notes: The Court reversed the Minnesota Court of Appeals and upheld the District Court's decision. The case involved anti-fusion ballot laws and their constitutionality under the freedom of association.

cases.full_name: California Democratic Party v. Bill Jones
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: California's blanket primary violates a political party's First Amendment right of association because it forces parties to associate with and have their nominees determined by nonmembers, thereby impairing the party's basic function to choose its own leaders.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that invalidating blanket primary schemes of multiple states constitutes extraordinary intrusion into state election law.

cases.notes: Proposition 198 changed California's primary from closed to blanket. District Court and Court of Appeals had upheld the blanket primary; Supreme Court reversed.

cases.full_name: Michael Clingman, Secretary, Oklahoma State Election Board, et al. v. Beaver
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state's semiclosed primary system that requires voters to register with a party before participating in its primary does not violate the First Amendment right to freedom of association when the burden on associational rights is minimal and justified by legitimate state interests, including the preservation of parties as viable and identifiable interest groups.
cases.notes: The Court held that not every electoral law burdening associational rights is subject to strict scrutiny. The Tenth Circuit had reversed the district court's ruling for Oklahoma.

cases.full_name: Christian Legal Society Chapter v. Martinez
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public university's all-comers policy requiring registered student organizations to allow any student to participate, become a member, or seek leadership positions regardless of status or beliefs is a reasonable, viewpoint-neutral condition on access to a limited public forum and does not violate the First Amendment.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Agreed with the majority holding and addressed CLS's argument regarding the university's nondiscrimination policy, clarifying that while the First Amendment may protect discriminatory practices off campus, it does not require a public university to validate or support such practices.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Noted that a limited forum may exclude some speakers based on their affiliation, as occurred in this case.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the majority's approach arms public institutions with a weapon for suppressing unpopular speech and criticized the principle that there is no freedom for expression that offends prevailing standards of political correctness in higher education.

cases.notes: The case involves First Amendment protections for student organizations in the limited public forum context of a public university. The majority applied a less restrictive level of scrutiny appropriate to limited public forums.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A trial court may not impose a prior restraint on press publication of information about a criminal case, even to protect the accused's right to a fair trial, absent a showing that the restraint would effectively serve that interest and that less restrictive alternatives would not suffice.
cases.notes: Unanimous decision. The Court held that while pretrial publicity concerns are legitimate, prior restraints on the press are not a permissible remedy. Chief Justice Burger emphasized that a community cannot be restrained from discussing matters intimately affecting life within it.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state cannot authorize or facilitate recitation of an official prayer in public schools, even if participation is voluntary and the prayer is not tied to a particular religion, because such a practice violates the Establishment Clause of the First Amendment.

opinions.author: Douglas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that state financing of a religious exercise violated the First Amendment.

opinions.author: Stewart
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that permitting voluntary prayer did not establish an official religion.

cases.notes: Vote was 6-1 with one Justice not participating. The decision established that government-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause regardless of voluntariness or religious neutrality.

cases.full_name: School District of Abington Township, Pennsylvania v. Schempp
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Public schools cannot sponsor Bible readings and recitations of the Lord's Prayer because such mandatory religious exercises violate the First Amendment's Establishment Clause.

opinions.author: Stewart
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the Establishment Clause had not necessarily been violated on the records and would remand for further hearings.

cases.notes: Consolidated case involving Pennsylvania and Maryland school prayer practices. Pennsylvania decision affirmed; Maryland decision reversed and remanded. Vote was 8-1 with Justice Stewart dissenting.

cases.full_name: Santa Fe Independent School District v. Doe
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A school district policy permitting student-led, student-initiated prayer at football games violates the Establishment Clause because the prayers constitute public speech authorized by government policy on government property at government-sponsored school events, involving both perceived and actual government endorsement of prayer.

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the majority opinion displayed hostility to religion in public life and that student-led prayer should be characterized as private student speech.

cases.notes: The District Court initially enjoined the policy, then modified it to permit only nonsectarian, nonproselytizing prayer. The Court of Appeals held the modified policy invalid. The Supreme Court affirmed, rejecting the District's characterization of the prayers as private student speech.

cases.short_name: Organization for a Better Austin v. Keefe
cases.full_name: Organization for a Better Austin v. Keefe
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state court injunction prohibiting the distribution of handbills and leaflets constitutes an impermissible prior restraint on free speech.
cases.notes: Majority opinion with 8-1 vote. Author not specified in source text.

cases.full_name: Schenck v. Pro-Choice Network of Western New York
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Fixed buffer zones prohibiting demonstrations within fifteen feet of clinic entrances, parking lots, and driveways are constitutional as content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions that serve the government's interest in public safety and access without imposing an excessive burden on free speech. Floating buffer zones prohibiting demonstrators from coming within fifteen feet of people or vehicles seeking clinic access are unconstitutional because they impose a greater burden on free speech than necessary to serve the government's interests.
cases.notes: The Court distinguished between fixed and floating buffer zones, upholding the former while striking down the latter based on a content-neutral time, place, and manner analysis.

cases.full_name: BE&K Construction Co. v. National Labor Relations Board
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The National Labor Relations Board lacked authority to assess liability for prosecution of an unsuccessful suit based solely on a retaliatory motive standard.
cases.notes: The text indicates this is an opinion of the Court but does not identify the majority author by name in the provided excerpt. The Court reversed the Board's decision and the Court of Appeals' affirmance, finding the Board lacked authority to use the retaliatory motive standard to assess liability under federal labor law.

cases.full_name: Bill Johnson's Restaurants, Inc. v. National Labor Relations Board
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The National Labor Relations Board may issue a cease-and-desist order to halt prosecution of a state-court civil suit brought by an employer to retaliate against employees for exercising federally protected labor rights without first finding that the suit lacks a reasonable basis in fact or law.
cases.notes: The case addresses whether the NLRB must find lack of reasonable basis in fact or law before issuing a cease-and-desist order against retaliatory civil litigation. The text indicates the Court reversed the Board's approach requiring such a finding.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress may not assign executive functions to the Comptroller General, an officer removable only by Congress, because such assignment violates the separation of powers doctrine by impermissibly vesting executive power in a legislative agent.
cases.notes: The case addresses the constitutionality of the Gramm-Rudman-Hollings Act's assignment of deficit reduction reporting functions to the Comptroller General. The District Court had invalidated the reporting provisions under the separation of powers doctrine.

cases.short_name: Cruzan v. Director Missouri Department of Health
cases.full_name: Cruzan v. Director, Missouri Department of Health
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A competent person has a liberty interest under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to refuse unwanted medical treatment, but a state may require clear and convincing evidence of an incompetent person's wishes before permitting withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment.
cases.notes: The Court affirmed the Missouri Supreme Court's decision requiring clear and convincing evidence of an incompetent person's desire to refuse life-sustaining treatment. The opinion recognizes a liberty interest in refusing unwanted medical treatment but upholds the state's procedural requirement.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Hyde Amendment, which restricts federal Medicaid funding for abortions to those necessary to preserve the life of the mother, does not violate the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment or the Religion Clauses of the First Amendment, and Title XIX does not require states to fund abortions when federal reimbursement is unavailable.
cases.notes: The case presents both statutory and constitutional questions regarding public funding of abortions under Medicaid and the Hyde Amendment. The source text is a syllabus and opinion excerpt; full vote count and complete opinion information not provided in excerpt.

cases.short_name: Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States
cases.full_name: Heart of Atlanta Motel, Inc. v. United States
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 is constitutional as applied to restaurants and other public accommodations engaged in interstate commerce.
cases.notes: This case upheld the constitutionality of Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 as applied to a restaurant. The Court reversed the three-judge District Court's issuance of an injunction restraining enforcement of the Act. The companion case Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States (No. 515) addressed the same Act as applied to hotels and motels.

cases.full_name: Marvin Horne v. Department of Agriculture
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Takings Clause of the Fifth Amendment applies with equal force to personal property as well as real property; a government requirement to reserve personal property as a condition of market participation constitutes a per se taking requiring just compensation, and returning net proceeds from the sale does not satisfy the compensation requirement.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that Takings Clause only requires compensation when taking results in public legal right to property; questioned whether such right exists in reserve requirement context.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued case should be remanded to calculate just compensation; contended that if compensation accounts for market benefits conferred by reserve requirement, no Takings Clause violation occurs.

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued per se taking only occurs when government deprives individual of every property right; contended reserve requirement allowing retention of net proceeds does not constitute per se taking; asserted government may condition property rights on regulated commerce participation.

cases.notes: 8-1 decision. Chief Justice Roberts delivered majority opinion. Justice Thomas concurred. Justice Breyer concurred in part and dissented in part, joined by Justices Ginsburg and Kagan. Justice Sotomayor dissented.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Federal Trade Commission Act's restriction limiting presidential removal of commissioners to removal for cause—inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office—is valid under the Constitution and restricts the President's removal power beyond the scope of Myers v. United States.
cases.notes: Court of Claims certified two questions regarding the President's power to remove a Federal Trade Commissioner. The Court upheld the statutory restriction on removal, distinguishing the FTC as a quasi-legislative and quasi-judicial body from the purely executive officers at issue in Myers.

cases.full_name: Immigration and Naturalization Service v. Lopez-Mendoza
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The exclusionary rule does not apply in civil deportation proceedings because the body or identity of a defendant is not suppressible as fruit of an illegal arrest, and the deterrent purpose of the exclusionary rule is not served in the deportation context where the INS has comprehensive internal oversight and the social cost of releasing an alien in continued violation of law outweighs minimal benefits.
cases.notes: The case involved two respondents (Lopez-Mendoza and Sandoval-Sanchez) in separate deportation proceedings. The Court held that deportation proceedings are civil actions and the exclusionary rule does not apply. The majority author is not identified in the source text.

cases.full_name: Mike Johanns, Secretary of Agriculture, et al. v. Livestock Marketing Association
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Compelled funding of government speech does not violate the First Amendment, and a mandatory beef promotion and research fund financed by cattle producers constitutes government speech immune from First Amendment challenge.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the characterization of the beef advertisements as government speech.

cases.notes: The Court reversed the Eighth Circuit's holding that the advertising was compelled private speech in violation of the First Amendment. The decision established that compelled funding of government speech, as opposed to private speech, does not raise First Amendment concerns.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress has broad power under Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment to enact legislation that prohibits state laws, even if the judiciary has not yet determined those state laws violate the Amendment itself; Section 4(e) of the Voting Rights Act of 1965, which prohibits enforcement of English literacy requirements for voting, is a proper exercise of congressional power under Section 5 and supersedes conflicting state law by the Supremacy Clause.
cases.notes: The opinion discusses the scope of congressional enforcement power under Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment, establishing that Congress need not await judicial determination of unconstitutionality before enacting remedial legislation.

cases.full_name: Joseph Kennedy v. Bremerton School District
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Free Exercise and Free Speech Clauses of the First Amendment protect an individual engaging in personal religious observance from government reprisal; a school district cannot suppress such religious expression based on Establishment Clause concerns when the expression occurs outside the scope of official duties and does not involve coercion of students.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate concurring opinion

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate concurring opinion

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissent from majority's abandonment of Lemon test and reframing of Establishment Clause doctrine

cases.notes: Overrules the Lemon test and endorsement test for Establishment Clause analysis. Replaces them with a historical practices and understandings framework. The Court held that Kennedy's prayers occurred outside the scope of his official duties as a coach and therefore did not constitute government speech or coercion of students.

cases.full_name: Pleasant Grove City, Utah v. Summum
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The placement of a monument in a public park is government speech, not private speech subject to Free Speech Clause scrutiny, when the government retains final authority over which monuments are displayed.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Embraced the majority's government speech reasoning

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Agreed with government speech reasoning and noted no Establishment Clause violation, citing the historical meaning of Ten Commandments displays

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Cautioned that government speech should be a rule of thumb, not a rigid category, and proposed a balancing test for disproportionate speech burdens

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Warned against categorical treatment of public monuments as government speech

cases.notes: Unanimous judgment (9-0) with multiple concurring opinions. The majority opinion commanded 8 justices (Alito, Roberts, Stevens, Scalia, Kennedy, Thomas, Ginsburg, Breyer). Stevens and Ginsburg wrote separately but joined the majority reasoning. Scalia and Thomas wrote separately. Breyer wrote separately. Souter concurred in judgment only with a cautionary note.

cases.short_name: R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul
cases.full_name: R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A content-based regulation of speech that targets expression based on the subject matter it addresses is facially unconstitutional under the First Amendment, even if the speech falls within a traditionally unprotected category such as fighting words, unless the regulation is narrowly tailored to serve a compelling government interest and does not discriminate on the basis of viewpoint.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the Minnesota Supreme Court's decision upholding the St. Paul Bias-Motivated Crime Ordinance. Although the ordinance targeted fighting words (a category of unprotected speech), the Court held that content-based discrimination within an unprotected category violates the First Amendment.

cases.full_name: Regents of the University of California v. Bakke
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A university may not employ a rigid racial quota in admissions, but race may be considered as one factor among many in a holistic review to achieve educational diversity.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in judgment that race may be considered in admissions; disagreed with Powell on the permissibility of race-conscious remedies.

opinions.author: Chief Justice Burger
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in judgment that the special admissions program was unlawful and respondent must be admitted.

cases.notes: Fractured decision. Powell announced the judgment but no single rationale commanded a majority. Powell's view that race may be considered as one factor (supported by Brennan group on this point) represents the holding on permissibility of race in admissions. The Court was divided on whether race-conscious remedies are permissible and on the proper standard of review.

cases.short_name: Rosenberger v. Rector and Visitors of University of Virginia
cases.full_name: Ronald W. Rosenberger v. Rector and Visitors of the University of Virginia
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public university may not deny funding from a student activities fund to a student religious publication based on the religious viewpoint of its content when the university funds other student publications on a viewpoint-neutral basis, as such denial constitutes viewpoint discrimination in violation of the Free Speech Clause and does not violate the Establishment Clause.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's viewpoint discrimination analysis and Establishment Clause conclusion

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The Court held that viewpoint discrimination in the allocation of student activity funds violates the Free Speech Clause and that funding religious student publications does not violate the Establishment Clause when funding is allocated on a viewpoint-neutral basis.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A congressional reapportionment plan that creates bizarrely shaped districts based on race, even if facially race-neutral and motivated by remedial purposes, raises an equal protection challenge and must be justified by a compelling governmental interest.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's recognition of an equal protection claim based on district shape and racial motivation.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The Court held that bizarrely shaped districts can trigger equal protection scrutiny when race appears to be the predominant factor, even absent explicit racial classifications. The case established that race-conscious redistricting, even for remedial purposes, must satisfy strict scrutiny.

cases.short_name: Torres v. Texas Department of Public Safety
cases.full_name: Leroy Torres v. Texas Department of Public Safety
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress properly exercised its power to raise and support the Armed Forces when it authorized private damages suits against nonconsenting States under the Uniformed Services Employment and Reemployment Rights Act of 1994, as States implicitly consented to the exercise of this federal power upon joining the Union.
cases.notes: Justice Elena Kagan authored a concurring opinion. Justice Clarence Thomas authored a dissenting opinion, joined by Justices Samuel Alito, Neil Gorsuch, and Amy Coney Barrett.

cases.full_name: United Haulers Association, Inc. v. Oneida-Herkimer Solid Waste Management Authority
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A local flow control ordinance requiring waste to be delivered to publicly-owned facilities does not violate the dormant Commerce Clause when it does not discriminate against out-of-state private businesses and imposes only an incidental burden on interstate commerce that is outweighed by local public benefits.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Would have held that the ordinance discriminated against commerce and was unconstitutional.

cases.notes: The majority distinguished this ordinance from previously struck-down ordinances by emphasizing that the favored facilities were publicly operated. The Court applied a balancing test rather than rigorous scrutiny of economic effects.

cases.full_name: Carol Anne Bond v. United States
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A defendant has standing to challenge the constitutionality of a federal statute on the ground that it violates the Tenth Amendment and interferes with powers reserved to the States.

opinions.author: Ginsburg
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Emphasized that a defendant has a personal constitutional right not to be convicted under a constitutionally invalid law.

cases.notes: Unanimous decision. The Court held that Bond has standing to challenge the federal Chemical Weapons Convention Implementation Act on Tenth Amendment grounds, reversing the Third Circuit's holding that she lacked such standing.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state retains sovereign authority to build improvements to its shore and withdraw water from a shared river under an interstate compact, absent explicit language in the compact divesting that authority.
cases.notes: Case concerns interpretation of the 1785 Compact between Virginia and Maryland regarding the Potomac River and the Black-Jenkins arbitration agreement.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state's prohibition on assisting suicide does not violate the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment because the asserted right to physician-assisted suicide is not a fundamental liberty interest deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition.
cases.notes: The Court established a two-part method for substantive due process analysis: (1) the asserted right must be objectively deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition, and (2) the Court must carefully describe the asserted fundamental liberty interest. The Court rejected respondents' various formulations of the interest and held that the right to physician-assisted suicide has no place in the Nation's traditions. The Court also applied rational basis review to uphold the state's legitimate interests in prohibiting intentional killing, preserving human life, preventing suicide, protecting medical ethics, and protecting vulnerable groups.

cases.full_name: Reginald A. Wilkinson, Director, Ohio Department of Rehabilitation and Correction, et al. v. Austin
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Procedural protections provided by Ohio's New Policy for classifying prisoners for placement at a supermaximum security facility satisfy the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment when the procedures sufficiently minimize the risk of erroneous placement and account for the state's legitimate interests in prison security and resource allocation.
cases.notes: Unanimous decision. The Court affirmed the Sixth Circuit's reversal of the district court's substantive modifications to Ohio's prison placement policy, holding that the procedural protections in the New Policy were constitutionally sufficient.

cases.full_name: Dun and Bradstreet, Inc. v. Greenmoss Builders, Inc.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When false and defamatory statements do not involve matters of public concern, the First Amendment does not prohibit awards of presumed and punitive damages absent a showing of actual malice, and the Gertz rule limiting such damages does not apply to non-media defendants such as credit reporting agencies.
cases.notes: Plurality opinion. Justice Powell announced the judgment joined by Justices Rehnquist and O'Connor. The holding reflects the plurality's position that Gertz does not apply to speech on matters of private concern, particularly by non-media defendants. The case does not command a majority on all points but establishes that the Gertz actual malice requirement does not extend to private-concern speech by non-media speakers.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Burning an American flag as political protest constitutes expressive conduct protected by the First Amendment, and the government may not prohibit the expression of an idea simply because society finds the idea offensive or disagreeable.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Four justices dissented from the majority's holding that flag burning is protected expressive conduct.

cases.notes: Texas Court of Criminal Appeals had reversed Johnson's conviction under Texas flag desecration statute before Supreme Court review.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A federal funding restriction that prohibits legal services attorneys from making arguments challenging or amending existing welfare law violates the First Amendment because it regulates private speech and insulates federal law from legitimate judicial challenge.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the Appropriations Act does not directly regulate speech, does not establish a public forum, and does not discriminate on the basis of viewpoint.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The Court held that the LSC program was designed to facilitate private speech, not to promote a governmental message, and therefore funding restrictions that limit the arguments attorneys could make violated the First Amendment.

cases.full_name: Eric H. Holder, Jr., Attorney General, et al. v. Humanitarian Law Project
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The material support provision of the Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act, as amended by the Intelligence Reform and Terrorism Prevention Act, is not unconstitutionally vague as applied to the provision of training, expert advice or assistance, service, and personnel to designated foreign terrorist organizations.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's conclusion that the statute permits prosecution for coordinated teaching and advocacy furthering designated organizations' lawful political objectives; argued the government had not met its burden to show the speech prohibition served a compelling governmental interest.

cases.notes: The Court reversed the Ninth Circuit's holding that the terms 'service,' 'training,' 'other specialized knowledge,' and related provisions were unconstitutionally vague as applied to the plaintiffs. The majority reasoned that the statutory terms are sufficiently clear as applied to the particular forms of support at issue, distinguishing them from terms like 'annoying' and 'indecent' previously struck down as vague.

cases.full_name: Anthony Douglas Elonis v. United States
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A prosecution under 18 U.S.C. §875(c) for transmitting threats in interstate commerce must prove that the defendant acted with subjective intent to threaten; an objective reasonable person standard is insufficient to satisfy the mens rea requirement.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Agreed that subjective intent is required but argued the majority should have specified whether knowledge or recklessness is the appropriate standard, and contended recklessness should be the standard.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that nine circuit courts had already resolved this issue with a general intent standard, and that knowledge of posting the threats is sufficient to establish the intent element.

cases.notes: The Court reversed the Third Circuit's affirmance of Elonis' conviction. The majority held that subjective intent to threaten is required under §875(c), rejecting the objective reasonable person standard applied by the district court. Justice Alito's concurrence-in-part-dissent-in-part indicates fractured reasoning on the precise mens rea standard (knowledge vs. recklessness), though the majority opinion does not resolve this question.

cases.full_name: Lester Packingham v. State of North Carolina
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law prohibiting registered sex offenders from accessing social media websites violates the First Amendment because it is not narrowly tailored to serve the government's significant interest in protecting children from sexual abuse; the law burdens substantially more speech than necessary to advance that interest.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the majority erred in equating the entire internet with a traditionally public forum; contended that states should be able to regulate sex offenders' use of certain websites, but agreed the North Carolina law was overbroad because it encompassed websites unlikely to facilitate sex crimes against children.

cases.notes: Justice Gorsuch did not participate. The opinion form is signed_majority reflecting Kennedy's majority opinion, though Alito, Roberts, and Thomas concurred only in the judgment on narrower grounds. The vote is 8-0 because only 8 justices participated (Gorsuch recused).

cases.full_name: Eddie C. Thomas v. Review Board of the Indiana Employment Security Division
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not deny unemployment benefits to an individual solely because the individual's refusal to work violates a sincere religious belief, as such denial burdens the individual's First Amendment right to free exercise of religion without satisfying strict scrutiny.

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the Free Exercise Clause does not require a state to provide direct financial assistance to persons solely on the basis of their religious beliefs.

cases.notes: The Court applied the principle established in Sherbert v. Verner that a person may not be compelled to choose between exercising a First Amendment right and participation in an otherwise available public program.

cases.short_name: Gonzales v. O Centro Espirita Beneficente Uniao do Vegetal
cases.full_name: Alberto R. Gonzales, Attorney General of the United States v. O Centro Espirita Beneficente Uniao do Vegetal
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Under the Religious Freedom Restoration Act, the Federal Government must satisfy the compelling interest test—demonstrating that a substantial burden on religious practice is in furtherance of a compelling government interest and is the least restrictive means of furthering that interest—and the Government did not carry this burden regarding the uniform application of the Controlled Substances Act to prohibit sacramental use of a hallucinogenic tea.
cases.notes: Chief Justice Roberts delivered the opinion of the Court. The case applies the Religious Freedom Restoration Act of 1993 (RFRA) to a federal statute (Controlled Substances Act). The Court affirmed the grant of a preliminary injunction.

cases.short_name: Masterpiece Cakeshop v. Colorado Civil Rights Commission
cases.full_name: Masterpiece Cakeshop, Ltd. v. Colorado Civil Rights Commission
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state civil rights commission must apply anti-discrimination laws in a neutral manner with respect to religion; the Commission's application of Colorado's anti-discrimination statute violated the Free Exercise Clause where commissioners displayed clear and impermissible hostility toward the cake shop owner's sincere religious objections to creating cakes for same-sex weddings.

opinions.author: Ginsburg
