opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that neither the Commission's comments regarding Phillips' religious views nor disparate treatment of bakers objecting to anti-gay messages justified ruling in favor of Phillips.

opinions.author: Kagan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Agreed that the Commission failed to give neutral treatment to Phillips' religious views but declined to assign significance to disparate treatment of bakers refusing to create cakes with anti-gay messages.

opinions.author: Gorsuch
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that Phillips' case and cases of bakers objecting to anti-gay messages were similar and the Commission acted inappropriately in treating them differently.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that an order requiring Phillips to bake a wedding cake for a same-sex couple would violate his First Amendment rights.

cases.notes: The majority opinion was authored by Justice Kennedy. The case involved tension between free exercise of religion and anti-discrimination protections. The Court emphasized the particular hostility shown by the Commission toward Phillips' religious beliefs and the disparate treatment compared to other bakers with objections to anti-gay messages.

cases.full_name: 303 Creative LLC v. Jena Griswold, in her official capacity as Colorado Secretary of State, et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The First Amendment prohibits a state from compelling a website designer to create expressive designs conveying messages with which the designer disagrees, even when enforcing a public accommodations law that prohibits discrimination based on sexual orientation.

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the majority improperly granted a business open to the public a constitutional right to refuse service to members of a protected class, marking a departure from established public accommodations doctrine.

cases.notes: The case involved a challenge to Colorado's Anti-Discrimination Act (CADA) as applied to compelled speech. The majority held that expressive design of wedding websites constitutes protected First Amendment speech that cannot be compelled by the state, even in the context of public accommodations law. The dissent emphasized the tension between First Amendment compelled speech doctrine and civil rights protections.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Religious Freedom Restoration Act permits individuals to obtain money damages against federal officials in their individual capacities for violations of RFRA, as the statute's reference to obtaining 'appropriate relief against a government' encompasses individual federal officials and monetary damages constitute appropriate relief.
cases.notes: Justice Amy Coney Barrett took no part in the consideration or decision of this case. The Second Circuit panel reversed the District Court's dismissal of claims against the agents.

cases.full_name: Allegheny County v. American Civil Liberties Union
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A creche displayed alone on the Grand Staircase of a county courthouse violates the Establishment Clause because it has the impermissible effect of endorsing religion, but a menorah displayed outside a city-county building next to a Christmas tree and liberty sign does not violate the Establishment Clause.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Joined Blackmun on Parts I, II, and III-B regarding Establishment Clause analysis of holiday displays

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Joined Blackmun on Parts I, II, V, and VII regarding Establishment Clause endorsement analysis

cases.notes: Fractured decision. Justice Blackmun announced the judgment and delivered opinions in multiple parts with different coalitions joining different sections. The holding reflects what the Court agreed upon: the creche display violated the Establishment Clause, but the menorah display did not. The decision applies the endorsement test to holiday religious displays on public property.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Public school teachers may not provide instruction in parochial schools because such presence violates the Establishment Clause by creating an impermissible entanglement between church and state.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by agostini_v_felton_1997. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Public school teachers may provide remedial instruction to students in parochial schools without violating the Establishment Clause, as there is no presumption that such presence inevitably leads to state-sponsored religious indoctrination, and only policies generating excessive entanglement between church and state violate the Establishment Clause.
cases.notes: Overruled Aguilar v. Felton (473 U.S. 402). The Court rejected the presumption that public school teachers in parochial schools inevitably lead to religious indoctrination.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A federal education aid program providing secular materials and equipment to both public and private schools, including religiously affiliated schools, does not violate the Establishment Clause when aid is distributed on a neutral basis without regard to the religious character of recipient institutions.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the plurality's neutrality-based approach to Establishment Clause analysis

cases.notes: Plurality opinion (6-3). Justice Thomas wrote for a plurality of the Court. The holding reflects the neutrality principle articulated by the plurality: that neutral aid programs do not violate the Establishment Clause merely because religiously affiliated institutions are eligible recipients on the same terms as secular institutions.

cases.short_name: American Legion v. American Humanist Assn.
cases.full_name: American Legion et al. v. American Humanist Association et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A longstanding, religiously expressive monument, symbol, or practice on public property does not violate the Establishment Clause when the passage of time has imbued it with historical significance and secular meaning, and when removing it would not appear neutral to the community.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the Establishment Clause should not be incorporated against the States and that actual coercion is the sine qua non of an establishment of religion.

cases.notes: Fractured decision. Justice Alito delivered the opinion of the Court with respect to Parts I, II–B, II–C, III, and IV (the core holding on the Bladensburg Cross). Justice Alito, joined by Chief Justice Roberts, Justice Breyer, and Justice Kavanaugh, concluded in Parts II–A and II–D regarding Lemon and historical practice. Justice Thomas concurred separately on incorporation and coercion grounds. The holding reflects what commanded a majority on the core issue: the Bladensburg Cross does not violate the Establishment Clause.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Maine's nonsectarian requirement for otherwise generally available tuition assistance payments violates the Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment because the state may not discriminate against otherwise eligible recipients by disqualifying them from a public benefit solely on the basis of religious character.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argues that the majority gives almost exclusive attention to the Free Exercise Clause while paying almost no attention to the Establishment Clause, and that Maine's nonsectarian requirement strikes the correct balance between the two clauses.

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Highlights the Court's increasingly expansive view of the Free Exercise Clause that risks swallowing the space between the Religion Clauses.

cases.notes: The majority applied precedents from Trinity Lutheran Church of Columbia, Inc. v. Comer and Espinoza v. Montana Department of Revenue to hold that Maine may not subsidize some private schools but not others on the basis of religious character.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A city's flag-raising program that is not government speech cannot exclude a private organization's flag based on religious viewpoint, as such exclusion violates the Free Speech Clause of the First Amendment.

opinions.author: Kavanaugh
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Clarifies that government does not violate the Establishment Clause when treating religious and secular persons or organizations equally, but does violate the Free Speech Clause when excluding religious persons or organizations.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Proposes alternative test for government speech: whether the government is actually expressing its own views or the real speaker is a private party and the government is regulating private speech.

opinions.author: Gorsuch
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Criticizes the Lemon test for Establishment Clause analysis and argues Boston erroneously relied on the abandoned Lemon test.

cases.notes: Unanimous decision (9-0) with fractured reasoning on the government speech test. Majority applies a three-factor holistic inquiry; Alito concurrence proposes alternative test; Gorsuch concurrence criticizes Lemon test.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute prohibiting partial birth abortion without an exception for cases where the procedure is necessary to preserve the health of the mother places an undue burden on a woman's right to obtain an abortion and violates the Constitution as interpreted in Casey and Roe.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the Constitution does not prohibit states from banning partial birth abortion procedures.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. Affirmed the District Court and Court of Appeals decisions striking down Nebraska's partial birth abortion statute. The majority held that the statute violated the undue burden standard established in Planned Parenthood v. Casey by failing to include a health exception.

cases.full_name: Alberto R. Gonzales, Attorney General v. Leroy Carhart
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress may ban a specific abortion procedure (intact D&E or partial-birth abortion) without an exception for the health of the mother when Congress determines the procedure is never medically necessary, provided the ban is limited to cases where the physician intends to perform that specific procedure and delivers the fetus past anatomical landmarks, and the ban does not impose an undue burden on the right to abortion.

opinions.author: Ginsburg
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disputed the majority's consistency with Casey and Stenberg precedents and argued the opinion reflected hostility to the right to abortion.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The majority held that the Partial-Birth Abortion Ban Act of 2003 applies only to the intact D&E method and does not impose an undue burden on the right to abortion. The Court upheld the Act's omission of a health exception based on Congress's finding that the procedure is never medically necessary. Justice Ginsburg's dissent indicated significant doctrinal disagreement about consistency with prior precedent.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state statute that conditions the right to marry on payment of child support obligations violates the Equal Protection Clause and infringes the fundamental right to marry protected by the Fourteenth Amendment.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: One justice dissented from the majority's holding that marriage is a fundamental right.

cases.notes: The Court reaffirmed marriage as a fundamental right rooted in the right to privacy identified in Griswold v. Connecticut and the principle established in Loving v. Virginia. The majority rejected the state's interest in ensuring child support compliance as a sufficient justification for restricting the fundamental right to marry.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not condition appellate review of a trial court decree terminating parental rights on the affected parent's ability to pay record preparation fees, as such conditioning violates the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment when applied to indigent parents.
cases.notes: The Court held that just as a state may not block an indigent petty offender's access to appeal, so too may a state not deny appellate review based on inability to pay record preparation fees in parental termination cases. The case involves fundamental rights to parental relationship and equal protection concerns.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Proclamation restricting travel by citizens from eight countries does not violate the president's statutory authority under the Immigration and Nationality Act or the Establishment Clause, and the president possesses broad discretion under Section 1182(f) of the INA to suspend entry of non-citizens when determined to be detrimental to United States interests.

opinions.author: Kennedy
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Emphasized deference to executive authority and urged government officials to act in accordance with the Constitution.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Expressed concern over federal district courts issuing global or nationwide injunctions and called for the Court to address this trend.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Questioned whether the government is actually applying exemption and waiver programs as claimed and would remand for further proceedings.

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued the majority applied incorrect scrutiny standard to Establishment Clause claim and that the Proclamation fails even under rational basis review given the president's stated intent of a 'total and complete shutdown of Muslims.'

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The majority upheld the Proclamation on both statutory authority and Establishment Clause grounds. The case involved challenges to Executive Order 13,769 (EO-1), Executive Order 13,780 (EO-2), and the September 2017 Proclamation. The Court assumed without deciding that plaintiffs' claims were justiciable and did not resolve whether the district court's global injunction was impermissibly overbroad.

cases.full_name: Donald J. Trump v. United States of America
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A former President is absolutely immune from criminal prosecution for actions within his conclusive and preclusive constitutional authority, and is entitled to at least presumptive immunity from prosecution for all other official acts. There is no immunity for unofficial acts.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Filed a concurring opinion

cases.notes: Chief Justice Roberts delivered the opinion of the Court, joined in full by Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch, and Kavanaugh, JJ., and joined by Barrett, J., except as to Part III–C. This is the first criminal prosecution in the Nation's history of a former President for actions taken during his Presidency. The Court vacated and remanded the D.C. Circuit's affirmance of the District Court's denial of Trump's motion to dismiss based on Presidential immunity.

cases.full_name: Anthony Palazzolo v. State of Rhode Island
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A property owner may bring a takings claim under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments even if the owner acquired title to the property after the effective date of the challenged regulations, and the claim is not barred by the timing of the acquisition; however, a takings claim based on deprivation of all economic value fails if the property retains significant economic worth.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissented from the majority's holding on post-acquisition ripeness and standing

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. The Court reversed the Rhode Island Supreme Court's finding that the takings claim was unripe and that post-regulation acquisition of title barred the claim. The Court affirmed the state court's finding that the owner failed to establish total deprivation of economic value.

cases.full_name: Tahoe-Sierra Preservation Council, Inc. v. Tahoe Regional Planning Agency
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A temporary moratorium on development does not constitute a per se taking of property requiring just compensation; whether a taking occurred depends on consideration of the landowners' reasonable expectations, the actual economic impact, the public interest, and the reasons for the moratorium.

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that temporary deprivation of all economic use constitutes a categorical taking

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that temporary deprivation of all economic use constitutes a categorical taking

cases.notes: The Court rejected the categorical rule that any deprivation of all economic use, no matter how brief, constitutes a compensable taking, reasoning that such a rule would impose unreasonable financial obligations on governments for normal delays in processing land use applications.

cases.short_name: Arkansas Game & Fish Commission v. United States
cases.full_name: Arkansas Game & Fish Commission v. United States of America
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A temporary interference with property, including temporary flooding caused by government action, can constitute a compensable taking under the Fifth Amendment Takings Clause; there is no categorical exception for temporary takings, and courts must consider multiple factors including duration, severity, intent, and foreseeability when determining whether a taking has occurred.
cases.notes: Unanimous decision. The Court rejected the Federal Circuit's categorical rule that only permanent flooding constitutes a taking, establishing that temporary government interference with property can be compensable under the Fifth Amendment.

cases.full_name: Knick v. Township of Scott, Pennsylvania
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A property owner may bring a Fifth Amendment takings claim under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 in federal court without first exhausting state inverse-condemnation remedies; the state-litigation requirement established in Williamson County Regional Planning Commission v. Hamilton Bank of Johnson City is overruled.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Emphasized opposition to the position that no constitutional violation occurs if a mechanism for compensation exists after the taking.

opinions.author: Kagan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the Takings Clause text includes the qualifying phrase 'without just compensation' and does not forbid all takings, only uncompensated ones; criticized departure from stare decisis.

cases.notes: The majority held that the right of compensation arises at the time of the taking, not after. The Court found that Williamson County's requirement to exhaust state remedies before federal court access imposes an unjustifiable burden on takings plaintiffs and conflicts with takings jurisprudence.

cases.short_name: City of Philadelphia v. New Jersey
cases.full_name: City of Philadelphia v. New Jersey
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state law that prohibits the importation of most solid or liquid waste originating outside the state violates the Commerce Clause because it discriminates against interstate commerce, and such discrimination cannot be justified merely by advancing health and environmental objectives without economic discrimination.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the New Jersey Supreme Court's decision upholding the waste importation ban. The opinion addresses whether interstate movement of waste constitutes 'commerce' under the Commerce Clause and rejects the state court's attempt to apply a narrower definition of commerce when striking down state legislation.

cases.full_name: West Lynn Creamery, Inc. v. Healy
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state pricing order that imposes a tax on all milk dealers but distributes the tax revenue exclusively to in-state dairy farmers violates the Commerce Clause because it discriminates against interstate commerce and functions as a tariff.
cases.notes: The Court found that although the pricing order applied to all dealers, the discriminatory effect—reserving benefits exclusively for in-state producers—rendered it unconstitutional under the Commerce Clause.

cases.full_name: Comptroller of the Treasury of Maryland v. Wynne
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state tax scheme that discriminates against interstate commerce by failing the internal consistency test violates the dormant Commerce Clause, even absent facial discrimination, when the scheme would disadvantage interstate commerce if applied identically in every state.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the dormant Commerce Clause lacks constitutional basis and that the tax should be upheld absent facial discrimination indistinguishable from previously invalidated taxes.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the majority's dormant Commerce Clause analysis would invalidate laws passed by the same legislators who ratified the Constitution, rendering the doctrine incorrect.

opinions.author: Ginsburg
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the dormant Commerce Clause does not prevent operation of a tax scheme when discriminatory effect results from combination of two otherwise-lawful tax schemes, and that the internal consistency test need not be rigidly enforced.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision with three separate dissents. The majority applied the internal consistency test to invalidate Maryland's county tax scheme as violating the dormant Commerce Clause.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public employee's lack of a contractual or tenure right to re-employment does not defeat a claim that nonrenewal of employment violated the First and Fourteenth Amendments, and the government may not deny a benefit to a person on a basis that infringes constitutionally protected interests, especially freedom of speech.
cases.notes: Case decided with Board of Regents v. Roth. Establishes that lack of tenure does not bar First Amendment retaliation claims and that procedural due process may apply to non-tenured public employees with legitimate claims of entitlement to re-employment.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When a state extends the right to education to its citizens, it creates a property interest in that education protected by the Due Process Clause, and the state cannot deprive students of that interest on grounds of misconduct without providing fundamentally fair procedures, including at minimum notice and some kind of hearing.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's recognition of a property interest in education and the procedural requirements imposed.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. Students suspended from school without hearing challenged the constitutionality of the suspension procedures under the Due Process Clause.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A property interest in public employment is created only by state law through statute or contract, and absent an enforceable expectation of continued employment granted by the employer, an employee's discharge does not deprive him of a property interest protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: The opinion addresses two questions: (1) whether petitioner's employment status was a property interest protected by the Due Process Clause, and (2) whether a false explanation for discharge deprived him of an interest in liberty. The Court resolved the property interest question; the liberty interest analysis appears incomplete in the provided text.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state employee's random, unauthorized deprivation of a constitutionally protected liberty interest does not preclude a procedural due process claim under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 when the state could have provided predeprivation safeguards; the Parratt rule limiting such claims to postdeprivation remedies does not apply when the state has delegated authority to the employee to determine whether to deprive the person of liberty.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the District Court's dismissal and the panel's affirmance, adopting the Eleventh Circuit's en banc plurality reasoning that Parratt v. Taylor does not apply when predeprivation remedies were feasible. The case concerns the scope of the Parratt rule in the context of mental hospital admission procedures.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Defense of Marriage Act violates the Fifth Amendment's guarantee of equal protection by denying same-sex couples the federal rights and benefits of marriage that are available to opposite-sex couples whose marriages are recognized under state law, imposing a disadvantage, separate status, and stigma on same-sex married couples.

opinions.author: Roberts
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued the Court lacked jurisdiction and that DOMA's uniformity and stability interests were justified; did not address state marriage definition issues.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued the Court lacked jurisdiction and power to invalidate democratically enacted legislation; criticized majority's assertion of Supreme Court supremacy.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued the Government lacked standing but BLAG had standing; contended the Constitution does not guarantee a right to same-sex marriage under the history-and-tradition test; marriage definition should be left to the people.

cases.notes: 5-4 majority. The majority did not specify whether rational basis or strict scrutiny applied to DOMA. Justice Kennedy's majority opinion focused on the purpose and effect of DOMA as imposing stigma and disadvantage on same-sex couples under the Fifth Amendment's equal protection guarantee.

cases.full_name: Michael M. v. Superior Court of Sonoma County
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may criminalize unlawful sexual intercourse by males but not females under age 18 without violating the Equal Protection Clause, because young men and women are not similarly situated with respect to the risks of sexual intercourse, and the state has a substantial interest in preventing illegitimate pregnancy.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the plurality's equal protection analysis

cases.notes: Plurality opinion (5-4). No single rationale commanded a majority. The holding reflects what the plurality announced, but the case's precedential effect on equal protection doctrine is limited by its fractured nature.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A state may not deny free public K-12 education to undocumented school-age children based on their immigration status, as such denial violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
cases.notes: Class action challenging Texas Education Code § 21.031, which withheld state funding and authorized denial of enrollment to undocumented children. District Court found the statute violated Equal Protection Clause and was not supported by rational basis.

cases.short_name: Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard
cases.full_name: Students for Fair Admissions v. President and Fellows of Harvard College
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Race-based admissions programs at universities violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment when they fail to satisfy strict scrutiny, including the requirements that the government demonstrate a compelling interest in a measurable way, avoid racial stereotyping, and provide a logical endpoint for when race-based admissions will cease.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate concurring opinion

opinions.author: Gorsuch
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate concurring opinion

opinions.author: Kavanaugh
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate concurring opinion

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissent joined by Kagan and Jackson (Jackson did not participate in the Harvard portion)

cases.notes: 6-3 majority. Chief Justice Roberts authored the majority opinion. The case was originally consolidated with a similar case challenging University of North Carolina admissions policies but was severed. The holding reflects the majority's application of strict scrutiny to race-based admissions.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress has constitutional authority under the Commerce Clause to regulate interstate commerce and to prohibit the shipment in interstate commerce of goods produced under substandard labor conditions, including those involving minimum wages, maximum hours, and child labor, because such conditions have a significant effect on interstate commerce.
cases.notes: The Court upheld the Fair Labor Standards Act of 1938 against a Commerce Clause challenge. The decision relied heavily on Gibbons v. Ogden (1824) and established that Congress's commerce power cannot be enlarged or diminished by state action.

cases.short_name: National League of Cities v. Usery
cases.full_name: National League of Cities v. Usery
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Commerce Clause does not empower Congress to enforce the minimum-wage and overtime provisions of the Fair Labor Standards Act against the States in areas of traditional governmental functions.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by garcia_v_san_antonio_metropolitan_transit_authority_1985. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.short_name: Garcia v. San Antonio Metropolitan Transit Authority
cases.full_name: Garcia v. San Antonio Metropolitan Transit Authority
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Commerce Clause empowers Congress to enforce the minimum-wage and overtime provisions of the Fair Labor Standards Act against the States, and state immunity from federal labor law cannot be determined by reference to whether a function is 'traditional' or 'nontraditional.'
cases.notes: Overrules National League of Cities v. Usery, 426 U.S. 833 (1976). The Court held that the 'traditional governmental function' test is unworkable and inconsistent with federalism principles.

cases.short_name: Atwater v. City of Lago Vista
cases.full_name: Gail Atwater v. City of Lago Vista
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourth Amendment does not forbid a warrantless arrest for a minor criminal offense, such as a misdemeanor punishable only by a fine, if an officer has probable cause to believe that an individual has committed the offense in his presence.

opinions.author: O'Connor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the majority decision neglects the Fourth Amendment's express command in the name of administrative ease and cloaks unreasonable seizure with the mantle of reasonableness.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. Justice O'Connor dissented, arguing the decision improperly prioritizes administrative convenience over Fourth Amendment protections.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When two co-occupants are present and one consents to a search while the other refuses, the search violates the Fourth Amendment because it is unreasonable for police to enter shared premises over the express objection of a present occupant, even with the consent of another co-occupant.
cases.notes: The Court reversed the trial court's judgment and affirmed the Georgia Supreme Court's decision. The majority reasoned that a reasonable person would not enter shared premises when one occupant explicitly refuses consent, even if another consents.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: An officer may conduct a traffic stop based on an anonymous tip reporting specific unsafe driving behavior if, under the totality of the circumstances, the tip contains sufficient indicia of reliability—such as eyewitness knowledge and temporal proximity to the reported incident—to establish reasonable suspicion that the driver is engaged in criminal conduct, even without independent police observation of the alleged behavior.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that indicia of reliability in the anonymous tip were insufficient to establish reasonable suspicion without corroborating police observation of impaired driving behavior.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. Majority held that reasonable suspicion for a traffic stop may be based on an anonymous tip with sufficient indicia of reliability under the totality of the circumstances. Dissent argued that the tip lacked sufficient trustworthiness absent police observation of the reported behavior.

cases.full_name: Williamson County Regional Planning Commission v. Hamilton Bank of Johnson City
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A takings claim is not ripe for federal adjudication until the state has had an opportunity to determine the amount of compensation through its own procedures.
cases.notes: Overruled by Knick v. Township of Scott (2019).

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The imposition of the death penalty under then-existing statutes constitutes cruel and unusual punishment in violation of the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendments when applied in an arbitrary and capricious manner.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the death penalty is unconstitutional in all instances.

opinions.author: Marshall
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the death penalty is unconstitutional in all instances.

opinions.author: Unknown
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Focused on the arbitrary and capricious nature of death sentence imposition, including racial bias against black defendants.

opinions.author: Unknown
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Opposed the invalidation of existing death penalty statutes.

cases.notes: Per curiam opinion with extensive concurrences and dissents spanning over two hundred pages. The decision was fractured: only Justices Brennan and Marshall believed the death penalty unconstitutional in all cases; other concurrences focused on arbitrary and discriminatory application. The decision effectively invalidated all then-existing death penalty statutes and forced states and Congress to revise capital punishment laws.

cases.full_name: Troy Gregg v. State of Georgia
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The imposition of the death sentence for murder under Georgia's capital sentencing statute, which provides for a bifurcated trial with guilt and penalty stages and requires the jury to find at least one aggravating circumstance beyond a reasonable doubt before imposing death, does not violate the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendments.
cases.notes: Plurality opinion announced by Justice Stewart and joined by Justices Powell and Stevens. This case upheld capital punishment as constitutional and established the framework for modern death penalty jurisprudence.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The death penalty constitutes cruel and unusual punishment under the Eighth Amendment when imposed for the rape of an adult woman, as it is grossly disproportionate to the offense and the vast majority of states do not authorize capital punishment for rape.
cases.notes: Plurality opinion. The holding reflects the position of the plurality that commanded the judgment. The Court found the death penalty excessive in severity and revocability for rape because it did not involve the taking of human life.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A sentence of life imprisonment without parole for a non-violent felony, when the defendant's prior offenses were relatively minor and not crimes against people, constitutes cruel and unusual punishment in violation of the Eighth Amendment.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Four justices dissented from the majority's proportionality analysis.

cases.notes: 5-4 decision. Justice Powell authored the majority opinion. The case establishes a proportionality test for Eighth Amendment cruel and unusual punishment claims in the context of sentencing.

cases.full_name: Daryl Renard Atkins v. Commonwealth of Virginia
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Executions of mentally retarded offenders constitute cruel and unusual punishment prohibited by the Eighth Amendment because such offenders have diminished culpability and the traditional justifications for capital punishment—retribution and deterrence—do not apply with equal force to them.

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's application of evolving standards of decency to prohibit execution of mentally retarded offenders.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Criticized the decision as turning capital trial into a game and objected to the expansion of Eighth Amendment protections.

cases.notes: Overruled Penry v. Lynaugh's holding that the Eighth Amendment does not categorically prohibit execution of mentally retarded offenders. Virginia Supreme Court had relied on Penry in affirming the death sentence.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Executing a minor does not violate the Eighth Amendment prohibition on cruel and unusual punishment because a majority of Americans do not consider such executions to be cruel and unusual.
cases.notes: Stub node — overruled by roper_v_simmons_2005. Full node to be added when this case is modeled.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Executing a minor constitutes cruel and unusual punishment prohibited by the Eighth Amendment because evolving standards of decency, reflected in state legislative consensus and international opinion, demonstrate that such executions are disproportionate punishment for juveniles.

opinions.author: Rehnquist
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed with the majority's reliance on evolving standards of decency and international opinion to overturn precedent.

cases.notes: Overrules Stanford v. Kentucky (1989) which held that executing minors was not unconstitutional. The majority relied on evolving standards of decency analysis similar to that used in Atkins v. Virginia.

cases.full_name: Patrick Kennedy v. State of Louisiana
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Eighth Amendment bars states from imposing the death penalty for the rape of a child where the crime did not result, and was not intended to result, in the child's death, as such punishment constitutes cruel and unusual punishment in violation of a national consensus on the issue.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that no national consensus existed prohibiting the death penalty for child rape and opposed the majority's blanket rule approach, contending that individual facts such as the child's age, the sadistic nature of the crime, and the number of rapes should be considered.

cases.notes: The Court applied a balancing test examining national consensus and excessiveness of punishment, drawing on methodology from Atkins v. Virginia and Roper v. Simmons. The majority distinguished Coker v. Georgia as applying to all rapes regardless of victim age.

cases.full_name: Terrance Jamar Graham v. State of Florida
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Eighth Amendment's Cruel and Unusual Punishments Clause prohibits sentencing a juvenile offender to life imprisonment without parole for a non-homicidal crime.

opinions.author: Roberts
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Chief Justice Roberts concurred in the judgment but disagreed with the majority's categorical approach, instead relying on narrow proportionality review of noncapital sentences and Roper's culpability analysis.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Justice Thomas argued the majority improperly substituted its moral judgment for that of the people and their elected representatives regarding the appropriateness of the sentence.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Justice Alito's separate dissent noted that the majority's holding does not affect sentences of a term of years without parole, and argued the Court should not have reached the as-applied question.

cases.notes: The majority applied categorical analysis under Atkins, Roper, and Kennedy precedents, examining both objective indicia of society's standards and constitutional guidance from controlling precedents. The Court noted that life sentences without parole for juveniles in non-homicidal crimes have been rejected worldwide.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Eighth Amendment's prohibition against cruel and unusual punishment forbids the mandatory sentencing of life in prison without the possibility of parole for juvenile homicide offenders. Children are constitutionally different from adults for sentencing purposes, and a mandatory life sentence without parole constitutes an unconstitutionally disproportionate punishment for juveniles.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued for additional requirement that offender actually killed or intended to kill the victim before mandatory life sentence could be imposed

opinions.author: Roberts
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued the Court exceeded its role by answering moral and policy questions; contended the punishment was not sufficiently characterized as unusual to violate the Eighth Amendment

cases.notes: Companion cases: Miller v. Alabama and Jackson v. Hobbs (Kuntrell Jackson). Both involved mandatory life without parole sentences for juveniles convicted of homicide. The Court's holding applies to both cases.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A suspect's waiver of Miranda rights is valid even when the suspect is unaware that an attorney has attempted to contact him and even when police have misled an attorney about their interrogation plans, provided the suspect was informed of his Miranda rights and voluntarily waived them without police coercion.
cases.notes: The opinion addresses whether police conduct in misleading an attorney about interrogation plans, or a suspect's ignorance of an attorney's efforts to reach him, taints the validity of Miranda waivers. The text indicates this is an opinion of the Court but does not identify the majority author in the provided excerpt.

cases.full_name: Jose Padilla v. Commonwealth of Kentucky
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Defense counsel must inform a client whether a guilty plea carries a risk of deportation, as such advice falls within the scope of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel and counsel's failure to provide it may constitute ineffective assistance.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Criticized the majority for adopting a halfway standard regarding when counsel must advise on immigration consequences, predicting confusion and needless litigation.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued the majority improperly expanded the Sixth Amendment beyond providing counsel to requiring sound advice on collateral consequences of conviction.

cases.notes: The Court remanded to the Kentucky Supreme Court for proceedings consistent with the opinion but did not address whether Padilla was prejudiced by counsel's deficiency and entitled to relief.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Sixth Amendment right to effective assistance of counsel extends to the negotiation and consideration of plea offers, including offers that lapse or are rejected, and a defendant need not show that the outcome of the trial would have been different to demonstrate prejudice from counsel's failure to communicate a plea offer.
cases.notes: The opinion addresses the scope of the Sixth Amendment right to effective assistance of counsel in the context of plea bargain negotiations. The Court clarifies that counsel's deficient performance in failing to communicate a lapsed plea offer can constitute ineffective assistance, and prejudice can be shown by demonstrating the defendant would have accepted the more favorable offer.

cases.full_name: Blaine Lafler, Warden v. Anthony Cooper
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: When a defendant receives ineffective assistance of counsel regarding a plea offer, the proper Strickland test is whether, absent the ineffective counsel, the defendant would have accepted the offered plea that was less severe than the eventual sentence, and whether the trial court would have accepted the plea terms; the remedy is not specific performance but rather the prosecution may re-offer the plea and, if accepted, the trial court may decide how to amend the sentence.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that there is no right to habeas relief when counsel's advice caused a defendant to have a full and fair trial, and that a criminal defendant has no right to a plea bargain.

opinions.author: Roberts
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Joined the Scalia dissent except for assertions that the majority's decision elevates the plea bargain to a constitutional right.

opinions.author: Alito
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Criticized the majority's opaque discussion of the remedy.

cases.notes: 5-4 fractured decision. Majority opinion by Kennedy. Three separate dissents. The case addresses the application of Strickland v. Washington to ineffective assistance of counsel claims arising from deficient advice regarding plea offers.

cases.full_name: Barron ex rel. Tiernan v. Mayor of Baltimore
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fifth Amendment's limitations on government, including the just compensation clause, apply only to the federal government and not to the states.
cases.notes: Chief Justice Marshall wrote for a unanimous Court. The decision established that the Bill of Rights was not applicable to the states, a principle that remained law until the Fourteenth Amendment incorporation doctrine developed in the twentieth century.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Sixth Amendment guarantee of trial by jury in criminal cases is fundamental to the American scheme of justice and is incorporated into the Fourteenth Amendment, obligating states to provide jury trials in criminal cases, except for petty crimes punishable by no more than six months in prison and a $500 fine.
cases.notes: The case established that the right to jury trial in criminal cases is incorporated against the states via the Fourteenth Amendment. Petty offenses are excluded from the jury trial requirement.

cases.full_name: Otis McDonald, et al. v. City of Chicago
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Fourteenth Amendment incorporates the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms for self-defense against the states through the Due Process Clause.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Agreed with the plurality opinion but wrote separately to respond to Justice Stevens' dissent.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Agreed that the Fourteenth Amendment incorporates the Second Amendment but advocated for the Privileges or Immunities Clause as the appropriate incorporation mechanism rather than the Due Process Clause.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Disagreed that the Fourteenth Amendment incorporates the Second Amendment, arguing that personal firearm ownership is not a liberty interest protected by the Due Process Clause.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Argued that the Second Amendment's text, history, and rationale do not characterize it as a fundamental right warranting incorporation through the Fourteenth Amendment.

cases.notes: Fractured decision. Justice Alito's plurality opinion commanded a majority on the incorporation of the Second Amendment through the Due Process Clause. Justice Thomas concurred in the judgment but would have used the Privileges or Immunities Clause. The holding reflects what commanded a majority (5 justices) on the incorporation question, though the mechanism was disputed.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Keating-Owen Child Labor Act is unconstitutional because production is not commerce within the scope of Congress's interstate commerce power, and regulation of production is reserved to the states by the Tenth Amendment.

opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissented from the majority's invalidation of the child labor statute

cases.notes: The majority opinion revised the Tenth Amendment by adding the word 'expressly,' which does not appear in the constitutional text. The framers deliberately omitted 'expressly' from the amendment.

cases.short_name: Schechter Poultry Corp. v. United States
cases.full_name: A. L. A. Schechter Poultry Corporation v. United States
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress cannot delegate legislative authority to the President without establishing intelligible principles or standards to guide the President's exercise of that authority; the National Industrial Recovery Act violated the non-delegation doctrine by granting the President unbridled discretion to regulate industry without meaningful legislative standards.

opinions.author: Cardozo
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Agreed with the majority that the Act violated the non-delegation doctrine but emphasized the absence of intelligible principles and standards.

cases.notes: Unanimous decision. Chief Justice Hughes authored the majority opinion. Justice Cardozo wrote a concurring opinion joined by Justice Stone.

cases.full_name: Alberto R. Gonzales, Attorney General, et al. v. Angel McClary Raich, et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Congress has authority under the Commerce Clause to prohibit the local cultivation and use of marijuana, even when such use is authorized by state law, because intrastate marijuana use is part of a class of activities that substantially affects interstate commerce in the national marijuana market.

opinions.author: Unknown
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissented from the majority's broad interpretation of Commerce Clause authority

cases.notes: The Court distinguished this case from U.S. v. Lopez (1995) and U.S. v. Morrison (2000) on the ground that the CSA regulated economic activity as part of a valid statutory scheme, whereas those cases involved non-economic activity falling entirely outside Congress' commerce power.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The treaty power, when exercised in conjunction with congressional legislation implementing a treaty, may regulate matters within the national interest that would otherwise fall within state sovereignty under the Tenth Amendment, and such treaty-based regulations are supreme law under the Supremacy Clause and not subject to state challenge.
cases.notes: The Court upheld the Migratory Bird Treaty Act as a valid exercise of the treaty power. The decision establishes that the treaty power, combined with the Supremacy Clause, permits federal regulation of matters that might otherwise be reserved to the states.

cases.full_name: Philip D. Murphy, Governor of New Jersey, et al. v. National Collegiate Athletic Association, et al.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Professional and Amateur Sports Protection Act's provisions prohibiting states from authorizing or licensing sports gambling schemes violate the anticommandeering principle of the Tenth Amendment because they issue direct orders to state legislatures, and Congress lacks the constitutional power to regulate state legislative processes.

opinions.author: Thomas
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Separate concurring opinion

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Concurred in part and dissented in part; joined majority on all but Part VI-B

opinions.author: Ginsburg
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Dissented from the majority's anticommandeering analysis

cases.notes: Consolidated cases Nos. 16-476 and 16-477. Majority opinion joined by Roberts, Kennedy, Thomas, Kagan, and Gorsuch; Breyer joined all but Part VI-B. Ginsburg's dissent joined by Sotomayor and Breyer in part.

doctrinal_tests.label: Central Hudson Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The four-part intermediate scrutiny test governing government regulation of commercial speech. All four prongs must be satisfied for a regulation to survive constitutional challenge. The government bears the burden of justification.

prongs.label: Lawful and non-misleading
prongs.description: The speech must concern lawful activity and must not be misleading or fraudulent. If this threshold is not met, the speech receives no First Amendment protection and the analysis ends.
prongs.burden_note: Threshold requirement. If not met, no protection attaches and government may freely regulate.

prongs.label: Substantial government interest
prongs.description: The government must assert a substantial interest in restricting the speech. A merely reasonable or rational interest is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must identify and demonstrate a substantial — not merely legitimate — interest.

prongs.label: Directly advances
prongs.description: The regulation must directly and materially advance the asserted government interest. The government must demonstrate a reasonable fit between the regulation and the interest — a mere speculative connection is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the regulation actually advances the interest, not just that it might.

prongs.label: No more extensive than necessary
prongs.description: The regulation must be no more extensive than necessary to serve the government's interest. Some Justices have argued this requires the 'least restrictive means' available; the Court has generally applied a 'reasonable fit' standard rather than a least-restrictive-means requirement.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show reasonable fit. Circuit split exists on whether 'least restrictive means' is required. See 44 Liquormart (1996) concurrences.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Prong 4 has generated the most doctrinal tension. Justice Thomas has argued commercial speech should receive full First Amendment protection, eliminating the need for Central Hudson entirely. Sorrell (2011) left open whether content- or speaker-based restrictions trigger a higher standard.

doctrinal_tests.label: Brandenburg Incitement Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for determining when advocacy of illegal conduct loses First Amendment protection. Speech may be proscribed as incitement only if it meets both the intent prong (directed to producing imminent lawless action) and the likelihood prong (likely to produce such action). Both prongs must be satisfied. The test is conjunctive — meeting one prong is insufficient.

prongs.label: Directed to imminent lawless action
prongs.description: The speech must be directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action. 'Directed to' requires intent — mere recklessness is insufficient. 'Imminent' excludes abstract advocacy of future illegality, however forceful. The speaker must be aiming at producing immediate unlawful conduct.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove both intent and imminence. Abstract advocacy, even of violent overthrow, is protected.
prongs.contest_note: The imminence requirement is contested at the margins. Courts have disagreed on how immediate 'imminent' must be — hours? days? Some circuits have found imminence satisfied where violence was threatened for the near future but not the immediate moment. Hess v. Indiana (1973) operationalized imminence to require that violence be directed at the present, not future, time.

prongs.label: Likely to produce imminent lawless action
prongs.description: The speech must be likely to produce the imminent lawless action. This is an objective probability assessment — is there a realistic likelihood that the speech will actually cause immediate unlawful conduct given the circumstances? Mere tendency to lead to violence, or likelihood of future harm, is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate realistic probability of imminent harm, not mere possibility or tendency.
prongs.contest_note: The likelihood prong intersects with context in ways courts have handled inconsistently. Online speech contexts have generated significant disagreement — when is violent speech online 'likely' to produce imminent violence, given the disconnect between online advocacy and real-world action? Post-January 6 litigation has brought this to the foreground in lower courts.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Brandenburg per curiam was brief and left significant interpretive questions open. The test's intellectual origin lies in Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927) — both of which insisted on imminence as a constitutional requirement — but those were minority positions for 40+ years. SCHEMA ISSUE: source_case_id points to Brandenburg, but the doctrinal content traces to non-majority opinions in earlier cases. The schema cannot currently represent that the 'source' of a test's intellectual substance differs from the case that made it majority law.

doctrinal_tests.label: True Threats Test (Post-Counterman)
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional framework for evaluating whether a statement constitutes an unprotected true threat. Counterman v. Colorado (2023) held that the First Amendment requires proof of a subjective mental state — at minimum recklessness — before a statement can be punished as a true threat. A purely objective reasonable-person standard is constitutionally insufficient.

prongs.label: Objectively threatening statement
prongs.description: The statement must communicate a serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence against an identifiable individual or group. This is assessed from the perspective of a reasonable recipient in context.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the statement would be understood by a reasonable person as expressing serious violent intent.

prongs.label: Subjective mens rea — recklessness minimum
prongs.description: The speaker must have been subjectively aware of the threatening nature of the communications. At minimum, the speaker must have consciously disregarded a substantial and unjustifiable risk that their statements would be perceived as threatening. Higher mental states (knowledge, purpose) also suffice. Negligence — should have known — is constitutionally insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove at minimum that the defendant recklessly disregarded the threatening nature of the communication. Objective reasonable-person standard alone is insufficient post-Counterman.
prongs.contest_note: Counterman's majority held that recklessness is the constitutional floor but left open whether higher mental states are required. Justice Barrett (majority) and Justice Thomas (dissent) disagreed sharply on whether the First Amendment requires any subjective mental state for true threats. Circuit courts are actively working out: (1) whether purpose or knowledge is required in some contexts; (2) how to instruct juries on recklessness; (3) whether Counterman applies retroactively to convictions under objective-standard statutes. Active circuit split expected to produce cert petition within 2-3 years.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Counterman is the Court's most significant true threats decision since Virginia v. Black (2003). The recklessness standard resolves a long-standing circuit split but creates new interpretive questions. Pre-Counterman, the majority of circuits applied purely objective standards; post-Counterman those standards are constitutionally deficient. State statutes with objective-only true threats standards are now vulnerable to facial or as-applied challenges.

doctrinal_tests.label: Prior Restraint Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for evaluating government attempts to suppress speech before publication. Established in Near v. Minnesota and refined in New York Times v. United States, the test requires the government to meet an extremely heavy burden. The presumption against prior restraints is nearly absolute; the government must show that publication will directly and immediately cause serious harm that cannot be remedied after the fact.

prongs.label: Presumption of unconstitutionality
prongs.description: Any prior restraint on expression comes with a heavy presumption against its constitutional validity. The government must rebut this presumption affirmatively; the speaker need not demonstrate harm from the restraint.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of overcoming the presumption. Burden is higher than for subsequent punishment of equivalent speech.

prongs.label: Direct, immediate, and irreparable harm
prongs.description: The government must show that publication will cause direct, immediate, and irreparable harm. Speculative harm, remote harm, or harm that could be addressed through subsequent punishment is insufficient. The classic example from Near is publication of troop movements in wartime that would directly endanger soldiers.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate concrete, near-certain harm — not speculation or general concerns about national security, public order, or reputational damage.
prongs.contest_note: The standard for 'direct and immediate' harm is contested in national security contexts. Pentagon Papers (1971) held that the Nixon administration's showing was insufficient, but the six concurrences disagreed sharply on what would constitute a sufficient showing. The district court in Progressive (1979) found the H-bomb article sufficient — the only federal court to ever uphold a prior restraint on national security grounds — but the case was mooted before appellate review. The contested zone is: how concrete and certain must the harm be, and who decides?

prongs.label: No adequate alternative remedy
prongs.description: The harm must be one that cannot be adequately addressed through subsequent punishment or other less restrictive means. If criminal prosecution or civil liability after publication would adequately deter and remedy the harm, a prior restraint is not justified.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show that subsequent punishment is inadequate — that the speech, once published, causes irreversible harm that cannot be meaningfully remedied.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test as articulated is a distillation from Near and Pentagon Papers. No single majority opinion has cleanly enumerated all three prongs — they emerge from the weight of the doctrine across multiple cases. SCHEMA ISSUE C: Pentagon Papers produced nine separate opinions, none commanding a majority. The doctrinal content of what the government must show is assembled from concurrences and dissents, not from a majority holding. The schema's source_case_id points to Near as the doctrinal source, but the test's operational prongs are substantially shaped by Pentagon Papers — a case with no majority opinion.

doctrinal_tests.label: Madsen Content-Neutral Injunction Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for evaluating court injunctions that restrict speech or expressive activity in a content-neutral manner. Established in Madsen v. Women's Health Center (1994), this test is more demanding than ordinary time/place/manner review but less stringent than the near-absolute prior restraint standard. It applies specifically to injunctions issued against specific parties, not generally applicable laws.

prongs.label: Content neutrality
prongs.description: The injunction must be content-neutral — directed at the conduct of specific parties before the court, not at the message or viewpoint being expressed. An injunction targeting anti-abortion protesters specifically because of their message would be content-based and subject to strict scrutiny, not the Madsen standard.
prongs.burden_note: Court must find that the injunction's restrictions stem from the conduct and its effects, not from the content or viewpoint of the speech.
prongs.contest_note: Scalia's dissent in Madsen argued that injunctions targeting a specific group engaged in a specific protest are inherently content-based — you cannot separate the conduct from the message in protest contexts. This tension has not been definitively resolved and resurfaces in cases involving targeted protest injunctions.

prongs.label: Burdens no more speech than necessary
prongs.description: The injunction must burden no more speech than necessary to serve the significant governmental interests at stake. This is a proportionality inquiry: the restriction must be tailored to the harm, not broader than needed. Courts must examine each provision of the injunction separately against this standard.
prongs.burden_note: Government (or plaintiff seeking injunction) must show each restriction is necessary. Overbroad provisions are severable and may be invalidated without voiding the entire injunction.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Madsen is important not just for the test but for the institutional holding that courts may issue injunctions restricting speech when needed to protect the rights of others — but that those injunctions are subject to constitutional scrutiny. The Madsen test has been applied and refined in Schenck v. Pro-Choice Network (1997) and Hill v. Colorado (2000), both of which are outside this slice's scope.

doctrinal_tests.label: Actual Malice Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for determining whether a public official or public figure plaintiff may recover damages in a defamation action against a press or media defendant. Plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant published the statement with knowledge of its falsity or with reckless disregard for whether it was true or false. The standard is subjective and defendant-focused: it asks what the defendant actually believed, not what a reasonable publisher would have believed.

prongs.label: Public official or public figure plaintiff
prongs.description: The plaintiff must be a public official, all-purpose public figure, or limited-purpose public figure. Private figures are governed by the Gertz standard, not actual malice. The categorization of who qualifies as a public figure is itself a contested doctrinal question with its own factors.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant may raise plaintiff's status as a threshold issue. Court determines plaintiff's status as a matter of law.
prongs.contest_note: The boundary between limited-purpose public figures and private figures is heavily litigated and inconsistently applied across circuits. A person becomes a limited-purpose public figure by voluntarily injecting themselves into a particular public controversy — but what counts as voluntary, and which controversies qualify, varies. SCHEMA ISSUE A: The public figure taxonomy (public official, all-purpose public figure, limited-purpose public figure, private figure) is a classification framework that functions as a precondition to the test. It does not fit cleanly as a prong because it is itself a multi-factor inquiry with its own sub-criteria. The schema has no way to represent a structured pre-test classification as part of a DoctrinalTest.

prongs.label: Knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard
prongs.description: Plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that: (a) defendant knew the statement was false at the time of publication, OR (b) defendant acted with reckless disregard for whether the statement was true or false. Reckless disregard requires proof that the defendant subjectively entertained serious doubts about the truth of the statement. Negligence — even gross negligence — is constitutionally insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Clear and convincing evidence standard — higher than preponderance but lower than beyond reasonable doubt. Plaintiff must prove defendant's subjective state of mind, which is typically established through circumstantial evidence of the defendant's newsgathering process.
prongs.contest_note: The subjective recklessness standard — defendant must have entertained serious doubts — was established in St. Amant v. Thompson (1968). The standard for what constitutes 'reckless disregard' in the context of hot-button political reporting, social media republication, and AI-generated content is an active area of development. Some courts have struggled with applying a subjective standard to institutional defendants (corporations, news organizations) where no individual decision-maker had the requisite state of mind.

prongs.label: Statement of fact, not opinion
prongs.description: The statement must be capable of being proven false — i.e., it must be a statement of fact, not pure opinion or rhetorical hyperbole. A statement that cannot reasonably be interpreted as stating actual facts cannot satisfy the falsity element and is protected regardless of the speaker's intent.
prongs.burden_note: Whether a statement is fact or opinion is a question of law for the court. Plaintiff bears the burden of establishing the statement is capable of being proven false.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The actual malice standard has been extended beyond classic press defamation to: IIED claims by public figures (Hustler v. Falwell, 1988), false light invasion of privacy, and some tortious interference claims. The extension pattern is modeled through MODIFIES edges from the relevant cases.

doctrinal_tests.label: Public Figure Classification Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The framework for determining whether a defamation plaintiff is a public official, all-purpose public figure, limited-purpose public figure, or private figure — which in turn determines which constitutional standard governs the claim. Established across NYT v. Sullivan (public officials), Curtis Publishing v. Butts (public figures), and Gertz v. Robert Welch (private figures and the public figure taxonomy).

prongs.label: Public official
prongs.description: Government employees who have or appear to have substantial responsibility for or control over the conduct of governmental affairs. The category is not limited to elected officials — it includes appointed officials in positions with significant public trust. The line between public officials and low-level government employees is fact-specific.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must demonstrate plaintiff held a position of significant governmental responsibility. Courts look to the nature of the position, not just its title.

prongs.label: All-purpose public figure
prongs.description: Individuals who have achieved pervasive fame or notoriety in the community — celebrities, major public personalities. These individuals are public figures for all purposes and contexts. They must prove actual malice for any defamatory statement, regardless of whether it concerns their public role.
prongs.burden_note: Courts apply this category narrowly. True all-purpose public figures are rare — most cases involve limited-purpose public figures.

prongs.label: Limited-purpose public figure
prongs.description: Individuals who voluntarily inject themselves into a particular public controversy and thereby become public figures for the limited range of issues related to that controversy. They must prove actual malice only for statements relating to the controversy that made them public figures.
prongs.burden_note: Most heavily litigated category. Courts assess: (1) whether a public controversy existed, (2) whether plaintiff voluntarily thrust themselves into it, (3) whether the alleged defamation relates to plaintiff's role in that controversy.
prongs.contest_note: The voluntary injection requirement and the scope of the relevant controversy are inconsistently applied. Some circuits require active voluntary participation; others find that being named in a public controversy, even involuntarily, can make someone a limited-purpose public figure. What constitutes a 'public controversy' distinct from merely a matter of public interest is also unsettled.

prongs.label: Private figure
prongs.description: Anyone who does not qualify as a public official, all-purpose public figure, or limited-purpose public figure. Private figure plaintiffs need only prove fault (negligence or higher, set by state law) and are limited to actual damages unless they prove actual malice.
prongs.burden_note: For matters of public concern: plaintiff proves fault at whatever standard state law imposes (minimum: negligence). For matters not of public concern: state law has broader latitude.

doctrinal_tests.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE A (second instance): This test functions as a threshold classification that determines which other test applies. It is a meta-test — a test about which test governs — rather than a test applied to the merits. The schema has no way to represent a DoctrinalTest that is a precondition to another DoctrinalTest rather than an independent test applied to the underlying facts. The GOVERNED_BY edge from freedom_of_press to this test is accurate, but the conditional relationship between this test and actual_malice_test is not expressible in the current edge vocabulary.

doctrinal_tests.label: Sherbert Compelling Interest Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The pre-Smith constitutional standard for Free Exercise challenges. The government must satisfy strict scrutiny to enforce a law that substantially burdens religious practice: a compelling governmental interest pursued through the least restrictive means available. Established in Sherbert v. Verner (1963), extended in Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972). Superseded by Employment Division v. Smith (1990) for neutral, generally applicable laws, but remains operative for non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws and has been restored by statute (RFRA) for federal laws.

prongs.label: Substantial burden on religious practice
prongs.description: The law or government action must substantially burden the plaintiff's sincerely held religious belief or practice. A de minimis burden does not trigger the test. Courts do not evaluate the reasonableness or centrality of the belief — only its sincerity and the burden's substantiality.
prongs.burden_note: Plaintiff establishes prima facie case by showing substantial burden on sincere religious practice.
prongs.contest_note: What constitutes a 'substantial burden' is contested. Some circuits require that the government directly compel action contrary to religious belief; others find substantial burden where government conditions a significant benefit on conduct that violates religious belief (the Sherbert-Hobson's-choice pattern). The RFRA substantial burden standard has also generated significant circuit disagreement.

prongs.label: Compelling governmental interest
prongs.description: The government must demonstrate a compelling interest in enforcing the law against the religious objector. A general interest in uniform enforcement is insufficient — the government must show a particularized compelling interest in denying the specific exemption sought.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating compelling interest. The interest must be compelling as applied to the claimant, not just compelling in the abstract.

prongs.label: Least restrictive means
prongs.description: The government must pursue the compelling interest through the least restrictive means available. If a less burdensome alternative would achieve the same compelling interest, the government must use it.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating no less restrictive alternative is available.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Status: modified rather than overruled. Smith did not overrule Sherbert across the board — it limited Sherbert to non-neutral, non-generally-applicable laws. Sherbert remains operative in its original domain (unemployment compensation cases and analogous individualized exemption systems) and was restored by RFRA for federal laws. The Sherbert test is also the model for RFRA's statutory compelling interest test. SCHEMA ISSUE B: RFRA (42 U.S.C. § 2000bb) restored the Sherbert test as a statutory matter after Smith abandoned it constitutionally. The graph models the constitutional Sherbert test but cannot represent that a statute has restored an abandoned constitutional standard. The constitutional test and the statutory test now coexist and are applied in different contexts. Phase 2 statute layer problem.

doctrinal_tests.label: Smith Neutral General Law Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The post-1990 constitutional standard for Free Exercise challenges to neutral, generally applicable laws. Under Employment Division v. Smith, the Free Amendment does not relieve an individual of the obligation to comply with a valid and neutral law of general applicability on the ground that the law proscribes conduct that his religion prescribes. A neutral, generally applicable law that incidentally burdens religious practice does not require compelling interest justification — it need only satisfy rational basis review. The threshold question is whether the law is in fact neutral and generally applicable.

prongs.label: Law is neutral
prongs.description: The law's object must not be to target, restrict, or burden religious practice. A law is not neutral if it singles out religious conduct for special burdens, if it exempts comparable secular conduct while burdening religious conduct, or if its purpose (as revealed by text, history, or enforcement) is to disadvantage a particular religion or religion generally.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears initial burden of demonstrating the law is not neutral. If challenger establishes non-neutrality, burden shifts to government to satisfy strict scrutiny.
prongs.contest_note: The neutrality inquiry is contested at the margins. How much anti-religious legislative history is required to make a facially neutral law non-neutral?
