When does selective enforcement evidence non-neutrality? These questions are resolved inconsistently across circuits. Masterpiece Cakeshop v. Colorado Civil Rights Commission (2018) found non-neutrality based on commissioners' hostile statements — but the scope of that holding is disputed.

prongs.label: Law is generally applicable
prongs.description: The law must apply across the board without carving out exceptions for comparable secular conduct. A law is not generally applicable if it creates a system of individualized exemptions that allows secular reasons to excuse compliance but not religious ones, or if it exempts some comparable secular conduct while burdening religious conduct. Fulton (2021) significantly tightened this prong: even a categorical exception that permits discretionary secular exemptions makes the law not generally applicable.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the law is not generally applicable by showing comparable secular conduct is exempted. The comparison class — what counts as 'comparable' — is a contested question.
prongs.contest_note: The general applicability inquiry is the most actively litigated aspect of post-Smith doctrine. What counts as a 'comparable' secular exemption? How many secular exceptions are needed before a law loses general applicability? Fulton held that a single discretionary exemption for secular reasons suffices to make a law not generally applicable — but the scope of Fulton's rule is being actively worked out in lower courts. Multiple circuits have disagreed on application.

prongs.label: Rational basis if neutral and generally applicable
prongs.description: If the law is both neutral and generally applicable, it need only satisfy rational basis review to survive Free Exercise challenge — the government need not demonstrate a compelling interest or least restrictive means. The religious objector has no constitutional right to an exemption from a valid neutral general law.
prongs.burden_note: If the law is neutral and generally applicable, the government need only show a rational basis. Burden effectively shifts to challenger to show the law lacks even a rational basis, which is nearly impossible to establish.
prongs.contest_note: The rational basis tier for neutral general laws is contested at the Supreme Court level — not in application but in validity. At least four sitting justices (Alito, Thomas, Gorsuch, and Barrett in Fulton) have questioned whether Smith correctly held that neutral general laws only require rational basis. If Smith is overruled, this prong disappears entirely and Sherbert's compelling interest standard is restored. SCHEMA ISSUE C: This is the first instance in the graph of a prong that is contested not because of application disagreements but because the test itself may be overruled. The contested flag was designed for circuit splits on prong meaning — not for intra-Court challenges to the test's existence.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Smith is the most contested Free Exercise precedent. Scalia's majority opinion was joined by only four other justices; O'Connor, joined by Brennan, Marshall, and Blackmun, concurred in the judgment but rejected the majority's reasoning and argued Sherbert should have been applied. The 5-4 split on reasoning (though not on result) foreshadowed the precedent's contested status. Justice Alito has written extensively against Smith in Fulton concurrences and other writings. The Barrett-Kavanaugh-Thomas concurrence in Fulton explicitly invited reconsideration.

doctrinal_tests.label: Lemon Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The three-part Establishment Clause test established in Lemon v. Kurtzman (1971) and explicitly overruled in Kennedy v. Bremerton School District (2022). For 51 years, a government action survived Establishment Clause scrutiny only if it: (1) had a secular legislative purpose, (2) had a primary effect that neither advanced nor inhibited religion, and (3) did not foster excessive entanglement with religion. The test was widely criticized by justices across the ideological spectrum for producing inconsistent results, but it survived until Kennedy because no majority could agree on a replacement.

prongs.label: Secular purpose
prongs.description: The government action must have a secular legislative purpose. A purely religious purpose is unconstitutional. Courts look to stated purposes but may look beyond them if the stated purpose is a sham.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show the government action lacks any secular purpose. Courts gave government wide latitude here — almost any plausible secular purpose satisfied this prong.
prongs.contest_note: The secular purpose prong was widely criticized as toothless — courts rarely found a government action to lack any secular purpose. Justice Scalia called the prong 'a ghoul in a horror movie that repeatedly kill[s] and refuse[s] to die' in his concurrence in Lamb's Chapel v. Center Moriches Union Free School District (1993) — he concurred in the judgment while criticizing the Lemon test. The prong's weakness contributed to the Lemon test's eventual overruling.

prongs.label: Primary effect
prongs.description: The government action's principal or primary effect must neither advance nor inhibit religion. The Endorsement Test (Justice O'Connor) reformulated this prong: the government may not send a message to nonadherents that they are outsiders or a message to adherents that they are insiders.
prongs.burden_note: This prong generated significant litigation. Courts examined whether the government action would be perceived by a reasonable observer as endorsing religion.
prongs.contest_note: The Endorsement Test reformulation (Lynch v. Donnelly, 1984, O'Connor concurrence; County of Allegheny v. ACLU, 1989) was adopted by some justices but not all. The test asked what a reasonable observer would understand — but courts disagreed on who the reasonable observer was and what they knew. The Endorsement Test and the Lemon primary effect prong coexisted uneasily for decades.

prongs.label: No excessive entanglement
prongs.description: The government action must not foster excessive entanglement between government and religion. Entanglement includes both institutional entanglement (ongoing government supervision of religious organizations) and political entanglement (government action that generates divisive political battles along religious lines).
prongs.burden_note: This prong was used primarily to strike down programs requiring ongoing government monitoring of religious institutions receiving government funds.
prongs.contest_note: The excessive entanglement prong was eventually absorbed into the primary effect inquiry in Agostini v. Felton (1997), reducing Lemon from a three-part to an effectively two-part test in practice. The fact that courts were silently modifying the test's structure without formally overruling it contributed to its eventual demise.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The Lemon test is the first fully overruled DoctrinalTest in this graph. valid_until: 2022-06-27 (date of Kennedy v. Bremerton decision). status: overruled. The test governed Establishment Clause analysis for 51 years despite persistent criticism. Justice Scalia's Lamb's Chapel v. Center Moriches (1993) concurrence mocked the test as a 'ghoul in a horror movie.' Multiple justices called for its replacement in multiple cases. Kennedy v. Bremerton finally provided a majority willing to overrule it. SCHEMA ISSUE A: This is the first instance of a fully overruled DoctrinalTest with valid_until populated. The temporal model handles this correctly, but the visualization and query layer need to ensure overruled tests are displayed differently from active ones — particularly that queries for 'what test currently governs this area' exclude overruled tests.

doctrinal_tests.label: Coercion Test
doctrinal_tests.description: An Establishment Clause analytical framework focusing on whether government action coerces religious participation. Articulated in Justice Kennedy's Lee v. Weisman majority (1992): the Establishment Clause bars government from coercing anyone to support or participate in religion or its exercise. The coercion test asks whether government action exerts direct or indirect coercive pressure toward religious participation. It was developed as an alternative to Lemon in the school prayer context and remains operative post-Kennedy as a standalone analytical framework.

prongs.label: Government direction or control of religious activity
prongs.description: The government must have directed, controlled, or sponsored the religious activity at issue. Purely private religious expression that happens to occur on government property or at government events does not satisfy this prong.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show government involvement in directing or sponsoring the religious activity — not merely permitting it.
prongs.contest_note: The line between government direction and government permission is contested. Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) held that a public school football coach's post-game prayers were his own private religious expression, not government-directed — even though he was a government employee acting in his official capacity on school grounds. The dissent argued this mischaracterized the coercion inherent in the coach's supervisory relationship with players.

prongs.label: Coercion of religious participation
prongs.description: The government action must coerce participation in religious activity — either directly (legal compulsion) or indirectly (social pressure, implicit pressure from authority figures, peer pressure in school contexts). Lee v. Weisman held that even subtle indirect pressure can constitute unconstitutional coercion in the school context, where students are legally required to attend and are subject to authority.
prongs.burden_note: Courts have recognized that coercion need not be direct legal compulsion — indirect pressure, particularly in school settings where students are captive and subject to authority, can satisfy this prong.
prongs.contest_note: How much indirect pressure constitutes coercion is actively contested. The school prayer cases apply a sensitive coercion standard; cases involving adult audiences apply a more demanding standard. Kennedy v. Bremerton's majority found no coercion from a coach's personal prayers — the dissent argued this ignored the realistic coercive pressure on players. The post-Kennedy scope of the coercion standard in non-school contexts is unsettled.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The coercion test was Justice Kennedy's preferred alternative to Lemon, articulated in Lee v. Weisman. It survived Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) as a distinct analytical framework — the Kennedy majority did not overrule the coercion test, only Lemon. The coercion test is particularly important in school prayer contexts where the captive audience and authority relationships make indirect pressure more salient.

doctrinal_tests.label: Expressive Association Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional framework for evaluating whether a group has a First Amendment right to exclude members whose presence would burden the group's expressive message. Established in Roberts v. United States Jaycees (1984) and applied in Boy Scouts of America v. Dale (2000). The test asks whether the group engages in expressive activity, whether forced inclusion would significantly affect the group's ability to express its views, and whether the government's interest in preventing discrimination justifies the associational burden.

prongs.label: Group engages in expressive association
prongs.description: The group must engage in some form of expression — advocating a viewpoint, promoting a message, engaging in activities protected by the First Amendment. A group that exists purely for social purposes without an expressive dimension receives less protection than one with a clear expressive purpose.
prongs.burden_note: Group bears initial burden of establishing it engages in expressive association. Courts look to the group's stated purpose, activities, and messaging.
prongs.contest_note: What counts as 'expressive' association is contested at the margins. Dale expanded the category significantly — the Boy Scouts' opposition to homosexuality was found to be part of its expressive mission even though the Scouts had never publicly articulated that position. Critics argued this definition of 'expressive' was so broad as to give any organization with any viewpoint a license to discriminate.

prongs.label: Forced inclusion significantly burdens expressive message
prongs.description: Compelling the group to accept the member must significantly affect the group's ability to advocate its viewpoint. The government cannot force a group to include a member whose presence would impair the group's expression of its message in a significant way.
prongs.burden_note: Group must show that including the member would significantly burden its message — not merely that it prefers not to include them. The more clearly the member's presence conflicts with the group's expressive mission, the stronger the protection.
prongs.contest_note: The 'significantly burdens' standard is applied deferentially — courts generally accept the group's characterization of what burdens its message. This deference was criticized in Dale: the Court accepted the Boy Scouts' post-hoc articulation of its anti-homosexuality message without requiring that the position be actually expressed or consistently held.

prongs.label: Government interest does not justify the burden
prongs.description: The government's interest in compelling inclusion — typically anti-discrimination law — must be weighed against the associational burden. The more purely expressive the group's purpose, the less likely the government interest will override it. A large, quasi-commercial organization receives less protection than a small, ideologically cohesive advocacy group.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify the anti-discrimination interest against the associational burden. The Roberts Court drew a distinction between large commercial organizations (Jaycees — required to admit women) and intimate expressive associations (more protected).

doctrinal_tests.notes: source_case_id points to Roberts (1984) which established the test and has the ESTABLISHES edge. Dale (2000) significantly expanded and modified the test through a MODIFIES(expands) edge — Dale is the operative statement of how the test works in practice, but Roberts is the canonical source. The expressive association test was initially articulated in Roberts (1984) but its current form reflects Dale (2000), which significantly expanded the scope of expressive association protection and the deference courts give to groups' claims about their expressive purposes. source_case_id points to Dale as the operative statement of the test.

doctrinal_tests.label: Compelled Disclosure Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for evaluating government requirements that compel disclosure of associational membership or donor identity. Established in NAACP v. Alabama (1958) and significantly tightened in Americans for Prosperity Foundation v. Bonta (2021), which held that disclosure requirements are subject to exacting scrutiny: they must be narrowly tailored to a substantial government interest.

prongs.label: Compelled disclosure of protected associational information
prongs.description: The government must be compelling disclosure of membership lists, donor identities, or organizational affiliations that are protected by the First Amendment. Not all disclosure requirements trigger this test — routine business records and public organizational information do not implicate associational privacy.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the disclosure requirement reaches protected associational information, not merely public or non-sensitive organizational data.

prongs.label: Chilling effect on protected association
prongs.description: The disclosure requirement must create a reasonable probability of harm to members — harassment, retaliation, or deterrence of protected associational activity. The chilling effect need not be proven with certainty; a reasonable probability of harm suffices.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate a reasonable probability that disclosure would chill protected First Amendment activity. Courts look to evidence of past harassment or the nature of the organization's activities.
prongs.contest_note: What constitutes a sufficient showing of chilling effect has varied across circuits. Americans for Prosperity Foundation v. Bonta (2021) held that the state's blanket requirement that charities disclose major donors to the Attorney General was not narrowly tailored even without proof of specific harassment — the Court presumed the chilling effect from the nature of the requirement.

prongs.label: Substantial government interest, narrowly tailored
prongs.description: Post-Bonta, the government must demonstrate that its disclosure requirement is narrowly tailored to a substantial interest. A blanket disclosure requirement that is not limited to cases where disclosure is actually necessary to serve the government's interest fails this prong. The government cannot satisfy this prong by asserting a general regulatory interest in knowing who funds organizations.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating narrow tailoring. Bonta rejected California's blanket donor disclosure requirement as insufficiently tailored even to a legitimate anti-fraud interest.
prongs.contest_note: Bonta significantly tightened the compelled disclosure test by requiring narrow tailoring rather than merely a substantial relationship between disclosure and a state interest. The precise scope of Bonta's narrow tailoring requirement is being worked out in lower courts — particularly whether it effectively establishes strict scrutiny for donor disclosure or a somewhat less demanding standard.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The compelled disclosure test connects to the anonymous speech doctrine in core_political_speech. Both protect the ability to engage in expressive activity without government-compelled identification. Buckley v. Valeo (1976) established a somewhat different (more permissive) standard for campaign finance disclosure that exists in tension with the NAACP/Bonta line — that tension is a Phase 2 issue requiring the campaign finance doctrine to be modeled.

doctrinal_tests.label: Tinker Substantial Disruption Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The governing test for student speech in public schools. School officials may restrict student expression only when they can reasonably forecast that the speech will substantially disrupt or materially interfere with school activities or the rights of other students. The test requires specific, articulable facts supporting the forecast — not speculation or undifferentiated fear. Established in Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (1969).

prongs.label: Student expression on school grounds
prongs.description: The expression must occur in the school context — on school grounds, at school events, or in school-sponsored activities. The scope of 'school context' for off-campus digital speech is an actively contested question not yet resolved by the Supreme Court.
prongs.burden_note: Student establishes prima facie case by demonstrating expression occurred in school context.
prongs.contest_note: The application of Tinker to off-campus social media speech is heavily contested in lower courts. Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (2021) addressed a cheerleader's off-campus Snapchat but the Court declined to set a clear rule, holding only that the school had overstepped in that particular case.

prongs.label: Substantial disruption or material interference
prongs.description: School officials must demonstrate a reasonable forecast — based on specific, articulable facts — that the student expression will substantially disrupt school operations or materially interfere with the rights of other students. Past incidents, credible threats, or specific evidence of planned disruption may support the forecast. Speculation, displeasure with the message, or desire to avoid controversy is insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of demonstrating substantial disruption or material interference. The showing must be based on specific facts, not generalized concern.
prongs.contest_note: What constitutes 'substantial disruption' is contested at the margins. Courts disagree on whether a school can suppress speech in anticipation of third-party disruption (the heckler's veto problem), and on how much weight to give to evidence that the speech topic is controversial in the school community.

doctrinal_tests.label: Miller Obscenity Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The three-part test for identifying obscenity established in Miller v. California (1973). Material is obscene and unprotected if it satisfies all three prongs. The test replaced the Memoirs v. Massachusetts 'utterly without redeeming social value' standard with a more workable framework that allows community standards to define the first two prongs while applying a national standard to the third.

prongs.label: Prurient interest appeal
prongs.description: Whether the average person, applying contemporary community standards, would find that the work, taken as a whole, appeals to the prurient interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish community standards and the work's appeal to prurient interest.

prongs.label: Patently offensive depiction
prongs.description: Whether the work depicts or describes, in a patently offensive way, sexual conduct specifically defined by the applicable state law.
prongs.burden_note: State law must specifically define the sexual conduct at issue — vague statutes are unconstitutional.

prongs.label: Lacks serious value (SLAPS test)
prongs.description: Whether the work, taken as a whole, lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value. This prong is judged by a national reasonable person standard, not community standards.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish lack of serious value. Pope v. Illinois (1987) clarified that a national reasonable person standard — not community standards — applies to this prong.

doctrinal_tests.label: O'Brien Expressive Conduct Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The four-part test for evaluating government regulation of expressive conduct established in United States v. O'Brien (1968). Applies when government regulates conduct that has an expressive component for reasons unrelated to its communicative impact. If the regulation targets the communicative aspect, strict scrutiny applies instead.

prongs.label: Within constitutional power
prongs.description: The regulation must be within the constitutional power of the government.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish constitutional authority for the regulation.

prongs.label: Substantial government interest
prongs.description: The regulation must further an important or substantial government interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must identify a substantial interest beyond suppression of expression.

prongs.label: Unrelated to suppression of expression
prongs.description: The government interest must be unrelated to the suppression of free expression. This is the critical prong — if the government's purpose is to suppress the message, the O'Brien test does not apply and strict scrutiny governs.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate its interest is in the non-communicative aspect of the conduct.
prongs.contest_note: Courts sometimes disagree about whether a regulation is truly unrelated to expression or whether it targets the communicative impact of the conduct. The O'Brien/strict scrutiny line depends on this determination.

prongs.label: No greater than essential
prongs.description: The incidental restriction on First Amendment freedoms must be no greater than is essential to the furtherance of the government interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the restriction is tailored to its non-expressive interest.

doctrinal_tests.label: Buckley Campaign Finance Framework
doctrinal_tests.description: The framework established in Buckley v. Valeo (1976) for evaluating campaign finance restrictions. Distinguishes between expenditure limits and contribution limits, applying different levels of scrutiny to each. Expenditure limits on political speech receive strict scrutiny; contribution limits receive a more deferential standard requiring a sufficiently important interest and closely drawn means.

prongs.label: Expenditure vs. contribution classification
prongs.description: Determine whether the restriction targets expenditures (spending to disseminate speech directly) or contributions (giving money to a candidate or committee). Expenditure limits receive strict scrutiny; contribution limits receive more deferential review.
prongs.burden_note: Classification determines applicable standard of review.
prongs.contest_note: The expenditure/contribution distinction has been contested since Buckley. Critics argue it is unprincipled — both involve spending money to influence elections. Citizens United declined to reconsider the distinction but extended expenditure protection to corporations.

prongs.label: Anti-corruption interest
prongs.description: For both expenditures and contributions, only the government interest in preventing quid pro quo corruption or its appearance is sufficient. Interests in equalizing political influence or preventing the appearance of undue influence are insufficient under Buckley.
prongs.burden_note: Government must identify evidence of actual or apparent quid pro quo corruption, not merely the appearance of influence.
prongs.contest_note: The scope of the anti-corruption interest is heavily contested. McConnell v. FEC (2003) took a broader view; Citizens United narrowed it back to quid pro quo corruption.

doctrinal_tests.label: Strict Scrutiny (Equal Protection)
doctrinal_tests.description: The most demanding standard of judicial review under the Equal Protection Clause. Applied to laws that classify on the basis of race, national origin, or alienage, or that burden the exercise of a fundamental right. A law subject to strict scrutiny is presumptively unconstitutional and will be upheld only if the government demonstrates that it is necessary to achieve a compelling governmental interest and is narrowly tailored to that end. Strict scrutiny is famously described as 'strict in theory and fatal in fact,' though in practice some laws survive.

prongs.label: Compelling governmental interest
prongs.description: The government must identify a compelling interest served by the classification. Accepted compelling interests include national security (debated), remedying specific documented past discrimination, and — until SFFA (2023) — student body diversity in higher education. Mere legitimate or important interests are insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of demonstrating compelling interest

prongs.label: Narrow tailoring
prongs.description: The classification must be narrowly tailored to achieve the compelling interest — it must be necessary, not merely helpful. Narrow tailoring requires consideration of race-neutral alternatives, avoidance of unnecessary burden on non-benefited groups, and (for affirmative action) temporal limitation. The government may not use racial quotas or mechanical point systems.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of demonstrating narrow tailoring
prongs.contest_note: The degree of fit required by narrow tailoring — whether it requires the least restrictive means or merely a close fit — has been contested across cases.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Strict scrutiny under equal protection was first articulated in Korematsu v. United States (1944), which applied the standard while upholding Japanese internment. In practice strict scrutiny is nearly always fatal — the major exceptions are Korematsu itself (subsequently repudiated in Trump v. Hawaii, 2018) and Grutter v. Bollinger (2003), which SFFA v. Harvard (2023) later overruled.

doctrinal_tests.label: Intermediate Scrutiny (Equal Protection)
doctrinal_tests.description: The middle tier of judicial review under the Equal Protection Clause. Applied to laws that classify on the basis of sex or illegitimacy. The government must demonstrate that the classification is substantially related to an important governmental interest. Intermediate scrutiny is more demanding than rational basis but less demanding than strict scrutiny. United States v. Virginia (1996) effectively heightened the standard for sex classifications by requiring an exceedingly persuasive justification.

prongs.label: Important governmental interest
prongs.description: The government must demonstrate that its classification serves an important governmental interest — more than merely legitimate but less than compelling. Accepted important interests include preventing drunk driving (Craig v. Boren), compensating for past economic discrimination (Califano v. Webster), and maintaining the effectiveness of military forces (Rostker v. Goldberg).
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of demonstrating important interest

prongs.label: Substantial relation / Exceedingly persuasive justification
prongs.description: The classification must be substantially related to achieving the important interest. After United States v. Virginia (1996), sex classifications also require an exceedingly persuasive justification — a standard that effectively approaches strict scrutiny. The justification must be genuine, not hypothesized post hoc.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden; post-VMI must show exceedingly persuasive justification for sex classifications
prongs.contest_note: Whether post-VMI intermediate scrutiny for sex is functionally equivalent to strict scrutiny is contested. The Court has not formally elevated sex to a suspect classification.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Craig v. Boren (1976) formally established intermediate scrutiny as the standard for sex-based classifications. The standard was developed to avoid the 4-justice plurality in Frontiero v. Richardson (1973) that would have elevated sex to a suspect classification requiring strict scrutiny.

doctrinal_tests.label: Rational Basis Test (Equal Protection)
doctrinal_tests.description: The default, most deferential standard of judicial review under the Equal Protection Clause. Applied to all laws that do not involve suspect or quasi-suspect classifications or fundamental rights. A law satisfies rational basis if it is rationally related to any legitimate governmental interest — even one not articulated by the legislature. Courts will hypothesize conceivable rational bases and sustain the law unless no rational basis is conceivable. The challenger bears the burden of negating every possible rational basis.

prongs.label: Legitimate governmental interest
prongs.description: Any legitimate governmental interest suffices — the government need not articulate it, and courts will hypothesize one. Virtually any plausible government purpose qualifies: economic regulation, administrative convenience, promotion of health or safety. Only bare desire to harm a politically unpopular group is illegitimate.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears burden of negating all conceivable legitimate interests

prongs.label: Rational relation
prongs.description: The law must be rationally related to the legitimate interest — not perfectly tailored, just not arbitrary. The fit may be loose, overinclusive, or underinclusive. Courts defer to legislative judgments about the relationship between means and ends. In rare cases of animus-based classifications, the Court applies rational basis with more scrutiny, asking whether any legitimate interest actually explains the classification.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears burden; highly deferential to legislature
prongs.contest_note: Rational basis with bite — applied in Moreno, Cleburne, and Romer — is more demanding than standard rational basis but has not been formally acknowledged as a distinct tier. Its doctrinal status remains contested.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Rational basis emerged from the post-Lochner settlement — the Court's abandonment of substantive economic due process review after 1937. Carolene Products (1938) established rational basis as the default standard while reserving heightened review for fundamental rights and discrete and insular minorities.

doctrinal_tests.label: Clear and Convincing Evidence Standard for Withdrawal of Life-Sustaining Treatment
doctrinal_tests.description: A procedural standard requiring that evidence of an incompetent person's wishes regarding withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment be proved by clear and convincing evidence, balancing the individual's liberty interest against state interests in preserving life and protecting against surrogate abuse.

prongs.label: Individual Liberty Interest
prongs.description: The competent person's constitutionally protected liberty interest in refusing unwanted medical treatment, including life-sustaining hydration and nutrition.
prongs.burden_note: The challenger seeking to withdraw treatment must prove the incompetent person's wishes by clear and convincing evidence.

prongs.label: State Interests
prongs.description: The state's interests include: (a) general interest in protection and preservation of human life; (b) safeguarding the personal element of an individual's choice between life and death; (c) guarding against potential abuses by surrogates; (d) ensuring accurate factfinding through adversarial process; and (e) declining to make judgments about quality of life while asserting an unqualified interest in preservation of human life.
prongs.burden_note: The state bears the burden of articulating and defending its interests; the clear and convincing evidence standard distributes the risk of erroneous decision on those seeking to terminate treatment.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies specifically to decisions regarding withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment for incompetent persons. The Court emphasized that an erroneous decision to withdraw treatment cannot be corrected, whereas an erroneous decision not to withdraw maintains the status quo and may be corrected by future events. The test does not require states to accept substituted judgment by family members absent substantial proof that their views reflect the patient's wishes.

doctrinal_tests.label: Meyer Substantive Due Process Rationality Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A state law that interferes with liberty protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause must have a reasonable relation to a legitimate purpose within the state's competency to effect. The legislature's determination of what constitutes proper exercise of police power is not final but is subject to judicial supervision. Arbitrary legislation lacking reasonable relation to a legitimate state purpose violates substantive due process.

prongs.label: Legitimate State Purpose
prongs.description: The state must identify a purpose within its competency to effect.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish a legitimate purpose.

prongs.label: Reasonable Relation
prongs.description: The legislative means must have a reasonable relation to the identified legitimate purpose. The means cannot be arbitrary or wholly unrelated to the purpose.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate reasonable relation between means and purpose.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Meyer applied this test to strike down Nebraska's foreign language teaching prohibition, finding that while the state had a legitimate interest in promoting civic unity and English language proficiency, the means (complete prohibition of modern language instruction before eighth grade) was arbitrary and lacked reasonable relation to that purpose, particularly in peacetime. The Court emphasized that desirable ends cannot be promoted by prohibited means.

doctrinal_tests.label: Lochner Police Power Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether a state regulation is a valid exercise of police power or an unconstitutional interference with liberty of contract. The regulation must have a fair, reasonable, and appropriate relation to a legitimate public purpose (safety, health, morals, or welfare), and the connection between the means and the end must be direct and substantial, not remote or shadowy.

prongs.label: Legitimate Public Purpose
prongs.description: The regulation must relate to a legitimate state interest in safety, health, morals, or general welfare of the public.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the regulation addresses a legitimate public purpose.

prongs.label: Fair, Reasonable, and Appropriate Means
prongs.description: The regulation must be a fair, reasonable, and appropriate exercise of police power in relation to the stated purpose. The connection between the regulation and the public purpose must be direct and substantial, not remote, shadowy, or arbitrary.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the regulation is reasonably related to the legitimate purpose and not arbitrary or meddlesome.

prongs.label: No Arbitrary Interference with Liberty of Contract
prongs.description: The regulation must not constitute an arbitrary or unreasonable interference with the liberty of contract protected by the Fourteenth Amendment. The regulation cannot be justified merely by assertion that it relates to public health; there must be a material and substantial danger to public health or employee health.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the regulation is not arbitrary and has a material connection to the stated public purpose.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test requires courts to examine whether a regulation is truly a health law or merely a pretext for regulating labor. The Court must look beyond the proclaimed purpose to the natural and legal effect of the statute. The test applies heightened scrutiny to regulations that interfere with liberty of contract, requiring more than a remote or speculative connection to public health.

doctrinal_tests.label: Palko Ordered Liberty Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether a right enumerated in the Bill of Rights is so fundamental to ordered liberty and due process that it applies to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment. The test asks whether the right is rooted in the traditions and conscience of the people and whether it is essential to a scheme of ordered liberty such that its denial would violate fundamental principles of liberty and justice.

prongs.label: Rooted in Traditions and Conscience
prongs.description: The right must be rooted in the traditions and conscience of the American people and recognized as fundamental throughout the nation's history.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate that the right is deeply embedded in American legal and political traditions.

prongs.label: Essential to Ordered Liberty
prongs.description: The right must be of the very essence of a scheme of ordered liberty, such that neither liberty nor justice would exist if it were sacrificed. It must be a principle of justice so fundamental that a fair and enlightened system of justice would be impossible without it.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show that the right is indispensable to the concept of ordered liberty and due process.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test represents the Court's selective incorporation framework. Rights that fail the test (such as double jeopardy protection, grand jury indictment, jury trial, and immunity from self-incrimination) are not incorporated. Rights that pass (such as freedom of speech, press, religion, assembly, and right to counsel in capital cases) are incorporated and bind the states. The test emphasizes that the mere presence of a right in the Bill of Rights does not automatically make it applicable to the states.

doctrinal_tests.label: Parental Fundamental Right Protection Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for evaluating whether state interference with parental decisions concerning child care, custody, and control violates the Due Process Clause. The test examines whether the state has accorded appropriate deference to a fit parent's determination, whether there is a presumption favoring parental judgment, and whether the state action is narrowly tailored to address legitimate state interests.

prongs.label: Fit Parent Status
prongs.description: Whether the parent is fit. A presumption exists that fit parents act in their children's best interests.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish unfitness or harm/potential harm to justify interference.

prongs.label: Deference to Parental Judgment
prongs.description: Whether the state accords appropriate deference and weight to the fit parent's determination of the child's best interests.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show it gave proper deference to parental judgment; failure to do so violates the fundamental right.

prongs.label: Narrowness of State Action
prongs.description: Whether the state action is narrowly tailored and does not sweep too broadly in permitting interference with parental decisions.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that the statute or application is not overbroad and is limited to legitimate protective purposes.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies heightened scrutiny to state interference with parental decisions. The Court emphasized that absent a showing of harm or potential harm to the child, or absent a finding that the parent is unfit, the state should not inject itself into the private realm of the family. The test does not require a formal harm showing as a threshold matter in all cases, but the plurality declined to decide that precise question. The plurality explicitly declined to identify the precise standard of review, stating only that the statute failed as applied. The test reflects balancing rather than a formal scrutiny tier.

doctrinal_tests.label: Escobedo Pretrial Right to Counsel Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A multi-factor test determining whether the right to counsel attaches at the pretrial interrogation stage, requiring consideration of whether the investigation has focused on a particular suspect, the suspect is in custody, interrogation is being conducted, the suspect has requested counsel, and the suspect has not been warned of the right to remain silent.

prongs.label: Investigation Focused on Particular Suspect
prongs.description: The investigation must have ceased to be a general inquiry into an unsolved crime and must have begun to focus on a particular suspect.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the investigation remains general; if focused on suspect, right to counsel attaches.

prongs.label: Suspect in Police Custody
prongs.description: The suspect must have been taken into police custody or otherwise restrained of liberty.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show suspect was not in custody to avoid counsel requirement.

prongs.label: Interrogation Process Conducted
prongs.description: The police must be carrying out a process of interrogations that lends itself to eliciting incriminating statements.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show no interrogation occurred to avoid counsel requirement.

prongs.label: Suspect Requested and Was Denied Counsel
prongs.description: The suspect must have requested an opportunity to consult with his lawyer and been denied that opportunity.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show suspect did not request counsel or was granted access to counsel.

prongs.label: No Effective Warning of Right to Remain Silent
prongs.description: The police must not have effectively warned the suspect of his absolute constitutional right to remain silent.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show suspect was warned of right to remain silent to avoid exclusion of statement.

doctrinal_tests.notes: When all five prongs are satisfied, the accused has been denied the Assistance of Counsel in violation of the Sixth Amendment, and any statement elicited during interrogation is inadmissible. The test applies a functional rather than formal approach, rejecting reliance on whether formal indictment has occurred.

doctrinal_tests.label: Miranda Warnings Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test requiring law enforcement to provide specific warnings to suspects in custody before interrogation, and to obtain a valid waiver of rights before questioning.

prongs.label: Custody Requirement
prongs.description: The suspect must be in custody or otherwise deprived of freedom of action in any significant way.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish that the suspect was in custody.

prongs.label: Interrogation Requirement
prongs.description: There must be interrogation or its functional equivalent (express questioning or words or actions likely to elicit an incriminating response).
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish that interrogation occurred.

prongs.label: Warning of Right Against Self-Incrimination
prongs.description: The suspect must be warned that they have the right to remain silent and that anything they say can be used against them in court.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide this warning before custodial interrogation.

prongs.label: Warning of Right to Counsel
prongs.description: The suspect must be warned that they have the right to an attorney and that one will be provided if they cannot afford one.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide this warning before custodial interrogation.

prongs.label: Valid Waiver
prongs.description: The suspect must knowingly, intelligently, and voluntarily waive their rights before any interrogation proceeds.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish a valid waiver of rights.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test establishes the procedural requirements for lawful custodial interrogation. Failure to comply results in suppression of any statements obtained.

doctrinal_tests.label: Terry Stop and Frisk Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-part test for evaluating the constitutionality of a stop and frisk: (1) the officer must have reasonable suspicion based on articulable facts that criminal activity is afoot; and (2) the frisk must be limited to a pat-down of outer clothing for weapons, conducted for officer safety.

prongs.label: Reasonable Suspicion
prongs.description: The officer must have reasonable suspicion based on articulable facts that criminal activity is afoot or that the person may be armed and dangerous.
prongs.burden_note: Government must articulate specific facts and rational inferences supporting the suspicion.

prongs.label: Limited Frisk for Weapons
prongs.description: The frisk must be limited to a pat-down of the outer clothing to determine whether the person is armed, and may not extend to a full search for evidence of crime unless a weapon is felt.
prongs.burden_note: Officer must limit the search to outer garments and to the purpose of detecting weapons.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test applies when an officer conducts an investigatory stop without probable cause for arrest. The frisk is justified by officer safety concerns and must be strictly limited in scope.

doctrinal_tests.label: Totality of the Circumstances Test for Probable Cause
doctrinal_tests.description: A flexible standard for determining whether an informant's tip, combined with corroborating facts, establishes probable cause for a search warrant. The issuing magistrate must make a practical, common-sense decision whether there is a fair probability that contraband or evidence of a crime will be found in a particular place, considering all circumstances set forth in the affidavit.

prongs.label: Fair Probability Standard
prongs.description: The magistrate must determine whether there is a fair probability that contraband or evidence of a crime will be found in the particular place to be searched.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish fair probability based on totality of circumstances.

prongs.label: Substantial Basis for Magistrate's Conclusion
prongs.description: A reviewing court must ensure that the magistrate had a substantial basis for concluding that probable cause existed.
prongs.burden_note: Reviewing court must verify the magistrate's reasoning was grounded in the facts presented.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test replaces the rigid two-pronged Aguilar-Spinelli test. Corroboration of informant details by independent police work is of significant value under this standard. The elements of veracity, reliability, and basis of knowledge are understood as closely intertwined issues that inform the common-sense probable cause determination.

doctrinal_tests.label: Strickland Ineffective Assistance of Counsel Test
doctrinal_tests.description: Two-part test for evaluating claims of ineffective assistance of counsel under the Sixth Amendment. Defendant must show deficient performance and prejudice resulting from that deficiency.

prongs.label: Deficient Performance
prongs.description: Counsel's performance was deficient. The proper standard is reasonably effective assistance, considering all circumstances. Counsel's representation must be evaluated from counsel's perspective at the time, with strong presumption that conduct falls within the wide range of reasonable professional assistance. Judicial scrutiny must be highly deferential and eliminate hindsight bias.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must show counsel's performance fell below an objective standard of reasonableness.

prongs.label: Prejudice
prongs.description: The deficient performance prejudiced the defense. Defendant must show there is a reasonable probability that, but for counsel's unprofessional errors, the result of the proceeding would have been different. A reasonable probability is one sufficient to undermine confidence in the outcome. Court must consider the totality of evidence before the judge or jury.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must demonstrate reasonable probability of different outcome absent counsel's errors.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Court need not determine deficient performance before examining prejudice; if easier to dispose on lack of prejudice, that course should be followed. Applies in federal collateral proceedings, direct appeal, motions for new trial, and federal habeas challenges to state convictions.

doctrinal_tests.label: Good-Faith Reliance on Warrant Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test determining whether evidence obtained pursuant to a search warrant is admissible despite the warrant's invalidity, based on whether the officer's reliance on the warrant was objectively reasonable.

prongs.label: Detached and Neutral Magistrate
prongs.description: The warrant must have been issued by a detached and neutral magistrate, not one who merely serves as a rubber stamp for police.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the magistrate performed a neutral and detached function.

prongs.label: Objectively Reasonable Reliance
prongs.description: The officer's reliance on the magistrate's probable-cause determination and on the technical sufficiency of the warrant must be objectively reasonable.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the officer could not reasonably question the warrant's validity.

prongs.label: No Knowing or Reckless Falsity
prongs.description: The affidavit supporting the warrant must not contain information that the affiant knew was false or would have known was false except for reckless disregard of the truth.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show knowing or reckless falsity in the affidavit.

prongs.label: Substantial Basis for Probable Cause
prongs.description: The affidavit must provide the magistrate with a substantial basis for determining the existence of probable cause; the warrant cannot be based on an affidavit so lacking in indicia of probable cause as to render official belief in its existence entirely unreasonable.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the affidavit provided a substantial basis for probable cause.

prongs.label: Facial Validity of Warrant
prongs.description: The warrant must not be so facially deficient—such as in failing to particularize the place to be searched or the things to be seized—that executing officers cannot reasonably presume it to be valid.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the warrant was not facially deficient.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test applies when evidence is obtained pursuant to a search warrant that is ultimately found to be invalid. Suppression remains an appropriate remedy if the magistrate was misled by knowing or reckless falsity, wholly abandoned the detached and neutral role, or if the warrant is so facially deficient that reasonable reliance is impossible.

doctrinal_tests.label: Carroll Automobile Search Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A warrantless search of an automobile is valid if: (1) the seizing officer has probable cause to believe the vehicle contains contraband or goods subject to forfeiture; and (2) the search is conducted in circumstances where securing a warrant is impracticable due to the vehicle's mobility. The test balances the government's need to seize contraband in transit against the individual's Fourth Amendment protections.

prongs.label: Probable Cause
prongs.description: The seizing officer must have probable cause—facts and circumstances sufficient to warrant a man of prudence and caution in believing—that the automobile contains contraband or goods subject to forfeiture and destruction.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish probable cause based on facts known to the officer or reasonably trustworthy information received.

prongs.label: Impracticability of Warrant
prongs.description: The search must occur in circumstances where it is not practicable to secure a warrant because the vehicle can be quickly moved out of the locality or jurisdiction in which the warrant must be sought, distinguishing automobiles from fixed structures like dwellings.
prongs.burden_note: The government need not show it attempted to obtain a warrant; the inherent mobility of vehicles satisfies this prong.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Established in Carroll v. United States. The test reflects the Court's holding that the Fourth Amendment recognizes a necessary distinction between searches of dwellings (requiring warrant) and searches of vehicles in transportation (permissible without warrant upon probable cause). The Court emphasized that the seizure of contraband in transit is the primary purpose, with arrest of the transporter being incidental.

doctrinal_tests.label: Sense-Enhancing Technology Search Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A search occurs under the Fourth Amendment when: (1) the Government uses a device not in general public use; (2) to explore details of a private home; (3) that would previously have been unknowable without physical intrusion into a constitutionally protected area. Such a search is presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.

prongs.label: Device Not in General Public Use
prongs.description: The technology or device used must not be in general public use.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish that the device is in general public use to avoid triggering search analysis.

prongs.label: Exploration of Home Details
prongs.description: The device must be used to explore details of a private home.
prongs.burden_note: Applies specifically to the home, which receives heightened Fourth Amendment protection.

prongs.label: Information Previously Unknowable Without Physical Intrusion
prongs.description: The information obtained could not previously have been obtained without physical intrusion into a constitutionally protected area.
prongs.burden_note: Government cannot justify warrantless use by arguing the information came from the home's external surface or did not reveal 'intimate details.'

doctrinal_tests.notes: When all three prongs are satisfied, the surveillance is a Fourth Amendment search and is presumptively unreasonable without a warrant. The test rejects mechanical interpretations based on whether information originates from external surfaces or whether it reveals 'intimate details.' In the sanctity of the home, all details are treated as intimate.

doctrinal_tests.label: Cell Phone Search Warrant Requirement Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test determining whether a warrantless search of digital information on a cell phone seized incident to arrest is permissible. The test requires that police generally obtain a warrant before searching digital information on a cell phone, with limited exceptions for exigent circumstances and physical examination to ensure the phone will not be used as a weapon.

prongs.label: Warrant Requirement
prongs.description: Police must generally obtain a warrant before searching digital information on a cell phone seized from an arrestee.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify warrantless search through a recognized exception.

prongs.label: Chimel Interests Not Furthered
prongs.description: Digital data on a cell phone does not present the officer safety or evidence destruction risks identified in Chimel, because data cannot be used as a weapon and concerns about remote wiping are better addressed through case-specific exigent circumstances analysis.
prongs.burden_note: Government cannot rely on Chimel rationales to justify warrantless cell phone searches.

prongs.label: Substantial Privacy Interests
prongs.description: Cell phones implicate substantially greater individual privacy interests than physical searches because they store vast amounts of personal information in one place, including data dating back years, and may contain information stored on remote servers.
prongs.burden_note: Government must overcome heightened privacy interests to justify warrantless search.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies the balancing approach articulated in Wyoming v. Houghton, weighing privacy intrusion against government interests. The test does not preclude warrantless searches in exigent circumstances or physical examination of the phone itself to ensure officer safety. The Court rejected proposed fallback rules that would have allowed searches based on relevance to the crime of arrest, officer safety, or analogy to pre-digital counterparts.

doctrinal_tests.label: Independent Source Test for Lineup Identifications
doctrinal_tests.description: When a post-indictment lineup is conducted in violation of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel, courtroom identifications may be admitted if the government demonstrates that the identifications were based on sources independent of the tainted lineup.

prongs.label: Taint from Lineup Violation
prongs.description: The government must first establish that a Sixth Amendment violation occurred at the lineup.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove the violation occurred.

prongs.label: Independent Source
prongs.description: The government must demonstrate that the courtroom identification was based on sources independent of the tainted lineup, such as the witness's own observations at the crime scene.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of proving independence.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test allows for admission of courtroom identifications despite a Sixth Amendment violation at the lineup if the government can establish an independent source for the identification.

doctrinal_tests.label: Gant Vehicle Search Incident to Arrest Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A warrantless search of a vehicle incident to arrest is permissible under two circumstances: (1) when the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of the passenger compartment, or (2) when it is reasonable to believe that evidence of the offense of arrest might be found in the vehicle.

prongs.label: Arrestee Access Prong
prongs.description: The arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of the passenger compartment, allowing access to weapons or destructible evidence.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show arrestee could access the interior.

prongs.label: Evidence of Offense Prong
prongs.description: It is reasonable to believe that evidence of the offense of arrest might be found in the vehicle.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show reasonable belief that evidence of the specific offense of arrest is in the vehicle.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Replaces the categorical approach of New York v. Belton with a fact-specific inquiry. Consistent with Thornton v. United States (2004).

doctrinal_tests.label: Buie Protective Sweep Test
doctrinal_tests.description: Standard for determining whether a warrantless protective sweep incident to arrest is justified under the Fourth Amendment. The searching officer must possess a reasonable belief based on specific and articulable facts which, taken together with rational inferences from those facts, reasonably warrant the officer in believing that the area swept harbored an individual posing a danger to the officer or others.

prongs.label: Reasonable Belief
prongs.description: The searching officer must possess a reasonable belief that the area swept harbored an individual posing a danger to the officer or others.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish reasonable belief based on specific and articulable facts.

prongs.label: Specific and Articulable Facts
prongs.description: The reasonable belief must be based on specific and articulable facts which, taken together with rational inferences from those facts, reasonably warrant the officer in believing danger exists.
prongs.burden_note: Government must articulate specific facts supporting the reasonable belief.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Applies to warrantless protective sweeps conducted incident to arrest in a home. The sweep must be narrowly confined to a cursory visual inspection of places where a person might be hiding.

doctrinal_tests.label: Blakely Sentencing Fact-Finding Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test determining whether judicial fact-finding at sentencing violates the Sixth Amendment jury trial right. Any fact (other than prior conviction) that increases the sentence beyond the statutory maximum must be admitted by the defendant or found by a jury beyond a reasonable doubt.

prongs.label: Statutory Maximum Threshold
prongs.description: Identify whether the sentence imposed exceeds the statutory maximum for the offense of conviction based on facts admitted in the plea or verdict alone.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish that the sentence exceeds the statutory maximum based only on admitted facts.

prongs.label: Fact Admission or Jury Finding
prongs.description: If the sentence exceeds the statutory maximum, any fact used to justify the increase must either be admitted by the defendant or found by a jury beyond a reasonable doubt.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove the aggravating fact by jury verdict or defendant admission.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to any sentencing enhancement that increases punishment beyond the statutory maximum. Prior convictions are excepted from this requirement.

doctrinal_tests.label: Batson Peremptory Challenge Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A three-step test to determine whether a peremptory challenge was exercised on the basis of race in violation of the Equal Protection Clause.

prongs.label: Prima Facie Case
prongs.description: The party challenging the peremptory strike must make a prima facie showing that the opposing party exercised the challenge on the basis of race.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears burden of establishing facts that raise an inference of discriminatory purpose.

prongs.label: Explanation by Opposing Party
prongs.description: If a prima facie case is established, the opposing party must provide a race-neutral explanation for the peremptory strike.
prongs.burden_note: Government or opposing party must articulate a legitimate, race-neutral reason for the challenge.

prongs.label: Credibility Determination
prongs.description: The trial court must determine whether the proffered explanation is credible and whether the challenger has proven purposeful discrimination by a preponderance of the evidence.
prongs.burden_note: Trial court evaluates the credibility of the explanation and whether discrimination has been proven.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies strict scrutiny to peremptory challenges to ensure they are not exercised on the basis of race.

doctrinal_tests.label: Manson Eyewitness Identification Reliability Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A totality of circumstances test for determining whether an eyewitness identification obtained under suggestive conditions is sufficiently reliable to be admitted into evidence.

prongs.label: Opportunity to View
prongs.description: How long the witness observed the suspect during the crime.
prongs.burden_note: Government shows adequate opportunity to view.

prongs.label: Degree of Attention
prongs.description: Whether the witness was paying attention to the perpetrator.
prongs.burden_note: Government shows witness was attentive.

prongs.label: Accuracy of Prior Description
prongs.description: Whether the witness's prior description of the perpetrator matches the defendant.
prongs.burden_note: Government shows description was accurate.

prongs.label: Level of Certainty
prongs.description: The witness's level of certainty at the time of confrontation.
prongs.burden_note: Government shows witness was certain.

prongs.label: Time Between Crime and Confrontation
prongs.description: The length of time between the crime and the identification procedure.
prongs.burden_note: Government shows short interval between crime and identification.

doctrinal_tests.notes: These five factors are weighed against the corrupting effect of the suggestive identification procedure.

doctrinal_tests.label: Barker Speedy Trial Balancing Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A four-factor balancing test for evaluating whether a defendant's Sixth Amendment right to a speedy trial has been violated, requiring courts to weigh the length of delay, the reason for delay, the defendant's assertion of the right, and prejudice to the defendant.

prongs.label: Length of Delay
prongs.description: The court must consider the length of the delay between arrest/indictment and trial.
prongs.burden_note: Longer delays weigh against the government.

prongs.label: Reason for Delay
prongs.description: The court must examine the reasons for the delay, including whether the government acted diligently.
prongs.burden_note: Delays caused by government negligence or deliberate tactics weigh heavily against the government; delays caused by the defendant or unavoidable circumstances weigh less heavily.

prongs.label: Defendant's Assertion of the Right
prongs.description: The court must consider whether and when the defendant asserted the speedy trial right.
prongs.burden_note: Failure to assert the right is a significant factor, though not dispositive.

prongs.label: Prejudice to the Defendant
prongs.description: The court must assess the prejudice to the defendant resulting from the delay, including oppressive pretrial incarceration, anxiety and concern of the accused, and the possibility that the defense will be impaired.
prongs.burden_note: Actual prejudice strengthens the defendant's claim, though the absence of demonstrable prejudice does not necessarily defeat it.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test replaced the absence of a clear standard that had existed after Klopfer v. North Carolina (1967) established the speedy trial right as fundamental. The test applies a balancing approach rather than a bright-line rule.

doctrinal_tests.label: Abuse-of-Discretion Standard for Sentencing Review
doctrinal_tests.description: Courts of appeals must review all sentences under a deferential abuse-of-discretion standard. The extent of difference between a particular sentence and the recommended Guidelines range is relevant but does not require extraordinary circumstances to justify a substantial variance.

prongs.label: Deferential Review Standard
prongs.description: The court of appeals must apply a deferential abuse-of-discretion standard to all sentences, regardless of whether they fall within, just outside, or significantly outside the Guidelines range.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger bears burden of showing abuse of discretion

prongs.label: Relevance of Guidelines Variance
prongs.description: The extent of difference between a particular sentence and the recommended Guidelines range is relevant to the reasonableness inquiry but does not trigger a proportionality requirement or demand extraordinary circumstances.
prongs.burden_note: District judge's discretionary decision is reviewed for reasonableness

doctrinal_tests.notes: Rejects the proportionality test that would require extraordinary circumstances to justify substantial variances from Guidelines. Applies to sentences both within and outside the Guidelines range.

doctrinal_tests.label: Plain Feel Test for Patdown Searches
doctrinal_tests.description: A test determining whether contraband detected during a protective patdown search may be seized under the Fourth Amendment, requiring that the officer's tactile examination remain within the scope of determining whether a weapon is present.

prongs.label: Lawful Patdown Search
prongs.description: The initial patdown search must be lawful and justified by the officer's need to determine whether the suspect is armed.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish lawful basis for patdown

prongs.label: Incriminating Character Immediately Apparent
prongs.description: The incriminating character of the object must be immediately apparent to the officer's sense of touch, without further manipulation beyond what is necessary to determine if it is a weapon.
prongs.burden_note: Officer's tactile examination must not exceed scope of weapon detection

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test prohibits seizure of contraband when the officer's manipulation of the object goes beyond what is necessary to determine whether it is a weapon, even if the contraband's incriminating character would have been immediately apparent had the officer confined the search to its lawful scope.

doctrinal_tests.label: Mathews Balancing Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A three-part balancing test for determining what process is due before government deprives a person of a protected interest. Courts must weigh: (1) the private interest affected; (2) the risk of erroneous deprivation under current procedures and the probable value of additional safeguards; and (3) the government's interest, including fiscal and administrative burdens.

prongs.label: Private Interest Affected
prongs.description: The nature and magnitude of the private interest that will be affected by the government action.
prongs.burden_note: Claimant must identify the protected interest at stake.

prongs.label: Risk of Erroneous Deprivation
prongs.description: The risk of erroneous deprivation of the private interest through the procedures currently employed, and the probable value of additional or substitute procedural safeguards.
prongs.burden_note: Shared burden to assess procedural adequacy and likelihood of error.

prongs.label: Government Interest
prongs.description: The government's interest, including the function involved and fiscal and administrative burdens that additional or substitute procedural requirements would entail.
prongs.burden_note: Government must articulate its interests, but courts weigh them against private interests.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to all procedural due process claims and requires flexible, context-specific analysis rather than categorical rules.

doctrinal_tests.label: Loudermill Pretermination Due Process Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A public employee with a property interest in continued employment must receive notice of charges and an opportunity to respond before termination, followed by a full post-termination hearing.

prongs.label: Pretermination Notice
prongs.description: The employee must be given notice of the charges against them before termination.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide notice

prongs.label: Opportunity to Respond
prongs.description: The employee must be afforded an opportunity to respond to the charges before removal takes effect.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide opportunity to respond

prongs.label: Post-Termination Hearing
prongs.description: A full post-termination hearing must be provided to determine whether the employee was properly discharged for cause.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide full hearing after termination

doctrinal_tests.notes: The pretermination process need not be elaborate; a brief opportunity to explain one's side of the story is sufficient. The full hearing can occur after termination.

doctrinal_tests.label: Enemy Combatant Detention Due Process Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test requiring that a citizen held in the United States as an enemy combatant receive a meaningful opportunity to contest the factual basis for that detention before a neutral decisionmaker, subject to the government's authorization of such detention in narrow circumstances.

prongs.label: Congressional Authorization
prongs.description: Congress must have authorized the detention of combatants in the narrow circumstances alleged.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate congressional authorization

prongs.label: Meaningful Opportunity to Contest
prongs.description: The detainee must be given a meaningful opportunity to contest the factual basis for the detention.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide this procedural opportunity

prongs.label: Neutral Decisionmaker
prongs.description: The opportunity to contest detention must be before a neutral decisionmaker.
prongs.burden_note: Government must ensure neutrality of the decisionmaker

doctrinal_tests.notes: Established by the plurality opinion in Hamdi v. Rumsfeld. The test applies specifically to United States citizens detained as enemy combatants on United States soil.

doctrinal_tests.label: Spending Power Condition Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether Congress may constitutionally condition the receipt of federal funds on a state's adoption of specified policies. The test examines whether the condition is reasonably related to the federal interest in the funds and whether the condition is not prohibited by another constitutional provision.

prongs.label: Legitimate Federal Interest
prongs.description: Congress must be pursuing a legitimate federal interest in conditioning the receipt of federal funds.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate that the federal interest is not legitimate.

prongs.label: Reasonable Relationship to Federal Interest
prongs.description: The condition imposed on the receipt of federal funds must be reasonably related to the federal interest.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate that the condition is not reasonably related to the federal interest.

prongs.label: No Constitutional Prohibition
prongs.description: The condition must not be prohibited by another constitutional provision.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate that the condition violates another constitutional provision.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Established in South Dakota v. Dole as the framework for analyzing conditional federal spending. The test applies rational basis scrutiny to the relationship between the condition and the federal interest.

doctrinal_tests.label: Herring Exclusionary Rule Application Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether evidence obtained in violation of the Fourth Amendment must be excluded, based on the culpability of the police and the potential of exclusion to deter wrongful police conduct.

prongs.label: Police Culpability
prongs.description: Assess the degree of culpability of the police in committing the Fourth Amendment violation, including whether the error was isolated negligence or more systemic misconduct.
prongs.burden_note: The court evaluates police conduct; isolated negligence weighs against exclusion.

prongs.label: Deterrent Effect
prongs.description: Evaluate whether excluding the evidence would have a meaningful deterrent effect on future police wrongdoing.
prongs.burden_note: The court assesses whether exclusion would deter the type of police conduct at issue.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies when police conduct a search based on a reasonable but mistaken belief caused by negligent recordkeeping errors.

doctrinal_tests.label: Brown Attenuation Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A three-factor test for determining whether the connection between unconstitutional police conduct and discovered evidence is sufficiently attenuated to permit admission of the evidence despite the Fourth Amendment violation.

prongs.label: Temporal Proximity
prongs.description: The time interval between the unconstitutional conduct and the discovery of evidence. Substantial time between the unlawful act and evidence discovery favors attenuation; short intervals favor suppression.
prongs.burden_note: Favors suppression when discovery occurs within minutes of the violation.

prongs.label: Presence of Intervening Circumstances
prongs.description: Whether an intervening circumstance has interrupted the causal chain between the unconstitutional conduct and evidence discovery. A valid, pre-existing, and untainted arrest warrant constitutes a strong intervening circumstance.
prongs.burden_note: Favors the government when a valid warrant predates the investigation and is entirely unconnected with the unlawful stop.

prongs.label: Purpose and Flagrancy of Official Misconduct
prongs.description: The nature and severity of the police misconduct. Negligent or good-faith errors favor attenuation; purposeful or flagrant violations favor suppression. The exclusionary rule exists to deter police misconduct, so this factor is particularly significant.
prongs.burden_note: Favors the government when misconduct is negligent rather than purposeful or flagrant, and when there is no evidence of systemic or recurrent misconduct.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Articulated in Brown v. Illinois, 422 U.S. 590 (1975). Applied in Utah v. Strieff to determine that discovery of a valid arrest warrant attenuated the connection between an unconstitutional investigatory stop and evidence seized incident to arrest. The test applies to intervening circumstances beyond defendant's independent acts.

doctrinal_tests.label: Penn Central Takings Balancing Test
doctrinal_tests.description: An ad hoc balancing test for determining whether a government regulation constitutes a taking requiring just compensation. Courts weigh three factors: the economic impact on the claimant, interference with investment-backed expectations, and the character of the government action.

prongs.label: Economic Impact
prongs.description: The economic impact of the regulation on the claimant.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show significant economic harm.

prongs.label: Interference with Investment-Backed Expectations
prongs.description: The extent to which the regulation interferes with distinct investment-backed expectations.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show reasonable investment-backed expectations were frustrated.
