prongs.label: Character of Government Action
prongs.description: The character of the government action, including whether it is a physical invasion or a restriction on land use.
prongs.burden_note: Courts weigh whether the government is physically occupying or regulating.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The dominant framework for regulatory takings analysis.

doctrinal_tests.label: Lucas Total Economic Deprivation Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-part test for determining whether a regulation effects a taking: (1) whether the regulation deprives the owner of all economically beneficial use of the land; and (2) if so, whether the state can defend by showing the proscribed use interests were not part of the owner's title to begin with.

prongs.label: Total Economic Deprivation
prongs.description: Whether the regulation deprives the property owner of all economically beneficial use of the land.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show complete loss of economic value.

prongs.label: Title Defense
prongs.description: Whether the state can defend by showing the proscribed use interests were not part of the owner's title to begin with.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the use was never part of the owner's property rights.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies when a regulation eliminates all economically beneficial use. If the first prong is satisfied, the government must satisfy the second prong or just compensation is required.

doctrinal_tests.label: Youngstown Jackson Three-Category Framework
doctrinal_tests.description: Justice Jackson's concurring opinion established the dominant framework for analyzing presidential power. Presidential authority falls into one of three categories based on the relationship between executive action and congressional intent.

prongs.label: Category 1 — Congressional Authorization
prongs.description: When the President acts pursuant to express or implied congressional authorization, executive power is at its maximum, combining the President's own powers plus all that Congress can delegate.
prongs.burden_note: Government shows Congress authorized the action.

prongs.label: Category 2 — Congressional Silence (Zone of Twilight)
prongs.description: When the President acts in absence of either congressional grant or denial, the President can rely only on independent powers. Congressional inertia or indifference may sometimes enable presidential action.
prongs.burden_note: Courts examine whether Congress has impliedly authorized or prohibited the action.

prongs.label: Category 3 — Congressional Prohibition
prongs.description: When the President takes measures incompatible with the expressed or implied will of Congress, presidential power is at its lowest ebb. The President must rely solely on constitutional grants not subject to congressional control.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show exclusive constitutional authority.

doctrinal_tests.notes: From Justice Jackson's concurrence, not the Black majority. The Jackson framework is the governing analytical tool courts actually apply.

doctrinal_tests.label: Pike Balancing Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A balancing test for evaluating state laws that burden interstate commerce. The test requires courts to weigh the burden imposed on interstate commerce against the putative local benefits of the state law.

prongs.label: Burden on Interstate Commerce
prongs.description: Identify whether the state law imposes a substantial burden on interstate commerce.
prongs.burden_note: The challenger must demonstrate a substantial burden on interstate commerce.

prongs.label: Balancing Local Benefits Against Burden
prongs.description: Balance the burden on interstate commerce against the putative local benefits of the state law. The law is unconstitutional if the burden is clearly excessive in relation to the local benefits.
prongs.burden_note: The state must justify the burden by demonstrating that local benefits are not clearly outweighed by the burden on interstate commerce.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to state laws that have a legitimate local purpose but incidentally burden interstate commerce. It is distinct from per se rules that invalidate discriminatory state laws.

doctrinal_tests.label: Arlington Heights Discriminatory Purpose Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A multi-factor test for determining whether a governmental decision was motivated by discriminatory purpose in equal protection challenges. Courts examine the historical background of the decision, the specific sequence of events leading to the challenged decision, departures from normal procedures, and substantive departures from the normal course of business, along with the impact of the decision.

prongs.label: Historical Background
prongs.description: Examination of the historical background of the decision, including the sequence of events leading to the challenged action.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must present evidence of historical context suggesting discriminatory purpose.

prongs.label: Departures from Normal Procedures
prongs.description: Examination of whether the decision involved departures from the normal course of business or established procedures.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show that the decision deviated from standard procedures in a way suggesting discriminatory motive.

prongs.label: Impact of the Decision
prongs.description: Consideration of the impact or effect of the decision, though discriminatory effect alone is insufficient without evidence of discriminatory purpose.
prongs.burden_note: Discriminatory impact may be probative of discriminatory purpose but does not alone establish a violation.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test establishes the framework for proving discriminatory purpose in equal protection cases. The Court emphasized that while discriminatory effect may be probative of discriminatory purpose, it is not sufficient by itself to establish an equal protection violation.

doctrinal_tests.label: Intermediate Scrutiny for Gender-Based Classifications
doctrinal_tests.description: A gender-based statutory classification is constitutional only if it serves important governmental objectives and the discriminatory means employed are substantially related to achievement of those objectives.

prongs.label: Important Governmental Objective
prongs.description: The gender-based classification must serve an important governmental objective.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the objective is important.

prongs.label: Substantial Relationship to Objective
prongs.description: The discriminatory means employed must be substantially related to achievement of the important governmental objective.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate substantial relationship between classification and objective.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to all gender-based statutory classifications under equal protection review. It represents a middle tier of scrutiny between rational basis and strict scrutiny.

doctrinal_tests.label: Congruence and Proportionality Test
doctrinal_tests.description: Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity under the Fourteenth Amendment if the legislation is congruent and proportional to the constitutional violation it seeks to remedy. The test requires: (1) sufficient evidence of constitutional violations by the states; (2) remedies that are reasonable prophylactic measures; and (3) measures reasonably targeted to a legitimate end.

prongs.label: Sufficient Evidence of Constitutional Violations
prongs.description: Congress must demonstrate sufficient evidence of constitutional violations by the states that the legislation seeks to remedy.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish adequate factual predicate

prongs.label: Reasonable Prophylactic Measure
prongs.description: The remedies required must constitute reasonable prophylactic measures, not imposing unreasonable burdens on the states.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show remedies are reasonable

prongs.label: Reasonably Targeted to Legitimate End
prongs.description: The measures must be reasonably targeted to a legitimate constitutional end.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate rational connection between means and ends

doctrinal_tests.notes: Applied in Tennessee v. Lane to Title II of the ADA. Distinguished from Alabama v. Garrett by focusing on Due Process violations rather than equal protection violations.

doctrinal_tests.label: Parental Fundamental Right Interference Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test to determine whether state interference with a parent's fundamental right to direct the care, custody, and control of children violates the Due Process Clause. The test examines whether the state has accorded appropriate deference to a fit parent's decisions, whether the parent is presumed unfit, and whether the state has a sufficient basis for intervention.

prongs.label: Fit Parent Presumption
prongs.description: There is a presumption that fit parents act in their children's best interests. The state must not disregard or overturn a fit parent's decision without giving special weight to the parent's determination.
prongs.burden_note: Government must rebut the presumption that a fit parent acts in the child's best interest.

prongs.label: Deference to Parental Decision
prongs.description: When the state intervenes in parental decisions, it must accord deference to the fit parent's determination of the child's best interests. A court may not apply the opposite presumption or place the burden on the parent to disprove that third-party visitation would be in the child's best interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show it has given appropriate deference to the parent's judgment.

prongs.label: Breadth and Scope of Interference
prongs.description: The state statute or action must not be breathtakingly broad or sweep too broadly in permitting interference with parental decisions. A statute that permits any person to petition at any time based solely on a best-interest standard, without requiring a threshold showing of harm or potential harm to the child, exceeds constitutional bounds.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that its interference is narrowly tailored and not overbroad.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Established in Troxel v. Granville as a plurality opinion. The test reflects the Court's conclusion that Washington's statute violated the Due Process Clause because it failed to accord deference to the fit parent's decision and was overbroad in permitting any person to petition for visitation at any time. The Court did not decide whether harm is a required element in all nonparental visitation statutes.

doctrinal_tests.label: Eyewitness Identification Totality of Circumstances Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for evaluating whether an eyewitness identification procedure violates due process: (1) whether the procedure was suggestive, and (2) if so, whether under the totality of circumstances there is a substantial likelihood of misidentification.

prongs.label: Suggestiveness
prongs.description: Whether the identification procedure was unnecessarily suggestive.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger shows procedure was suggestive.

prongs.label: Totality of Circumstances
prongs.description: Whether, under the totality of circumstances, there is a substantial likelihood of misidentification.
prongs.burden_note: Government shows no substantial likelihood of misidentification.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Later refined in Manson v. Brathwaite into five specific reliability factors.

doctrinal_tests.label: Nollan Exaction Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-part test for evaluating whether a government-imposed condition on a development permit constitutes a taking. The condition must: (1) substantially advance a legitimate government interest, and (2) that interest must be one that would justify denial of the permit entirely.

prongs.label: Substantial Advancement
prongs.description: The exaction must substantially advance a legitimate government interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate substantial advancement; mere speculation or generalized assertions are insufficient.

prongs.label: Permit Denial Justification
prongs.description: The government interest advanced must be one that would justify denial of the permit entirely.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the interest is sufficiently important to warrant complete denial of the development right.

doctrinal_tests.notes: If the exaction fails either prong, it constitutes an uncompensated taking in violation of the Fifth Amendment.

doctrinal_tests.label: Dolan Exactions Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-part test for determining whether a city may condition approval of a development permit on the dedication of property for public use. The test requires: (1) an essential nexus between the exaction and a legitimate state interest, and (2) rough proportionality between the degree of the exaction and the impact of the proposed development.

prongs.label: Essential Nexus
prongs.description: There must be an essential nexus between the exaction imposed by the city and a legitimate state interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish a connection between the permit condition and a legitimate public purpose.

prongs.label: Rough Proportionality
prongs.description: The degree of the exaction must be roughly proportional to the impact of the proposed development on the city's ability to serve the legitimate state interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that the exaction is roughly proportional to the projected impacts of the proposed development.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test refines and clarifies the essential nexus requirement from Nollan by adding a proportionality component. The test applies to permit conditions that require dedication of property for public use.

doctrinal_tests.label: Murr Parcel Definition Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining the relevant property parcel in regulatory takings analysis by examining state and local law treatment of the land, physical characteristics, and prospective value, applying an objective inquiry into the landowner's reasonable expectations about whether the property would be treated as a single parcel or separate ones.

prongs.label: State and Local Law Treatment
prongs.description: Consider how the land is treated under applicable state and local law, including zoning ordinances and property regulations.
prongs.burden_note: Objective inquiry

prongs.label: Physical Characteristics
prongs.description: Examine the physical characteristics of the land, including size, contiguity, and geographic features.
prongs.burden_note: Objective inquiry

prongs.label: Prospective Value
prongs.description: Consider the prospective value of the regulated land and the landowner's reasonable expectations about treatment as a single or separate parcel.
prongs.burden_note: Objective inquiry

doctrinal_tests.notes: Plurality opinion establishing the test. Rejects both the approach of defining property as only the portion targeted by regulation and the approach of allowing state law alone to define property. The test applies an objective, fact-intensive inquiry.

doctrinal_tests.label: Seila Law Removal Exception Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test identifying the two narrow exceptions to the general rule that Congress cannot restrict the President's removal power over executive officers.

prongs.label: Multimember Body Exception
prongs.description: Congress may grant for-cause removal protection to a multimember body of experts who are balanced along partisan lines, appointed to staggered terms, and perform only quasi-legislative and quasi-judicial functions without exercising executive power.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the agency meets all criteria: multimember composition, partisan balance, staggered terms, and quasi-legislative/quasi-judicial functions only.

prongs.label: Inferior Officer Exception
prongs.description: Congress may grant for-cause removal protection to an inferior officer with limited duties and no policymaking or administrative authority.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the officer is inferior and has limited duties with no policymaking or administrative authority.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The Court declined to extend these exceptions to single-director agencies with substantial executive power. The test is disjunctive: an agency structure must fit within one of these two exceptions to survive constitutional scrutiny, or it violates the separation of powers.

doctrinal_tests.label: Dormant Commerce Clause Discriminatory Impact Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A state regulation that is facially neutral but has a discriminatory impact on interstate commerce by removing competitive advantages of out-of-state producers or shielding local producers from equivalent burdens violates the dormant Commerce Clause as an unreasonable burden on interstate commerce.

prongs.label: Facial Neutrality
prongs.description: The regulation must be facially neutral on its face, not explicitly discriminating against out-of-state commerce.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show the regulation is facially neutral.

prongs.label: Discriminatory Impact
prongs.description: The regulation must have a discriminatory impact on interstate commerce by removing competitive advantages of out-of-state producers or shielding local producers from equivalent burdens.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the discriminatory effect on out-of-state commerce.

prongs.label: Unreasonable Burden
prongs.description: The burden on interstate commerce must be unreasonable in relation to the putative local benefits, even if the state asserts a legitimate police power justification.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify the regulation; if burden is unreasonable, the regulation violates the dormant Commerce Clause.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to facially neutral regulations with discriminatory effects. Police power justifications (such as fraud prevention or uniformity) are insufficient to overcome the unreasonable burden on interstate commerce.

doctrinal_tests.label: Dormant Commerce Clause Discrimination Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A state law that discriminates against out-of-state goods or nonresident economic actors can be sustained only on a showing that it is narrowly tailored to advance a legitimate local purpose.

prongs.label: Discrimination Against Out-of-State Actors
prongs.description: The law must discriminate against out-of-state goods or nonresident economic actors.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must establish discriminatory effect or purpose.

prongs.label: Narrow Tailoring to Legitimate Local Purpose
prongs.description: The state must show the law is narrowly tailored to advance a legitimate local purpose. Protectionism is not a legitimate local purpose.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify the discrimination through narrow tailoring to a legitimate purpose.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies even when a state invokes the Twenty-First Amendment. The Court held that Tennessee's residency requirement failed this test because it had at best a highly attenuated relationship to public health or safety.

doctrinal_tests.label: Pickering Balancing Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-step test for determining whether a public employee's speech is protected by the First Amendment. First, the court must determine whether the employee's speech addresses a matter of public concern. If so, the court must balance the employee's interest in commenting on matters of public concern against the government's interest as an employer in promoting efficiency of public services.

prongs.label: Matter of Public Concern
prongs.description: The employee's speech must address a matter of public concern rather than a matter of purely private or internal office interest. Speech concerning internal office grievances, transfer policies, and personnel matters does not constitute speech on matters of public concern.
prongs.burden_note: Employee bears burden of demonstrating speech addresses public concern

prongs.label: Balancing of Interests
prongs.description: If the speech addresses a matter of public concern, the court must balance the employee's interest in commenting on matters of public concern against the government's interest as an employer in promoting the efficiency of public services. The government may restrict or penalize the speech if its interest in efficiency substantially outweighs the employee's speech interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate substantial interference with operations or efficiency

doctrinal_tests.notes: The Pickering balancing test was established in Pickering v. Board of Education (1968) and applied and refined in Connick v. Myers (1983). In Connick, the Court clarified that speech on internal office matters does not constitute speech on matters of public concern and therefore does not trigger the balancing analysis.

doctrinal_tests.label: Expressive Association Compelled Message Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A state law violates the First Amendment right of expressive association when applied to require a private organization to admit a member if the member's presence would compel the organization to express a message inconsistent with its stated values and mission.

prongs.label: Organization Has Expressive Purpose
prongs.description: The organization must have a clearly articulated set of values or message it seeks to express or advance.
prongs.burden_note: Organization must demonstrate its expressive purpose

prongs.label: Forced Inclusion Would Compel Expression
prongs.description: Requiring the organization to admit the member would force the organization to express a message, either to members or to the public, that is inconsistent with its stated values.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show that forced inclusion would not compel expression, or organization must show that it would

doctrinal_tests.notes: Established in Boy Scouts of America v. Dale as a test for when state public accommodations laws violate First Amendment expressive association rights. The Court found that the Boy Scouts' assertion that homosexual conduct is inconsistent with its values, combined with the compelled message that would result from admitting Dale, satisfied both prongs.

doctrinal_tests.label: Free Exercise Clause Validity Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A law regulating conduct must satisfy two requirements: (1) it must have a permissible object (not suppressing religious belief or practice), and (2) it must satisfy the principle of general applicability (pursuing any asserted secular ends with respect to all similarly situated conduct, not merely conduct motivated by religious beliefs).

prongs.label: Permissible Object
prongs.description: The law must not have an impermissible object of suppressing religious belief or practice.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the law does not target religious conduct.

prongs.label: General Applicability
prongs.description: The law must apply generally to all similarly situated conduct. If the government asserts secular ends, those ends must be pursued with respect to all conduct that poses the same harm, not merely conduct motivated by religious beliefs.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the law applies equally to secular and religious conduct with equivalent effects.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to laws of general applicability that incidentally burden religious practice. The Court emphasized that even facially neutral laws can violate the Free Exercise Clause if they selectively target religious conduct while exempting secular conduct with equivalent harmful effects.

doctrinal_tests.label: Material and Substantial Interference Test
doctrinal_tests.description: School officials may suppress student speech only if they can demonstrate that the speech would materially and substantially interfere with the operation of the school or the rights of others.

prongs.label: Material Interference
prongs.description: The school must prove that the student speech would materially interfere with the operation of the school.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of proof.

prongs.label: Substantial Interference
prongs.description: The school must prove that the student speech would substantially interfere with the operation of the school.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of proof.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Mere fear of possible disruption is insufficient; actual material and substantial interference must be demonstrated. Applies to student speech in school settings.

doctrinal_tests.label: Anti-Fusion Ballot Law Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A balancing test that weighs the character and magnitude of the burden imposed by anti-fusion laws on freedom of association against the state's interests in ballot integrity and political stability.

prongs.label: Burden on Association Rights
prongs.description: Assess the character and magnitude of the burden imposed by the anti-fusion law on the freedom of association of political parties and candidates.
prongs.burden_note: The challenger must demonstrate that the burden is substantial.

prongs.label: State Interest
prongs.description: Evaluate whether the state has a legitimate interest in ballot integrity and political stability that justifies the restriction.
prongs.burden_note: The state must demonstrate a legitimate interest.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test applies a balancing approach rather than strict scrutiny. The Court found that prohibiting fusion does not overly burden association rights because parties remain free to endorse another party's candidate.

doctrinal_tests.label: Political Party Associational Rights Burden Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test to determine whether a primary election scheme violates a political party's First Amendment right of association by forcing the party to associate with nonmembers in selecting nominees.

prongs.label: Severity of Burden
prongs.description: Whether the primary scheme imposes a severe burden on the party's associational rights by forcing association with nonmembers in nominee selection.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify any severe burden on party associational rights.

prongs.label: Necessity of Burden
prongs.description: Whether any burden imposed is necessary to serve a compelling state interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate necessity; burden found to be unnecessary if it impairs the party's basic function.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The Court found that Proposition 198's blanket primary was both severe and unnecessary, violating the party's basic function to choose its own leaders.

doctrinal_tests.label: Nebraska Press Prior Restraint Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for evaluating whether a prior restraint on press publication in the context of criminal proceedings is constitutionally permissible. The test requires consideration of whether the restraint would effectively serve the accused's right to a fair trial and whether less restrictive alternatives would be inadequate.

prongs.label: Necessity and Effectiveness
prongs.description: The government must demonstrate that the prior restraint would effectively serve the accused's right to a fair trial.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of showing effectiveness

prongs.label: Absence of Less Restrictive Alternatives
prongs.description: The government must show that less restrictive alternatives would not adequately protect the accused's fair trial rights.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of showing inadequacy of alternatives

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies specifically to prior restraints on press publication in criminal proceedings. The Court emphasized that practical problems with implementation and the fundamental principle that a community cannot be restrained from discussing matters affecting it weigh heavily against such restraints.

doctrinal_tests.label: Zauderer Attorney Advertising Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A state may regulate attorney advertising only if: (1) the advertising is truthful and not misleading, and (2) the state has a substantial interest that cannot be served by less restrictive means.

prongs.label: Truthfulness and Non-Misleading Character
prongs.description: The advertising must be truthful and not misleading.
prongs.burden_note: Burden on state to show advertising is false or misleading to justify discipline.

prongs.label: Substantial State Interest and Narrow Tailoring
prongs.description: The state must demonstrate a substantial interest that cannot be served by less restrictive means.
prongs.burden_note: Burden on state to justify regulation by substantial interest and narrow tailoring.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to attorney advertising specifically and reflects heightened protection for commercial speech involving professional services.

doctrinal_tests.label: Petition Clause Public Concern Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A threshold test determining whether a public employee's petition claim under the Petition Clause is actionable. The employee must demonstrate that the petition relates to a matter of public concern.

prongs.label: Petition Requirement
prongs.description: The employee must have engaged in conduct constituting a petition to the government.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must establish that the conduct qualifies as petition.
prongs.contest_note: Justice Scalia expressed doubt whether a lawsuit constitutes constitutionally protected petition.

prongs.label: Public Concern Requirement
prongs.description: The petition must relate to a matter of public concern.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the petition addresses a matter of public concern.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The Court rejected the Third Circuit's conclusion that the public concern test does not limit public employees' Petition Clause claims.

doctrinal_tests.label: Hazelwood Pedagogical Concerns Test
doctrinal_tests.description: Schools may exercise editorial control over student speech disseminated under their auspices if their actions are reasonably related to legitimate pedagogical concerns.

prongs.label: School Sponsorship or Auspices
prongs.description: The student speech must be disseminated under the school's auspices (e.g., school-sponsored newspaper, yearbook, or similar publication).
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must establish that speech was disseminated under school auspices.

prongs.label: Reasonable Relationship to Legitimate Pedagogical Concerns
prongs.description: School action restricting or refusing to sponsor the speech must be reasonably related to legitimate pedagogical concerns, including maintaining high standards for student speech and refusing speech inconsistent with shared values of a civilized social order.
prongs.burden_note: School must demonstrate reasonable relationship between restriction and legitimate pedagogical concern.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Applies specifically to school-sponsored student speech. Does not apply to student speech on matters of public concern outside the school-sponsored context.

doctrinal_tests.label: Shaw Race-Conscious Redistricting Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test to determine whether a reapportionment plan violates equal protection by using race as a predominant factor in drawing district lines. The test examines whether the district shape and circumstances suggest that race, rather than traditional redistricting principles, was the predominant factor motivating the plan.

prongs.label: Predominant Factor Analysis
prongs.description: Whether race was the predominant factor in drawing the district lines, as evidenced by the district's bizarre shape and other circumstances.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must establish that race was predominant; government must then justify the plan.

prongs.label: Compelling Interest Justification
prongs.description: If race was the predominant factor, the government must demonstrate a compelling governmental interest for the race-conscious plan.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears burden of justifying the race-conscious redistricting.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test applies strict scrutiny to race-conscious redistricting. The Court remanded for the District Court to determine whether a compelling governmental interest justified North Carolina's plan.

doctrinal_tests.label: Structural Waiver Test for Sovereign Immunity Abrogation
doctrinal_tests.description: Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity when the federal power is complete in itself and the States consented to the exercise of that power—in its entirety—in the plan of the Convention.

prongs.label: Federal Power is Complete in Itself
prongs.description: The federal power at issue must be complete and self-contained, not dependent on state cooperation.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate the federal power is complete in itself.

prongs.label: States Consented to the Exercise of That Power in Its Entirety
prongs.description: The States must have implicitly consented to the exercise of that federal power in its entirety upon joining the Union and adopting the plan of the Convention.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate States consented to the federal power in the plan of the Convention.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to determine whether Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity in the exercise of enumerated federal powers. The Court applied this test to Congress's power to raise and support the Armed Forces.

doctrinal_tests.label: Dormant Commerce Clause Balancing Test for Local Ordinances
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for evaluating whether a local ordinance violates the dormant Commerce Clause by balancing the burden on interstate commerce against local public benefits, applied when the ordinance does not discriminate against out-of-state commerce.

prongs.label: Nondiscrimination
prongs.description: The ordinance must not discriminate against out-of-state private businesses; it may favor publicly-operated facilities.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show discrimination against out-of-state commerce.

prongs.label: Burden on Interstate Commerce
prongs.description: Assess whether the ordinance imposes more than an incidental burden on interstate commerce.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the burden; government may show it is incidental.

prongs.label: Balance Against Local Benefits
prongs.description: Balance any burden on interstate commerce against the ordinance's financial, health, and environmental benefits to the local community.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate legitimate local benefits that outweigh any incidental burden.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies to local ordinances that do not discriminate against out-of-state commerce. The Court declined to apply rigorous scrutiny, instead deferring to local policy analysis.

doctrinal_tests.label: Glucksberg Substantive Due Process Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-part test for determining whether an asserted liberty interest is a fundamental right protected by the Due Process Clause: (1) the interest must be objectively deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition, and (2) the Court must carefully describe the asserted fundamental liberty interest with specificity rather than at a high level of generality.

prongs.label: History and Tradition
prongs.description: The asserted liberty interest must be objectively deeply rooted in this Nation's history and tradition.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate that the interest is deeply rooted in history and tradition.

prongs.label: Careful Description
prongs.description: The Court must carefully describe the asserted fundamental liberty interest with appropriate specificity, not at a high level of generality.
prongs.burden_note: The interest must be narrowly and specifically defined, not broadly characterized.

doctrinal_tests.notes: If an asserted interest fails either prong, it is not a fundamental liberty interest protected by the Due Process Clause. If the interest is not fundamental, rational basis review applies to the state regulation. The test requires that interests be described narrowly; broad formulations such as 'the right to die' or 'liberty to choose how to die' are rejected in favor of precise characterization of the actual liberty at stake.

doctrinal_tests.label: Prisoner Supermaximum Placement Due Process Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A procedural due process test for evaluating whether state procedures for classifying and placing prisoners in supermaximum security facilities comply with the Fourteenth Amendment. The test requires procedures that sufficiently minimize the risk of erroneous placement while accounting for legitimate state interests in prison security and resource allocation.

prongs.label: Risk Minimization
prongs.description: The procedures must sufficiently minimize the risk of erroneous placement in the supermaximum facility.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that procedures adequately reduce erroneous placement risk.

prongs.label: State Interest Accommodation
prongs.description: The procedures must account for and accommodate legitimate state interests in prison security (including gang prevention) and preservation of scarce resources.
prongs.burden_note: Government may rely on its penological interests to justify limited procedural protections.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies a balancing approach that recognizes necessarily limited procedural protections in the prison context. The test does not require elaborate procedures but rather sufficient safeguards including notice, opportunity for rebuttal, and multi-tier review.

doctrinal_tests.label: RFRA Compelling Interest Test
doctrinal_tests.description: Under the Religious Freedom Restoration Act, when the Federal Government substantially burdens a person's exercise of religion, the Government must demonstrate that the burden is in furtherance of a compelling government interest and is the least restrictive means of furthering that interest.

prongs.label: Compelling Government Interest
prongs.description: The Government must demonstrate that the burden on religious practice is in furtherance of a compelling government interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of proof

prongs.label: Least Restrictive Means
prongs.description: The Government must demonstrate that the burden is the least restrictive means of furthering the compelling government interest.
prongs.burden_note: Government bears the burden of proof

doctrinal_tests.notes: Codified in 42 U.S.C. § 2000bb-1(b). This test applies to federal government action. In this case, the Court found the Government failed to satisfy the test regarding uniform application of the Controlled Substances Act.

doctrinal_tests.label: Government Speech Holistic Inquiry Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A three-factor test to determine whether expression constitutes government speech. The test considers: (1) the history of the expression at issue; (2) the public's perception as to who is speaking; and (3) the extent to which the government has controlled the expression. The inquiry is holistic and no single factor is dispositive.

prongs.label: History of the Expression
prongs.description: Whether there is a history of the government engaging in the type of expression at issue.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate historical practice.

prongs.label: Public Perception
prongs.description: Whether the public would perceive the expression as the government's own message or as private speech.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the public would associate the message with the government.

prongs.label: Government Control
prongs.description: The extent to which the government has controlled the content and form of the expression.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate meaningful control over the expression.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test is applied holistically; no single factor is dispositive. In Shurtleff, although history favored the government, the other two factors (public perception and lack of control) outweighed it, resulting in a finding that the flag-raising program was not government speech.

doctrinal_tests.label: Presidential Immunity Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A multi-step test to determine whether a former President is entitled to immunity from criminal prosecution. First, determine whether the conduct is official or unofficial. If official, determine whether it falls within the President's exclusive constitutional authority (absolute immunity) or shared authority (presumptive immunity). If unofficial, no immunity applies.

prongs.label: Official Versus Unofficial Conduct
prongs.description: Determine whether the alleged conduct is official or unofficial. Official conduct is undertaken pursuant to constitutional and statutory authority within the outer perimeter of the President's official responsibilities. Courts may not inquire into the President's motives and may not deem an action unofficial merely because it allegedly violates a generally applicable law.
prongs.burden_note: Threshold determination; if unofficial, no immunity applies.

prongs.label: Exclusive Constitutional Authority
prongs.description: If the conduct is official, determine whether it falls within the President's conclusive and preclusive constitutional authority. If so, absolute immunity applies.
prongs.burden_note: If the President's authority is conclusive and preclusive, the President is absolutely immune.

prongs.label: Presumptive Immunity for Shared Authority
prongs.description: If the conduct is official but does not fall within the President's exclusive constitutional authority, the President is entitled to at least presumptive immunity. The Government bears the burden of rebutting this presumption by showing that applying a criminal prohibition to the act would pose no dangers of intrusion on the authority and functions of the Executive Branch.
prongs.burden_note: Government must rebut the presumption of immunity.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Established in Trump v. United States (2024). The test requires careful fact-specific analysis, particularly in distinguishing official from unofficial conduct. Courts must not inquire into Presidential motives and must recognize the outer perimeter of official responsibility.

doctrinal_tests.label: Temporary Moratorium Takings Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A multi-factor test for determining whether a temporary moratorium on development constitutes a taking of property requiring just compensation, focusing on landowners' reasonable expectations, actual economic impact, public interest, and reasons for the moratorium.

prongs.label: Landowners' Reasonable Expectations
prongs.description: Whether the landowners had reasonable expectations regarding the use of their property at the time of acquisition.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate unreasonable deprivation of expectations

prongs.label: Actual Economic Impact
prongs.description: The actual economic impact of the moratorium on the landowners' property interests.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate significant economic harm

prongs.label: Public Interest
prongs.description: The public interest served by the moratorium and the government's land-use planning objectives.
prongs.burden_note: Government may justify temporary restrictions through public interest considerations

prongs.label: Reasons for the Moratorium
prongs.description: The reasons and justifications for imposing the temporary moratorium on development.
prongs.burden_note: Government must articulate legitimate reasons for the temporary restriction

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test rejects categorical rules that any deprivation of all economic use, no matter how brief, constitutes a compensable taking. The test applies a balancing approach to temporary land-use restrictions.

doctrinal_tests.label: Fifth Amendment Takings Multifactor Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether government action constitutes a compensable taking under the Fifth Amendment. Courts must consider multiple factors including the duration and severity of the interference, the intent behind the government action, and the degree to which the interference was a foreseeable result of an authorized government action. No single factor is dispositive, and temporary interference does not categorically exclude a finding of taking.

prongs.label: Duration and Severity of Interference
prongs.description: The length and severity of the government's interference with property rights is one factor to consider, but is not dispositive and does not create a categorical exception for temporary takings.
prongs.burden_note: Shared; courts weigh this factor among others

prongs.label: Intent Behind Government Action
prongs.description: The intent or purpose of the government action is a relevant factor in determining whether a taking has occurred.
prongs.burden_note: Shared; courts weigh this factor among others

prongs.label: Foreseeability of Interference
prongs.description: The degree to which the interference was a foreseeable result of an authorized government action is a relevant factor in the takings analysis.
prongs.burden_note: Shared; courts weigh this factor among others

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test rejects the Federal Circuit's categorical rule that only permanent takings are compensable. The Court emphasized that prior precedent clearly establishes that temporary interference can constitute a compensable taking, and courts must apply a multifactor balancing approach rather than categorical rules.

doctrinal_tests.label: Internal Consistency Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test under the dormant Commerce Clause that asks whether a tax scheme would disadvantage interstate commerce if applied identically in every state. If so, the tax is unconstitutional.

prongs.label: Hypothetical Uniform Application
prongs.description: Determine whether the tax scheme, if applied identically in every state, would disadvantage interstate commerce.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that the tax scheme does not create a discriminatory burden on interstate commerce under hypothetical uniform application.
prongs.contest_note: Justice Ginsburg argued in dissent that the internal consistency test need not be rigidly enforced and should not apply when discriminatory effect results from combination of two otherwise-lawful tax schemes.

prongs.label: No Discriminatory Burden on Interstate Commerce
prongs.description: The tax scheme must not impose a discriminatory burden on interstate commerce relative to intrastate commerce.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the scheme is non-discriminatory under uniform application.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Applied in Comptroller v. Wynne to invalidate Maryland's county tax scheme. The test focuses on the structural effect of the tax rather than facial discrimination.

doctrinal_tests.label: Goss Student Suspension Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether a student suspension complies with Due Process requirements. The test requires that before a student is suspended, the student must receive notice of the charges and be afforded some kind of hearing.

prongs.label: Notice
prongs.description: The student must be given notice of the charges against them.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide notice before suspension.

prongs.label: Hearing
prongs.description: The student must be afforded some kind of hearing.
prongs.burden_note: Government must provide an opportunity for a hearing before suspension.

doctrinal_tests.notes: Establishes minimum procedural protections for student suspensions under the Due Process Clause. The Court indicated these are minimum requirements; more extensive procedures may be required in other contexts.

doctrinal_tests.label: Race-Based Admissions Strict Scrutiny Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for evaluating whether race-based admissions programs satisfy the Equal Protection Clause. The test requires that the government demonstrate: (1) a compelling interest in a measurable way; (2) that the program avoids racial stereotyping; and (3) a logical endpoint for when race-based admissions will cease. All three prongs must be satisfied for the program to survive strict scrutiny.

prongs.label: Measurable Compelling Interest
prongs.description: The government must demonstrate a compelling interest in race-based admissions in a measurable way, not merely asserted or assumed.
prongs.burden_note: Government must affirmatively demonstrate the compelling interest with measurable evidence.

prongs.label: Avoidance of Racial Stereotyping
prongs.description: The race-based admissions program must not result in or rely upon racial stereotyping of applicants or groups.
prongs.burden_note: Government must ensure the program does not stereotype based on race.

prongs.label: Logical Endpoint
prongs.description: The race-based admissions program must have a logical endpoint or termination date, not continue indefinitely.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish when race-based admissions will cease.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test refines and tightens the strict scrutiny standard as applied to race-based admissions. It builds on but supersedes the framework established in Grutter v. Bollinger and Regents v. Bakke by adding explicit requirements for measurability, non-stereotyping, and temporal limits.

doctrinal_tests.label: Randolph Co-Occupant Consent Search Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining the validity of a consent-based warrantless search when multiple co-occupants are present. The search is unconstitutional if one co-occupant expressly refuses consent while another consents, provided the refusing occupant is present and able to object.

prongs.label: Multiple Co-Occupants Present
prongs.description: Two or more co-occupants must be present at the time of the search request.
prongs.burden_note: Government must establish that the search involves multiple co-occupants.

prongs.label: Express Refusal by One Occupant
prongs.description: One co-occupant must expressly refuse consent to the search.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show that no co-occupant expressly refused consent.

prongs.label: Consent by Another Occupant
prongs.description: Another co-occupant consents to the search.
prongs.burden_note: Government may rely on consent from one co-occupant, but this is insufficient if another refuses.

doctrinal_tests.notes: If all three prongs are satisfied, the search is unconstitutional. The test reflects the principle that a reasonable person would not enter shared premises when one occupant explicitly refuses, even with another's consent.

doctrinal_tests.label: Gregg Capital Sentencing Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional test for imposing capital punishment requiring: (1) a bifurcated trial with separate guilt and penalty stages; (2) the jury must find at least one aggravating circumstance beyond a reasonable doubt before death may be imposed; (3) the jury must be permitted to consider mitigating circumstances; and (4) appellate review to ensure consistency and prevent arbitrary application.

prongs.label: Bifurcated Trial Structure
prongs.description: Capital cases must proceed in two stages: a guilt stage and a separate penalty stage.
prongs.burden_note: Government must structure the trial to separate guilt determination from penalty determination.

prongs.label: Aggravating Circumstance Finding
prongs.description: The jury must find at least one aggravating circumstance beyond a reasonable doubt before the death penalty may be imposed.
prongs.burden_note: Government must prove at least one aggravating circumstance beyond a reasonable doubt.

prongs.label: Consideration of Mitigating Circumstances
prongs.description: The jury must be permitted to consider any mitigating circumstances presented by the defendant.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant may present mitigating evidence; jury must be free to consider it.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test established the constitutional framework for capital sentencing that prevents arbitrary and capricious application of the death penalty while permitting states to impose capital punishment.

doctrinal_tests.label: Solem Proportionality Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A three-part test for determining whether a sentence violates the Eighth Amendment's prohibition on cruel and unusual punishment by being grossly disproportionate to the offense.

prongs.label: Gravity of Offense and Harshness of Penalty
prongs.description: Courts must examine the nature of the offense and the severity of the sentence imposed.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify the proportionality of the sentence to the offense.

prongs.label: Sentences Imposed on Other Criminals in the Same Jurisdiction
prongs.description: Courts must compare the sentence imposed to sentences imposed for commission of other crimes in the same jurisdiction.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify why this defendant received a harsher sentence than others convicted of more serious crimes.

prongs.label: Sentences Imposed for Commission of the Same Crime in Other Jurisdictions
prongs.description: Courts must consider sentences imposed for the same crime in other jurisdictions.
prongs.burden_note: Government must justify why this jurisdiction imposes a significantly harsher sentence than other jurisdictions.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The test applies to sentences that may be grossly disproportionate to the offense. A sentence is unconstitutional under the Eighth Amendment if it is grossly disproportionate when measured against these three factors.

doctrinal_tests.label: Eighth Amendment Capital Punishment Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-part test for determining whether a particular crime may be punished by death under the Eighth Amendment: (1) whether there is a national consensus against the punishment, and (2) whether the punishment would be excessive in light of the crime and its circumstances.

prongs.label: National Consensus
prongs.description: Examine whether there is a national consensus against imposing the death penalty for the particular crime, considering the number of states that have adopted similar laws and the direction of legislative and jury trends.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate a national consensus supporting the punishment.
prongs.contest_note: The dissent argued that five states adopting similar laws constituted a national consensus; the majority disagreed.

prongs.label: Excessiveness
prongs.description: Determine whether the punishment would be excessive in relation to the crime, considering factors such as whether the crime resulted in or was intended to result in the victim's death.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that the punishment is not excessive.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test builds on methodology established in Atkins v. Virginia and Roper v. Simmons. The dissent contended that individual facts of the case, including the age of the child, the sadistic nature of the crime, and the number of rapes, should be considered rather than applying a blanket rule.

doctrinal_tests.label: Graham Categorical Eighth Amendment Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A two-step categorical analysis for determining whether a sentence imposed on a class of offenders violates the Eighth Amendment: (1) consider objective indicia of society's standards, and (2) determine whether the punishment violates the Constitution guided by standards elaborated by controlling precedents.

prongs.label: Objective Indicia of Society's Standards
prongs.description: Examine objective evidence of how society views the punishment in question, including domestic and international practice.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that the punishment reflects contemporary standards of decency.

prongs.label: Constitutional Guidance from Controlling Precedents
prongs.description: Determine whether the punishment violates the Constitution as guided by standards elaborated in controlling Supreme Court precedents.
prongs.burden_note: Government must show the punishment is consistent with constitutional principles established in prior cases.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies when a case implicates a particular type of sentence as it applies to an entire class of offenders. The Court emphasized that life sentences without parole for juveniles in non-homicidal crimes fail both prongs.

doctrinal_tests.label: Sixth Amendment Effective Assistance in Plea Negotiations Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A defendant claiming ineffective assistance of counsel in the context of plea negotiations must demonstrate: (1) counsel's performance was deficient (e.g., failure to communicate a plea offer), and (2) the deficient performance caused prejudice. Prejudice in the plea context does not require showing the trial outcome would have been different, but rather that the defendant would have accepted the more favorable plea offer had counsel properly communicated it.

prongs.label: Deficient Performance
prongs.description: Counsel's performance must fall below an objective standard of reasonableness. In the plea negotiation context, this includes the duty to communicate plea offers to the defendant within the offer window.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must show counsel's conduct was objectively unreasonable.

prongs.label: Prejudice
prongs.description: The defendant must demonstrate that counsel's deficient performance caused prejudice. In the plea context, prejudice is shown by evidence that the defendant would have accepted the more favorable plea offer had counsel communicated it, not by demonstrating a different trial outcome.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must show a reasonable probability that, but for counsel's deficient performance, the defendant would have accepted the plea offer.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies the Strickland framework to the specific context of plea negotiations. The Court clarifies that prejudice in the plea context differs from prejudice at trial—the defendant need not show the trial outcome would have been different.

doctrinal_tests.label: Lafler Ineffective Assistance in Plea Context Test
doctrinal_tests.description: When a defendant claims ineffective assistance of counsel regarding a plea offer, the court must determine whether, absent the ineffective counsel, the defendant would have accepted the offered plea that was less severe than the eventual sentence, and whether the trial court would have accepted the plea terms.

prongs.label: Deficient Performance
prongs.description: Counsel's advice regarding the plea offer was deficient, falling below an objective standard of reasonableness.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must show counsel's performance was deficient.

prongs.label: Defendant Would Have Accepted Plea
prongs.description: Absent the ineffective counsel, the defendant would have accepted the offered plea that was less severe than the eventual sentence.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must show reasonable probability of acceptance.

prongs.label: Trial Court Would Have Accepted Plea
prongs.description: The trial court would have accepted the terms of the plea offer.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must show reasonable probability of acceptance by trial court.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test applies Strickland v. Washington to the specific context of ineffective assistance regarding plea offers. The remedy is not specific performance of the original plea, but rather the prosecution may re-offer the plea and, if accepted, the trial court may decide how to amend the sentence.

doctrinal_tests.label: McDonald Incorporation Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A test for determining whether a right should be incorporated against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause. A right qualifies for incorporation if it is fundamental to the Nation's scheme of ordered liberty or deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition.

prongs.label: Fundamental to the Nation's Scheme of Ordered Liberty
prongs.description: The right must be fundamental to the Nation's scheme of ordered liberty.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the right is fundamental to ordered liberty.

prongs.label: Deeply Rooted in the Nation's History and Tradition
prongs.description: The right must be deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must demonstrate the right is deeply rooted in history and tradition.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test is disjunctive — satisfaction of either prong suffices for incorporation. The Court applied this test to the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms, finding it satisfied both prongs based on the reasoning in District of Columbia v. Heller.

doctrinal_tests.label: Intelligible Principle Test
doctrinal_tests.description: A delegation of legislative authority is constitutional only if Congress establishes intelligible principles or standards that guide and limit the delegatee's discretion in implementing the delegated authority.

prongs.label: Intelligible Principle or Standard
prongs.description: Congress must establish intelligible principles or standards that define the scope and limits of the delegated authority.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that Congress provided meaningful guidance.

prongs.label: Absence of Unbridled Discretion
prongs.description: The delegatee cannot be granted unbridled or unfettered discretion to make whatever laws or regulations it believes necessary to achieve a general goal.
prongs.burden_note: Government must demonstrate that the delegatee's discretion is meaningfully constrained.

doctrinal_tests.notes: This test articulates the constitutional requirement that delegations of legislative authority must be accompanied by intelligible principles or standards. The Court emphasized that the NIRA failed this test because it provided no rules or standards to evaluate industrial activity.

edges.notes: The 14th Amendment Due Process Clause incorporates the 1st Amendment speech protections against state governments. Valid_from reflects Gitlow (1925), first case to hold this.
The 1st Amendment applies to state governments via the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause.
edges.theory: selective incorporation via Due Process Clause

edges.citation: Virginia State Board of Pharmacy v. Virginia Citizens Consumer Council, 425 U.S. 748 (1976)
edges.notes: Commercial speech became a recognized doctrinal sub-area only after Virginia Pharmacy Board extended First Amendment protection to it.

SCHEMA NOTE: Valentine established a rule by denying protection — not by articulating a positive doctrine. This edge exposes a gap: ESTABLISHES implies positive creation. Consider adding holding_type: [affirmative | by_negation] to Case nodes, or a separate ESTABLISHES_BY_NEGATION edge type.

The Court did not explicitly say it was overruling Valentine, but the holding is directly incompatible. SCHEMA NOTE: Need a way to represent implicit vs. explicit overruling. Consider adding overrule_type: [explicit | implicit] to OVERRULES edges. The Court stated that the notion of unprotected commercial speech had 'passed from the scene,' using language stronger than typical implicit overrulings.

SCHEMA NOTE: Posadas both applies AND modifies Central Hudson — the 'greater includes lesser' reasoning expanded the test's deference to government. A single node can have both APPLIES and MODIFIES edges to the same target. Is that right, or should MODIFIES subsume APPLIES? Consider making MODIFIES a sub-type that implies application.

SCHEMA NOTE: DISTINGUISHES is technically correct but undersells what happened — 44 Liquormart effectively repudiated Posadas's 'greater includes lesser' reasoning without formally overruling it. Consider adding a LIMITS edge type, or a direction attribute on MODIFIES: [narrows | expands | complicates | repudiates]. This is the Sorrell problem anticipated in our design discussion.
Tightened application of prongs 3 and 4. Rejected broad deference to legislative judgment. Direction: narrows (government-friendly application).

Direction: complicates. Sorrell raised the possibility that content- or speaker-based commercial speech restrictions might trigger scrutiny higher than Central Hudson intermediate scrutiny — potentially strict scrutiny. The Court declined to resolve this, leaving the framework unsettled. SCHEMA NOTE: This is the clearest case for a direction attribute on MODIFIES. 'Complicates' is not the same as 'narrows' or 'expands'.
Cross-slice reference — amendment_1 defined in commercial_speech slice.

SCHEMA ISSUE A: Brandenburg established this test as majority law, but the test's intellectual substance was articulated in dissents and concurrences in earlier cases — Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919), Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927). source_case_id on DoctrinalTest, and ESTABLISHES edges generally, have no mechanism to represent this distinction between 'case that made this majority law' and 'non-majority opinions that originated the doctrine's content.'
Cross-slice reference — amendment_1 defined in commercial_speech slice.

Direction: clarifies. Hess operationalized the imminence prong — 'later' is not imminent. This is a clarification, not a narrowing or expansion: it resolves ambiguity in prong 1 without changing the scope of the test.

Direction: expands. Claiborne extended the Brandenburg/incitement framework to civil liability contexts. The doctrine previously operated in criminal prosecution cases; Claiborne established it applies with equal force against civil tort claims, expanding the doctrine's scope of application.
MODIFIES is not present for the test node itself — Claiborne applied the Brandenburg test without changing its prongs. Applied the test in a civil context for the first time.

Direction: expands. Snyder extended the public concern doctrine from the public employee speech context (where Connick v. Myers originated it) to private plaintiff intentional infliction of emotional distress claims. The doctrine node's valid_from is 1983 (Connick), but Connick is outside this slice's scope. SCHEMA ISSUE B: This MODIFIES edge has Snyder as source, but the target doctrine originated in Connick (1983), which has no case node in this slice. The ESTABLISHES edge for this doctrine is missing — it would need to come from a Connick node that doesn't exist here. Cross-slice node dependency not yet formally supported.

Direction: clarifies. Counterman resolved the circuit split on the mens rea standard for true threats, settling that recklessness is the constitutional floor. The true threats category itself was established earlier (Virginia v. Black, 2003; R.A.V., 1992) — Counterman clarified the mental state element rather than creating the category. SCHEMA ISSUE C: The true_threats_doctrine node has valid_from: 2023 because that's when the current formulation became operative. But the category predates Counterman by decades. The ESTABLISHES edge for the category (from Virginia v. Black or R.A.V.) is missing — those cases are out of scope. Same cross-slice dependency problem as Issue B.
The post-Counterman true threats test is new — prior circuit standards varied and are now constitutionally deficient. Counterman is genuinely the source_case_id for this test formulation.
Added in v0.2 fix — parent_id existed on node but CHILD_OF edge was missing from edge list.
Added in v0.2 fix — parent_id existed on node but CHILD_OF edge was missing from edge list.

Repudiates: Virginia Pharmacy effectively repudiated the commercial_speech_no_protection doctrine by establishing that commercial speech receives First Amendment protection — directly negating the prior no-protection rule. Renamed from OVERRULES (invalid Case->Doctrine) to MODIFIES(repudiates).

Historical governance only. The no-protection rule governed commercial speech from Valentine v. Chrestensen (1942) until Virginia State Bd. of Pharmacy (1976) established First Amendment coverage. valid_until marks the overruling date.

Cross-slice reference — amendment_1 defined in commercial_speech slice. Prior restraint doctrine is grounded in the Press Clause as well as the Speech Clause of the First Amendment. Near emphasized the press freedom dimension; Pentagon Papers applied both.

edges.condition_note: General test for prior restraint validity; Madsen applies specifically to injunctions

edges.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE E: This GOVERNED_BY edge is conditionally applicable — the Madsen test governs only when the government instrument is a content-neutral injunction against specific parties. The prior_restraint_test governs classic prior restraints (licensing schemes, administrative censorship). The schema cannot express this conditionality on edge type. A future edge attribute — 'condition' or 'instrument_type' — would be needed.
edges.condition_note: Applies only when the prior restraint instrument is a judicial injunction, not a statute or licensing scheme

edges.notes: Prior restraint doctrine and core political speech doctrine are closely related: prior restraint is the procedural mechanism by which core political speech is most severely threatened. Near v. Minnesota involved a political newspaper. Pentagon Papers involved political speech (government conduct of the Vietnam War). The RELATED_TO edge here is the first use of this edge type in the graph — per schema Issue #12, the editorial criteria for RELATED_TO need to be defined. This edge is justified by: (1) shared constitutional text (both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1), (2) overlapping case law (political speech cases often invoke prior restraint analysis), and (3) the doctrines operate as complementary protections — prior restraint is the 'how it's suppressed' dimension of the same protection that core political speech doctrine addresses from the 'what is protected' dimension.

Pentagon Papers clarified — through six concurrences — what the government must show to satisfy the prior restraint test's direct-harm prong. The per curiam itself said only that the government had not met its burden; the operational content of what would suffice comes from the concurrences. Direction: clarifies (operationalizes the burden without changing the test's structure).

Progressive applied the prior restraint test and found the government's showing sufficient — the only federal court ever to do so in a national security prior restraint case. valid_until reflects mootness date. The holding is district court only, never reviewed on appeal, and has no precedential force. SCHEMA ISSUE B: The APPLIES edge is structurally identical to APPLIES edges from SCOTUS cases, but this case's holding is non-precedential, unreviewd, and mooted. The schema has no way to weight or qualify edge authority by court level or procedural posture.

Clarifies: Alexander clarified the boundary between prior restraint and subsequent punishment, holding that RICO forfeiture of assets from past obscene speech is subsequent punishment not prior restraint. This clarifies the doctrine's scope without narrowing or expanding it. Changed from DISTINGUISHES (invalid Case->Doctrine) to MODIFIES(clarifies).

Madsen clarified that content-neutral injunctions against specific parties are not classic prior restraints and are governed by a distinct intermediate standard. This clarifies the doctrine's scope without narrowing or expanding it: it marks a boundary between what counts as a classic prior restraint and what is a different (though related) form of speech restriction.

The Press Clause of the First Amendment ('or of the press') is the textual grounding. Courts have generally not treated the Press Clause as conferring rights categorically different from those of ordinary speakers, but it provides the constitutional foundation for the distinctive press freedom doctrines modeled here.

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1, specifically the Press Clause. (2) Overlapping case law — Near v. Minnesota (1931) and NYT v. United States (1971) appear in both doctrinal lineages; Near is the foundational prior restraint case and the foundational press freedom case. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — prior restraint addresses the mechanism of pre-publication suppression; freedom of the press addresses post-publication liability and press-specific constitutional protections. Different analytical mechanisms protecting the same press freedom interest.

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Sullivan's public concern analysis imports reasoning from the core political speech tradition; Snyder v. Phelps (core_political_speech slice) applies public concern doctrine in a press-adjacent context. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — core political speech doctrine governs what speech is protected; freedom of the press doctrine governs how press defendants are treated in defamation and related torts. Overlapping but not hierarchical — press freedom is not a sub-category of core political speech.

SCHEMA ISSUE A: This GOVERNED_BY edge is accurate but incomplete. The public_figure_classification_test is a threshold classification that determines which other test applies (actual_malice_test vs. negligence). The schema has no way to represent a DoctrinalTest that is a precondition to another DoctrinalTest. The conditional relationship between this test and actual_malice_test requires a new edge type or edge attribute.

Sullivan applied First Amendment protections against a state defamation judgment via the 14th Amendment. This is the incorporation mechanism — 1st Amendment applied against state through 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Sullivan distinguished the prior restraint context from the post-publication liability context. Near established press freedom from prior restraint; Sullivan established press freedom from post-publication defamation liability. Brennan's Sullivan opinion cited Near as part of the historical foundation for robust press protection.

Expands: Butts extended the constitutional protection — and the actual malice standard — from public officials to public figures. The plurality applied a different standard (highly unreasonable conduct) but Warren's concurrence, which was adopted in Gertz, applied full actual malice. The expansion is ultimately confirmed by Gertz.

Warren's Butts concurrence argued for applying full actual malice to public figures. Gertz's majority adopted this position, superseding Harlan's plurality standard from Butts. This is a clean INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES instance: a concurrence in an earlier case became the majority rule in a later case.

Clarifies: Gertz established that actual malice applies to public figures (confirming Warren's Butts concurrence, superseding Harlan's plurality standard) and established the public figure taxonomy that determines who must prove actual malice. It also clarified that actual malice governs presumed and punitive damages even for private figure plaintiffs.

Extends: Hustler extended the actual malice standard to IIED claims by public figures — a different cause of action (tort, not defamation) in a new legal context. The doctrine's prongs are unchanged; its applicability is expanded to a new category of claims. This is a clean use of the MODIFIES(extends) direction value added in v0.4.

Snyder v. Phelps extended Hustler's reasoning to private plaintiff IIED claims in a public concern context, distinguishing Hustler (public figure plaintiff) while reaching a similar result through the public concern doctrine. The doctrinal connection between these two cases is important for understanding the full scope of IIED protection for press-adjacent speech.

Clarifies: Milkovich added the opinion/fact distinction as prong 3 of the actual malice test by establishing that a statement must be capable of being proven false to be actionable. This clarifies the scope of the test without narrowing or expanding it.
Stub governance edge. reporters_privilege_doctrine is modeled as a stub pending Phase 2 statute layer. pending_establishment on the doctrine node documents the Branzburg dependency.
Reporter's privilege claims, to the extent they exist constitutionally, derive from the Press Clause of the First Amendment.
The Free Exercise Clause is textually distinct from the Speech and Press Clauses — 'or prohibiting the free exercise thereof' — and provides the constitutional grounding for this area.

condition.context: Applies to non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws, and to individualized exemption systems
condition.note: Sherbert remains operative where Smith does not apply — non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws, unemployment compensation cases, and is restored by RFRA for federal laws

condition.context: Applies only to neutral, generally applicable laws
condition.note: Smith test governs neutral general laws only. Non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws trigger strict scrutiny regardless of Smith.

edges.notes: RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) involved a licensing scheme for religious solicitation that is simultaneously a prior restraint case and a Free Exercise case. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — prior restraint governs the mechanism of pre-publication suppression; free exercise governs government targeting of religious practice. Different analytical frameworks protecting related First Amendment interests.
Cantwell incorporated the Free Exercise Clause against states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Expands: Sherbert significantly expanded the protection of religiously motivated conduct by requiring the government to satisfy strict scrutiny before enforcing laws that substantially burden religious practice — even neutral generally applicable laws.

Expands: Yoder extended the Sherbert compelling interest framework to compulsory education laws and established that the government's interest in compulsory schooling was not compelling enough to override deeply held religious objections backed by a demonstrated religious way of life. The case set the high-water mark for Free Exercise protection.

Narrows: Smith significantly narrowed Sherbert by holding that the compelling interest test applies only to non-neutral or non-generally-applicable laws — not to neutral general laws that incidentally burden religious practice. This is not a full overruling of Sherbert (which remains operative in unemployment compensation cases and analogous contexts) but a major narrowing of its scope.

Narrows: Smith significantly narrowed Free Exercise protection for religiously motivated conduct by holding that the Free Exercise Clause does not require religious exemptions from neutral, generally applicable laws.

Harlan's Sherbert dissent anticipated an important strand of Smith's reasoning: concern that constitutionally compelled religion-specific exemptions from neutral laws raise structural and administrability problems.
