Clarifies: Lukumi gave definitive content to what Smith meant by neutral and generally applicable — standards that Smith articulated but did not fully define. This is a classic clarification: it resolves ambiguity in the test without narrowing or expanding its scope.

Clarifies: Fulton significantly expanded the general applicability inquiry by holding that a categorical exception in a contract that allows for discretionary secular exemptions makes the entire policy not generally applicable — even if no such exemption had ever been granted. This is a clarification that in practice significantly expands when strict scrutiny applies, but without formally changing the Smith framework.

Expands the general-applicability branch of the doctrine by treating discretionary secular exemptions as sufficient to defeat general applicability, even absent evidence that such exemptions were actually granted. The neutrality branch is not directly implicated by Fulton's holding.
The Establishment Clause — 'Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion' — is the textual source. Incorporated against states via 14th Amendment in Everson (1947).

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both clauses appear in the same sentence of the First Amendment and are GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) implicated both; Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) decided both Free Exercise and Establishment Clause claims; RFRA cases raise both. (3) Complementary but structurally tensioned — the two clauses protect the same interest (religious liberty) but through opposing commands: Establishment Clause prohibits government promotion of religion; Free Exercise Clause prohibits government suppression of religion. Laws that accommodate religion risk Establishment Clause challenge; laws that are neutral toward religion risk Free Exercise challenge. This structural tension is unique to the Religion Clauses — no other RELATED_TO pair has this opposing-commands relationship. SCHEMA ISSUE D: The Religion Clauses' structural tension is different from the complementary relationship between prior restraint and core political speech. Consider adding a relationship_type attribute to RELATED_TO edges: complementary | structurally_tensioned | historically_linked. relationship_type: structurally_tensioned — the two clauses protect the same interest through opposing commands, creating genuine constitutional tension rather than mere doctrinal overlap.
Historical governance only. The Lemon test governed Establishment Clause analysis from 1971 until it was explicitly overruled by Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022). valid_until: 2022-06-27.

condition.context: Particularly applicable in school and captive-audience contexts
condition.note: The coercion test survived Kennedy v. Bremerton and remains operative, especially in school prayer contexts where captive audience and authority relationships make coercion analysis most relevant.

edges.notes: The post-Kennedy governing standard. Operative from June 27, 2022 forward.
Everson incorporated the Establishment Clause against the states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Clarifies: Lemon established the first structured analytical framework for Establishment Clause analysis, giving the doctrine a concrete operational test. This clarified how courts apply the doctrine without narrowing or expanding its substantive scope.
Lee applied Lemon alongside the new coercion test. valid_until reflects Lemon's overruling.

Scalia's Lee dissent (1992) argued for a history-and-tradition approach to Establishment Clause analysis rather than the coercion test or Lemon. Gorsuch's Kennedy majority adopted this approach 30 years later. This is the second-longest INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES gap in the graph (30 years, compared to Holmes-Abrams to Brandenburg at 50 years).

Complicates: Van Orden introduced significant uncertainty about the Lemon test's continued vitality. The plurality openly moved toward a history-and-tradition approach; Breyer's controlling concurrence applied ad hoc balancing. Combined with McCreary's opposite result on the same day, Van Orden significantly complicated the doctrinal picture.

Van Orden and McCreary were companion cases decided the same day reaching opposite results on factually different Ten Commandments displays. The contrast illustrates that Establishment Clause analysis of religious displays is highly context-specific. Companion-case contrast; direction is conventional rather than temporal.
valid_until reflects Lemon's overruling by Kennedy.

Scalia's McCreary dissent (2005) argued the Establishment Clause was not intended to require government neutrality between religion and irreligion — the founding-era understanding permitted acknowledgment of a monotheistic God. This historical argument was adopted by the Kennedy v. Bremerton majority 17 years later.

edges.citation: Kennedy v. Bremerton School District, 597 U.S. 507, 534 (2022)
edges.notes: Kennedy treated Lemon and the endorsement test as no longer governing and stated that the Court had abandoned them. This functions as an explicit repudiation of Lemon's governing force, even if the opinion did not use the simplest formulaic 'Lemon is overruled' phrasing.

Repudiates: Kennedy explicitly abandoned the Lemon test. Changed from OVERRULES (invalid Case->DoctrinalTest) to MODIFIES(repudiates). The Case->Case OVERRULES edge (kennedy_overrules_lemon) handles the case-level overruling; this edge handles the test-level repudiation. W001 cascade acknowledged: the lemon_test is ESTABLISHED by lemon_v_kurtzman which is overruled — the test is correctly marked status:overruled with valid_until:2022-06-27.

Complicates: Kennedy replaced a settled (if criticized) analytical framework with a deliberately open-ended historical inquiry. The doctrine is now more complex and less predictable than it was under Lemon — the test's content is actively being worked out in lower courts. This is the clearest instance of the 'complicates' direction in the graph: a case that replaced certainty with uncertainty.

Kennedy created tension with prior coercion cases, especially in school settings, but did not expressly reformulate Lee's coercion standard. The decision held on its own facts that the district's response violated Free Exercise and Free Speech and that the Establishment Clause did not require suppression of the coach's prayer.
Grounds the expressive association branch. Intimate association branch is separately grounded in amendment_14_section_1 — see intimate_association_doctrine GROUNDED_IN edge.

Doctrine-level GROUNDED_IN edge for intimate association, which is grounded in the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause, not the First Amendment. This is the first use of a Doctrine-level GROUNDED_IN edge in the graph — the schema permits GROUNDED_IN from Area or Doctrine to ConstitutionalProvision.

RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Hurley v. Irish-American Gay Group (1995) is simultaneously a compelled speech case (core political speech) and a compelled association case; Dale's expressive association analysis imports compelled speech reasoning. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — core political speech protects expression; freedom of association protects the right to join with others in expressive activity. Compelled association doctrine often merges with compelled speech doctrine. SCHEMA ISSUE B: The overlap between compelled association and compelled speech (Hurley, Dale) means some cases are simultaneously in both doctrinal lines. The schema models this correctly through edges but the conceptual boundary between the two areas is genuinely porous.
NAACP applied the First Amendment through the 14th Amendment against state action — foundational incorporation application.

Expands: Hurley extended the expressive association framework to parade organizers — establishing that an event itself (not just a membership organization) can be an expressive association whose organizers have the right to control the event's message. This expanded the category of entities that can invoke expressive association protection beyond membership organizations.

Expands: Dale significantly expanded expressive association protection by (1) applying it to a large national organization; (2) giving substantial deference to the organization's characterization of its expressive purpose; and (3) treating an unarticulated anti-homosexuality stance as part of the organization's expressive mission. This expanded both the category of organizations that qualify and the deference courts give to their claims.
The Petition Clause — 'or the right of the people...to petition the Government for a redress of grievances' — is the textual source.

RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Button (1963) recognized litigation as political expression, connecting petition and speech doctrine; Brandenburg's protections for political advocacy extend to petitioning activity. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — core political speech protects expression of political views; freedom of petition protects the right to direct that expression at government. Petitioning is a specific form of political expression directed at government action.

RELATED_TO criteria: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — NAACP v. Button (1963) is simultaneously a petition case and an association case — the NAACP's litigation activity is both petitioning government and expressive association; NAACP v. Alabama (1958, association slice) and Button (petition slice) are companion cases from the same civil rights context. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — freedom of association protects the right to join together; freedom of petition protects the right to direct collective action at government. Group petitioning is the intersection.
First Amendment implicit association grounding for intimate association doctrine.

Clarifies: Dale clarified that organizational assertions of expressive burden receive near-total judicial deference — courts will not second-guess an organization's characterization of its own expressive mission. This resolved a question left open by Roberts about how much deference courts owe to organizational self-characterization.

Dale distinguished Roberts by holding that the Boy Scouts — unlike the Jaycees — is an expressive organization whose selectivity on the contested characteristic (sexual orientation) is integral to its expressive mission. The Jaycees' exclusion of women was unrelated to its expressive activities; the Boy Scouts' exclusion of gay scoutmasters was allegedly central to its expressive mission of teaching Scout values.

Narrows: Bonta added a narrow tailoring requirement to the compelled disclosure test, tightening the standard beyond the prior substantial relationship test. This narrows the government's ability to compel disclosure — government must now show not just a substantial interest but that its disclosure requirement is narrowly tailored to serve that interest.
Expands: Bonta expanded the protection for associational privacy by adding the narrow tailoring requirement — making it harder for government to justify compelled disclosure requirements.
Stub governance edge — intimate_association_doctrine is modeled as a stub pending Due Process slice.

Clarifies: California Motor Transport extended the Noerr doctrine to courts and agencies (not just legislatures), gave it full First Amendment grounding, and introduced the sham exception. This is a clarification that both expanded the doctrine's reach and defined its outer limit.

Clarifies: Guarnieri clarified that the Petition Clause does not provide greater protection than the Speech Clause for public employee claims — both require that the petition or speech address a matter of public concern. This resolved a circuit split on whether public employee petition claims had an independent standard.

The longest INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES gap in the graph — 50 years. Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) argued that the First Amendment requires imminence and likelihood of harm before speech may be punished, and that the marketplace of ideas should be trusted to reject dangerous expression. Brandenburg adopted this reasoning wholesale in 1969, overruling the bad tendency test that the Abrams majority had applied.

Holmes's Gitlow dissent (1925) reiterated and deepened the Abrams argument — requiring imminence, likelihood of harm, and deference to the marketplace of ideas. Brandenburg (1969) adopted this position 44 years later. Two Holmes dissents, two INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES edges, both converging on Brandenburg.
Gitlow was the first case to incorporate a Bill of Rights provision against the states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

condition.context: Applies in public school settings only
condition.note: Student speech doctrine is a specialized sub-doctrine of core political speech operative only in the public school context

condition.context: Applies in public school settings only
condition.note: Tinker test governs student speech in public schools

edges.notes: Narrows: Bethel carved out the first exception to Tinker's substantial-disruption test, holding that lewd, vulgar, or plainly offensive speech may be restricted in the school context without a disruption showing.
Narrows: Hazelwood carved out the second exception to Tinker — school-sponsored speech may be regulated for legitimate pedagogical concerns without showing substantial disruption.
Narrows: Morse carved out the third exception to Tinker — speech promoting illegal drug use may be restricted without a disruption showing.

Clarifies the boundary between permissible accommodation and impermissible preference in the aid/accommodation doctrine — a tax exemption that confers a pure financial benefit on religion without a corresponding burden crosses into impermissible preference.

Clarifies that exempting religious organizations from generally applicable civil regulations is permissible accommodation, not establishment — the exemption relieves a genuine regulatory burden on religious autonomy.

Texas Monthly distinguished Amos on the ground that a tax exemption conferring pure financial benefit differs from a regulatory exemption relieving a genuine burden on religious exercise. This distinction defines the accommodation/preference line.
Narrows: R.A.V. added viewpoint-neutrality requirement even within the fighting words category — government cannot selectively target fighting words based on topic or viewpoint.

Virginia v. Black distinguished R.A.V. by holding that a ban on cross burning with intent to intimidate is NOT impermissibly content-discriminatory. It falls within the R.A.V. exception permitting regulation of a subset of unprotected speech when the basis for the regulation consists entirely of the very reason the entire class of speech is unprotected (fear of violence).

Mitchell distinguished R.A.V. by holding that sentence enhancements for bias-motivated conduct target criminal acts rather than expression — the First Amendment does not prohibit evidentiary use of speech to establish criminal motive.

Narrows: Cohen limited the fighting words doctrine by requiring that challenged speech be directed at a specific individual in a face-to-face confrontation — generalized offensive expression in a public space is not fighting words.

Expands: Ferber created a new categorical exception for child pornography that operates independently of the Miller test — child pornography is unprotected even if it would not satisfy the Miller obscenity standard.
Metromedia applied Central Hudson to outdoor advertising restrictions, holding that sign ordinances cannot prefer commercial speech over noncommercial speech.
Expands: Richmond Newspapers expanded press rights by establishing a First Amendment right of access to criminal trials — a positive press right, not just a shield from compelled disclosure.

Clarifies: Tornillo established that the First Amendment prohibits government from compelling newspapers to publish content they would not otherwise publish, securing absolute editorial autonomy for the print press. This is distinct from defamation doctrine — it governs compelled speech, not liability for published speech.

Narrows: Dun & Bradstreet limited Sullivan/Gertz by holding that their First Amendment protections apply only to speech on matters of public concern — private speech receives no special constitutional protection in defamation.

Narrows: Zelman narrowed the Establishment Clause's restrictions on government aid by holding that neutral voucher programs that flow to religious schools through private choice do not constitute an establishment even if the aid directly funds religious education.

Clarifies via O'Connor concurrence: Lynch v. Donnelly produced the endorsement test (O'Connor, J., concurring) — government violates the Establishment Clause when it sends a message that religion is favored or that religious adherence is relevant to political standing.
Schenck's 'clear and present danger' test was the first articulation of the incitement framework. It governed until formally replaced by Brandenburg's imminence requirement.

Dennis applied a gravity-discounted danger test during the Cold War era — effectively approving punishment for political speech based on the seriousness of the threatened harm rather than its imminence. This approach was repudiated by Brandenburg.

Brandeis's Whitney concurrence articulated the imminence requirement and the marketplace of ideas rationale that became Brandenburg's governing test 42 years later. Along with Holmes's Abrams and Gitlow dissents, Whitney's Brandeis concurrence is one of the foundational minority opinions that became the law of the land.

Sherbert v. Verner reached a seemingly inconsistent result with Braunfeld — both involved neutral laws burdening religious exercise, but Sherbert required compelling interest justification while Braunfeld did not. Employment Division v. Smith resolved the tension by returning to the Braunfeld approach.

Clarifies: Feiner identified the outer limit of hostile audience protection — when imminent uncontrollable violence exists, police may order speaker to desist — but courts have read Feiner narrowly to avoid the heckler's veto.

Roth was the foundational case first categorically excluding obscenity from First Amendment protection and establishing the community standards/prurient interest test. Miller v. California (1973) replaced the Roth test with the SLAPS standard.

Repudiates: Miller explicitly rejected and replaced the Roth/Memoirs 'utterly without redeeming social value' standard with the three-part SLAPS test, providing a more workable definition of obscenity.

Ashcroft limited Ferber by holding that virtual child pornography not involving actual children falls outside the Ferber categorical exception — Ferber's rationale (harm to real children in production) does not apply to virtual images.

Clarifies: Mosley articulated the foundational content-neutrality principle — government has no power to restrict expression because of its message, ideas, subject matter, or content — and established that selective exemptions from content-neutral restrictions based on message violate this principle.
Clarifies: Perry formalized the three-category public forum taxonomy (traditional, designated, nonpublic) that now structures all public forum analysis.

Tornillo distinguished Red Lion by refusing to apply broadcast-style content obligations to newspapers — the scarcity rationale does not apply to print media, which is entitled to full editorial autonomy.

Clarifies: Turner established that cable television is intermediate between broadcast (Red Lion) and print (Tornillo) — cable operators receive more First Amendment protection than broadcasters. Content-neutral structural regulations (like must-carry rules) are subject to intermediate scrutiny under O'Brien, not the deferential broadcast standard. Content-based regulations on cable receive strict scrutiny.
Pap's A.M. resolved the Barnes plurality confusion by establishing that a majority adopts the secondary effects rationale for nude dancing ordinances.
Zelman extended Mueller's neutrality/private choice framework from indirect tax deductions to direct tuition vouchers flowing to religious schools.

Narrows: Gooding established that fighting words statutes must be narrowly drawn to track Chaplinsky's precise definition — broader statutes sweeping in merely offensive speech are unconstitutionally overbroad.

Expands: Lovell expanded prior restraint doctrine beyond judicial injunctions (as in Near) to encompass administrative licensing schemes, holding that granting an official unbridled discretion to permit or deny distribution of literature is a facially unconstitutional prior restraint.

Applies the categorical exclusion of libel (pre-Sullivan) to group defamation. Beauharnais relied on the historical exclusion of libelous speech from First Amendment protection, not the fighting words doctrine. R.A.V. v. City of St. Paul (1992) effectively undermined Beauharnais by prohibiting viewpoint discrimination even within unprotected categories.

R.A.V. effectively undermined Beauharnais by holding that content discrimination within unprotected categories (like fighting words) is presumptively unconstitutional — the group libel approach of Beauharnais cannot survive R.A.V.'s viewpoint-neutrality requirement.

Debs applied the Schenck clear and present danger test to uphold conviction of the Socialist Party's presidential candidate for anti-war speech — broader application than Schenck's leaflet distribution.
Frohwerk applied the Schenck clear and present danger test to uphold conviction of a newspaper editor who published anti-war articles.

Narrows: Alameda Books tightened the secondary effects doctrine by requiring actual evidentiary support linking the regulation to the targeted effects — cities cannot simply assert secondary effects without providing evidence.
Adderley laid the intellectual groundwork for the nonpublic forum category, which Perry subsequently formalized and systematized into the modern three-category public forum framework.

opinion_ref.author: Black
opinion_ref.note: Adderley majority established the nonpublic forum concept that Perry systematized

opinion_ref.author: Rehnquist
opinion_ref.note: Van Orden plurality moved away from Lemon toward history-and-tradition analysis later embraced in Kennedy

edges.notes: Van Orden's plurality moved away from Lemon and toward a history-and-tradition mode of analysis later embraced in Kennedy v. Bremerton. Along with Scalia's Lee and McCreary dissents, Van Orden is a key way station in the intellectual arc from Lemon to Kennedy.

Virginia v. Black clarified the true threats doctrine by holding that cross burning with intent to intimidate is a constitutionally proscribable true threat — articulating the 'serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence' formulation that became central to subsequent true threats analysis including Counterman.

Clarifies: Yates required that incitement prosecution prove advocacy of illegal action rather than abstract advocacy of dangerous ideas — effectively gutting Dennis without formally overruling it. The abstract doctrine/action advocacy distinction became central to Brandenburg's reformulation twelve years later.

Yates distinguished Dennis without overruling it by requiring proof of advocacy of illegal action rather than abstract doctrine — effectively rendering the Smith Act unenforceable against theoretical advocacy.

Clarifies: Reed hardened and broadened the content-based/content-neutral distinction by holding that any facial subject-matter distinction triggers strict scrutiny regardless of the government's motive — the most significant expansion of content-neutrality doctrine since Mosley (1972).
Reed extended Mosley's content-neutrality principle by making facial subject-matter distinctions categorically content-based, subject to strict scrutiny regardless of discriminatory intent.

Davis applied Buckley's expenditure protection to strike down the Millionaire's Amendment — a scheme that raised opponents' contribution limits when a self-funded candidate spent beyond a threshold. Any mechanism penalizing a candidate's decision to spend personal funds violates the First Amendment.
Expands: Davis extended Buckley's protection of candidate expenditures to cover asymmetric regulatory schemes that penalize spending decisions, not just direct expenditure caps.

Expands: Arizona Free Enterprise extended Davis's reasoning from asymmetric contribution limits to triggered public matching fund systems, completing the doctrinal foreclosure of legislative attempts to level the playing field through reactive funding schemes.

Clarifies: Stanley established that the obscenity doctrine's categorical exclusion does not extend to private possession in the home — the state's interest in regulating obscenity stops at the threshold of private possession for personal use.

Paris Adult Theatre explicitly held that Stanley's private-possession right does not extend to commercial exhibition — states may regulate obscene material in adult theaters even for consenting adults, because the privacy rationale of Stanley does not apply to commercial settings.

opinion_ref.author: Harlan
opinion_ref.note: Yates majority laid the abstract/action distinction that Brandenburg formalized

edges.notes: Yates laid the intellectual groundwork for Brandenburg by requiring proof of advocacy of illegal action rather than abstract doctrine. Brandenburg absorbed this distinction and formalized it into the modern imminence/likelihood test.
Upheld content-neutral ban on signs on public property under intermediate scrutiny, establishing aesthetic interests as a substantial government interest.
Ladue distinguished Taxpayers for Vincent by emphasizing the unique significance of the home as a forum — a total ban on residential signs forecloses a communication medium unavailable anywhere else.

Clarifies: Bolger established the three-factor test (the 'Bolger factors') for determining whether speech is commercial: (1) whether it is an advertisement, (2) whether it refers to a specific product, and (3) whether the speaker has an economic motivation.

Clarifies: Zauderer established a separate, more lenient standard for compelled commercial disclosures — requiring only reasonable relation to preventing deception — distinct from Central Hudson's intermediate scrutiny for restrictions on commercial speech. Clarifies: Zauderer created a bifurcated commercial speech standard — compelled disclosures only require reasonable relation to preventing deception (not Central Hudson's intermediate scrutiny), while restrictions on commercial speech still require Central Hudson analysis.

Narrows: Florida Bar created a limited exception to the commercial speech protection for targeted attorney direct mail established in Shapero, upholding a 30-day waiting period after accidents under Central Hudson.

Matal held that trademarks are private speech entitled to full First Amendment protection — viewpoint discrimination in trademark registration is unconstitutional regardless of the commercial context.

Narrows: City of Austin clarified and narrowed Reed v. Town of Gilbert's content-based definition — a regulation is not automatically content-based simply because one must read a sign to apply it. Only regulations requiring examination of communicative content (topic, viewpoint) are content-based.

Dred Scott (1857) predates the Fourteenth Amendment (1868) by eleven years. This edge represents the constitutional void that the Amendment was designed to fill — the Amendment's citizenship clause directly repudiated Taney's holding that Black persons could not be citizens.

Strauder was the first significant equal protection case, establishing that the Fourteenth Amendment prohibits racial discrimination in jury selection and reading the Amendment's purpose as securing full civic equality for Black citizens.

Narrows: Plessy dramatically narrowed the equal protection guarantee established in Strauder by holding that separate but equal facilities satisfied the Fourteenth Amendment — effectively gutting the Amendment's promise of civic equality for 58 years.

opinion_ref.author: Harlan
opinion_ref.note: Harlan's color-blind Constitution dissent was adopted as law in Brown 58 years later

edges.notes: Justice Harlan's Plessy dissent — arguing that the Constitution is color-blind and permits no caste distinctions — was vindicated and effectively adopted in Brown v. Board of Education 58 years later.

Brown overruled Plessy in the field of public education — holding that separate educational facilities are inherently unequal. The separate but equal doctrine was subsequently dismantled in other contexts. Coded as effective rather than explicit because Brown did not use the word overrule.

Expands: Brown dramatically expanded the equal protection guarantee by holding that separate educational facilities are inherently unequal and abolishing the separate but equal doctrine in public education.

Expands: Bolling extended equal protection principles to the federal government via the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause — making the federal government subject to the same non-discrimination requirements as states under the Fourteenth Amendment.
Bolling grounded federal equal protection in the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause rather than the Fourteenth Amendment, which applies only to states.

Korematsu was the first case to apply the strict scrutiny label to racial classifications — articulating that legal restrictions curtailing the rights of a single racial group are immediately suspect and subject to the most rigid scrutiny.

Washington v. Davis established the foundational rule that facially neutral laws require proof of discriminatory intent — not merely disparate impact — to trigger strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause.

Clarifies: Arlington Heights elaborated the Washington v. Davis discriminatory intent requirement with a multi-factor framework for proving intent from circumstantial evidence — impact, historical background, sequence of events, procedural departures, and legislative history.

McCleskey applied Washington v. Davis to hold that statistical evidence of systemic racial disparities in capital sentencing is insufficient to prove discriminatory intent in an individual defendant's case.

Clarifies: Adarand settled that strict scrutiny applies to all racial classifications regardless of purpose or benefited group — establishing the consistency and congruence principles that govern affirmative action analysis.

Grutter applied strict scrutiny to uphold race-conscious holistic admissions, establishing student body diversity as a compelling interest — the major exception to the near-fatal application of strict scrutiny to racial classifications.

opinion_ref.author: Thomas
opinion_ref.note: Thomas's Grutter dissent arguing diversity is not a compelling interest was adopted as law in SFFA 20 years later

edges.notes: Thomas's Grutter dissent — arguing that diversity is not a compelling interest and that all racial preferences are unconstitutional — was vindicated and effectively adopted by the Roberts Court majority in SFFA v. Harvard 20 years later.

Narrows: SFFA dramatically narrowed the affirmative action doctrine by eliminating student body diversity as a compelling interest and effectively ending race-conscious admissions in higher education.

Clarifies: Reed v. Reed was the breakthrough case invalidating a sex-based classification, opening the path toward heightened scrutiny for sex, but the quasi-suspect classification doctrine in its modern form was established in Craig v. Boren (1976).

Clarifies: Frontiero's plurality would have made sex a suspect classification requiring strict scrutiny — the high-water mark of that effort — but fell one vote short of a majority, leaving the standard unsettled until Craig v. Boren.

Craig v. Boren formally established intermediate scrutiny as the constitutional standard for sex-based classifications — settling the standard that Frontiero's plurality had failed to establish as a majority holding.
Clarifies: Craig formally established sex as a quasi-suspect classification subject to intermediate scrutiny, resolving the uncertainty left by Frontiero's fractured plurality.

Complicates: VMI added the exceedingly persuasive justification requirement to intermediate scrutiny for sex classifications — a standard that approaches but does not formally reach strict scrutiny, making the tier harder to define precisely.
Beazer illustrates rational basis review at its most deferential — sustaining a categorical employment exclusion based on conceivable safety and efficiency rationales despite evidence of overbreadth.

Clarifies: Moreno established that bare congressional desire to harm a politically unpopular group cannot constitute a legitimate government interest — introducing the animus principle into rational basis review that Cleburne and Romer would later develop.

Clarifies: Cleburne applied rational basis with unusual rigor — effectively rational basis with bite — striking a zoning denial based on irrational prejudice against the disabled while explicitly declining to make disability a quasi-suspect classification.

Clarifies: Romer applied Moreno's animus principle to sexual orientation, completing the Moreno-Cleburne-Romer rational basis with bite trilogy and establishing that bare desire to harm gay and lesbian persons is not a legitimate government interest.
Romer is the foundational constitutional protection for sexual orientation under equal protection, leading directly to Lawrence v. Texas (2003) and Obergefell v. Hodges (2015).

Harper established voting as a fundamental right whose unequal distribution triggers strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause — the foundational case for the fundamental rights strand of equal protection.
Connection captured through fundamental_rights_ep_doctrine rather than direct strict scrutiny test application.
Reynolds applied the fundamental right to vote to require population-based apportionment of both chambers of state legislatures — establishing one person, one vote as a constitutional requirement.
Connection captured through fundamental_rights_ep_doctrine rather than direct strict scrutiny test application.
Expands: Shapiro extended the fundamental rights EP doctrine to the right to travel interstate, establishing that welfare residency requirements penalizing travel trigger strict scrutiny.

Narrows: Rodriguez established the major limit on the fundamental rights EP doctrine — holding that education is not a fundamental right and wealth is not a suspect classification, foreclosing federal equal protection challenges to school funding disparities.

opinion_ref.author: O'Connor
opinion_ref.note: O'Connor's Romer equal protection reasoning was applied in Lawrence's equal protection path

edges.notes: Romer's animus-based equal protection analysis intellectually preceded Lawrence — O'Connor's Lawrence concurrence applied Romer's equal protection reasoning directly to the Texas sodomy statute.

Lawrence was decided on due process grounds but O'Connor's concurrence identified an equal protection path — the Texas statute's application only to same-sex couples raised equal protection concerns under Romer's animus rationale.

Expands: Obergefell extended the fundamental right to marry to same-sex couples under both the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses — the culmination of the Romer-Lawrence-Obergefell constitutional arc.

Obergefell fused due process and equal protection — it did not present as a clean strict scrutiny equal protection case. The RELATED_TO edge captures the connection without overstating doctrinal formalism.

opinion_ref.author: Warren
opinion_ref.note: Loving's recognition of marriage as a fundamental right was directly applied in Obergefell (majority opinion — plurality used as schema approximation for INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES)

edges.notes: Loving v. Virginia's recognition of marriage as a fundamental right and its rejection of race-based marriage restrictions as unconstitutional were directly applied in Obergefell — Kennedy's majority opinion cited Loving as the foundational precedent for the right to marry regardless of identity.

opinion_ref.author: Kennedy
opinion_ref.note: Romer's equal protection protection for gay persons preceded Obergefell's extension of marriage rights (majority opinion — plurality used as schema approximation for INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES)

edges.notes: Romer established that gay and lesbian persons are entitled to constitutional equal protection — the foundational premise that Obergefell extended to the right to marry.

opinion_ref.author: Harlan
opinion_ref.note: Harlan's color-blind Constitution dissent was invoked by Roberts in SFFA to justify colorblind equal protection

edges.notes: Justice Harlan's Plessy dissent — arguing the Constitution is color-blind and permits no caste distinctions — was explicitly invoked by Chief Justice Roberts in SFFA v. Harvard to justify the colorblind equal protection principle that bars race-conscious admissions.
Companion cases decided the same day holding state and federal school segregation unconstitutional under the Fourteenth and Fifth Amendments respectively.

Rice applied strict scrutiny to strike an ancestry-based voting restriction, holding that ancestry classifications that function as racial proxies are subject to the same constitutional scrutiny as explicit racial classifications.
Bandemer held partisan gerrymandering claims justiciable under equal protection — later overruled on justiciability by Rucho v. Common Cause (2019).

Complicates: Vieth's plurality would have overruled Bandemer as non-justiciable, but Kennedy's concurrence refused — leaving partisan gerrymandering's justiciability under equal protection nominally intact but practically undermined until Rucho v. Common Cause (2019).
Bush v. Gore applied the fundamental right to vote to require uniform standards in manual recounts — but expressly limited the holding to its unique facts, making its precedential value minimal.

Expands: Griffin established the access-to-courts strand of fundamental rights equal protection, holding that states cannot condition meaningful appellate review on ability to pay for a trial transcript.
Expands: Douglas extended Griffin's access-to-courts doctrine from the right to a trial transcript to the right to appointed counsel on first appeal as of right.
Dandridge established that welfare benefit classifications receive only rational basis review, rejecting poverty as a suspect classification or welfare as a fundamental right.
Rodriguez applied Dandridge's rational basis framework to reject education as a fundamental right and wealth as a suspect classification.
Clarifies: Saenz grounded the right to travel in the Privileges or Immunities Clause rather than equal protection, clarifying the constitutional basis for the travel right established in Shapiro.

Saenz distinguished Shapiro by grounding the right of new residents to equal welfare benefits in the Privileges or Immunities Clause rather than equal protection — a significant doctrinal shift in the constitutional basis for the travel right.

Saenz grounded the right of newly arrived citizens in the Privileges or Immunities Clause of Section 1 of the Fourteenth Amendment — a significant revival of that clause after its dormancy since the Slaughterhouse Cases (1873). SCHEMA NOTE: Add saenz_interprets_privileges_immunities edge when amendment_14_privileges_immunities clause node is added.
Bowers applied rational basis to uphold Georgia's sodomy law as applied to homosexual conduct, finding the longstanding criminalization of such conduct a sufficient rational basis.

Lawrence explicitly overruled Bowers v. Hardwick, stating it was wrong when decided and that its reasoning should be and now is rejected. The decision was grounded in substantive due process liberty rather than equal protection.

opinion_ref.author: Blackmun
opinion_ref.note: Blackmun's Bowers dissent arguing the case was about the fundamental right to be left alone was adopted in Lawrence

edges.notes: Blackmun's Bowers dissent — reframing the issue as being about the fundamental right to be left alone rather than a 'right to engage in homosexual sodomy' — was effectively adopted by Kennedy's Lawrence majority, which similarly reframed the question around liberty rather than the specific conduct.
The Due Process Clause is one of five clauses of the Fifth Amendment.
Dobbs explicitly overrules Roe v. Wade, holding that the Constitution does not confer a right to abortion.
Dobbs explicitly overrules Planned Parenthood v. Casey, which had reaffirmed Roe's essential holding.
Dobbs applies the substantive due process doctrine to determine whether abortion is a protected liberty interest, concluding that it is not deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition.
Dobbs applies stare decisis principles to justify overruling Roe and Casey, identifying five factors that weigh in favor of overruling.
Dobbs holds that rational-basis review is the appropriate standard for evaluating state abortion regulations, as abortion is not a fundamental constitutional right.
Obergefell explicitly overrules Baker v. Nelson, holding that the exclusion of same-sex couples from marriage does present a substantial federal question.
Obergefell establishes that the fundamental right to marry extends to same-sex couples under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Obergefell establishes that state laws denying same-sex couples the right to marry violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Substantive due process is grounded in the Due Process Clause of Section 1 of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Griswold established the constitutional doctrine of marital privacy as a fundamental right protected by the Bill of Rights.

Griswold invokes the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause, particularly through Harlan's concurrence which grounds the right to marital privacy in substantive due process rather than the Bill of Rights penumbra.

Lawrence clarifies and expands the substantive due process liberty doctrine by explicitly recognizing that liberty protects intimate conduct between consenting adults and extends beyond spatial bounds to include autonomy of self.
Lawrence establishes the doctrine that private, consensual sexual conduct between adults is protected from criminal prohibition under the Due Process Clause.
Cruzan established the doctrine that a competent person has a liberty interest under the Due Process Clause in refusing unwanted medical treatment.

Cruzan established the clear and convincing evidence standard as a constitutionally permissible procedural safeguard for decisions regarding withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment for incompetent persons.
Meyer applied the substantive due process liberty doctrine to protect parental rights and occupational freedom from arbitrary state interference.
Meyer established the substantive due process rationality test requiring that laws interfering with liberty have a reasonable relation to a legitimate state purpose.
Meyer established that parental rights to direct children's education, including the choice of subjects and instructors, are fundamental liberties protected by the Fourteenth Amendment.

Pierce applied and reinforced Meyer's doctrine of parental rights in education, holding that Oregon's compulsory public school attendance law unconstitutionally interfered with parents' fundamental right to direct their children's education.
Lochner v. New York is the foundational case establishing the doctrine of liberty of contract as a fundamental right protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause.
Lochner v. New York establishes the test for determining whether a state regulation is a valid exercise of police power or an unconstitutional interference with liberty of contract.
Lochner applies the doctrine of police power while establishing that this power has constitutional limits when it interferes with liberty of contract.
Palko established the ordered liberty doctrine as the framework for determining which Bill of Rights protections apply to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment.
Palko established the selective incorporation doctrine, rejecting total incorporation and adopting a case-by-case approach based on fundamental fairness and ordered liberty.
Palko established the ordered liberty test as the operative standard for determining incorporation of Bill of Rights protections into the Fourteenth Amendment.
Skinner establishes procreation and marriage as fundamental rights requiring constitutional protection under the Fourteenth Amendment.

Loving v. Virginia established that racial classifications in state statutes, particularly criminal statutes, are subject to the most rigid scrutiny and can only be upheld if necessary to accomplish a permissible state objective independent of invidious racial discrimination.

Loving v. Virginia established the freedom to marry as a fundamental right protected by the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, holding that this right cannot be restricted by invidious racial discriminations.
Moore established that family living arrangements involving blood relatives are protected liberty interests under substantive due process.
Skinner applied strict scrutiny to Oklahoma's sterilization act, holding that legislation affecting fundamental rights like procreation requires the most rigid scrutiny.
Troxel v. Granville established the parental fundamental right doctrine as a substantive due process protection under the Fourteenth Amendment.
Troxel v. Granville established the parental right protection test for evaluating state interference with parental decisions concerning child care, custody, and control.

Carolene Products applied rational basis review to uphold the Filled Milk Act, establishing that regulatory legislation affecting ordinary commercial transactions is presumed constitutional if it rests upon some rational basis.
Washington v. Davis established that discriminatory purpose, not merely disproportionate impact, is required to establish an equal protection violation under the Fifth Amendment.

Benton explicitly overruled Palko v. Connecticut, holding that the Double Jeopardy Clause applies to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment, contrary to Palko's holding that only a 'watered-down' version applied.
Benton established the doctrine that the Double Jeopardy Clause of the Fifth Amendment applies to the States through the Fourteenth Amendment.

Carolene Products established the rational basis test for equal protection review of economic and social legislation, including the famous Footnote 4 suggesting heightened scrutiny for legislation affecting discrete and insular minorities.

West Coast Hotel narrowed the Lochner-era liberty of contract doctrine, holding that liberty of contract is subject to reasonable state regulation in the interest of community health, safety, morals, and welfare. This effectively ended the Lochner era.
Explicitly overruled by syllabus point 6.
Criminal procedure rights apply to the states through selective incorporation via the Fourteenth Amendment.
Weeks v. United States established the exclusionary rule for federal prosecutions, holding that evidence obtained in violation of the Fourth Amendment must be excluded from trial.
The Court applied Fourth Amendment protections to the warrantless search and seizure of the defendant's home and private papers by a federal marshal.
Wolf applies the selective incorporation framework from Palko, holding that the Fourth Amendment's core protection is incorporated but the exclusionary rule is not required.
Wolf establishes that the Fourth Amendment's core protection against arbitrary police intrusion is a fundamental right incorporated into the Fourteenth Amendment.
Wolf establishes that states have flexibility in choosing remedies for Fourth Amendment violations, including alternatives to the exclusionary rule such as damages actions and police discipline.
Mapp explicitly overrules Wolf's holding that the exclusionary rule is not an essential ingredient of the Fourth Amendment right enforceable against the states.
Mapp established that the exclusionary rule is a constitutionally mandated doctrine applicable to state prosecutions.
Mapp applied and extended the Fourth Amendment right to privacy to state prosecutions through the Fourteenth Amendment.
Gideon established that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel applies to state criminal prosecutions through the Fourteenth Amendment.
Escobedo v. Illinois established the multi-factor test for determining when the right to counsel attaches at the pretrial interrogation stage.
Escobedo v. Illinois established that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel extends to critical pretrial stages, including police interrogation.
Escobedo v. Illinois established the functional distinction between investigatory and accusatory stages, rejecting a purely formal test based on indictment.
Miranda v. Arizona established the Miranda Doctrine as a constitutional requirement for custodial interrogation.
Miranda v. Arizona established the Miranda Warnings Test as the operative standard for determining the admissibility of statements obtained during custodial interrogation.
Katz explicitly rejects the trespass doctrine of Olmstead, holding that Fourth Amendment protection does not depend on physical intrusion into a protected area.
Katz explicitly rejects Goldman's holding that surveillance without physical penetration falls outside Fourth Amendment protection.
Katz establishes the reasonable expectation of privacy doctrine as the framework for determining Fourth Amendment protection, replacing the property-based trespass doctrine.
Terry v. Ohio established the constitutional framework for stop-and-frisk searches under the Fourth Amendment.
Terry v. Ohio established the two-part test for evaluating the constitutionality of stops and frisks.
Dickerson affirms and applies the Miranda doctrine, holding that it is a constitutional rule that governs admissibility in both state and federal courts.
Illinois v. Gates established the totality of the circumstances test as the standard for evaluating probable cause from informant tips.
Illinois v. Gates established the modern doctrine governing probable cause determinations based on informant tips, replacing the rigid Aguilar-Spinelli framework.

Betts applied the right to counsel doctrine in a limited, circumstance-dependent form, holding that the right does not require appointment of counsel in all state felony cases. Overruled by Gideon v. Wainwright.
Massiah established the doctrine that the Sixth Amendment protects a defendant's right to counsel after indictment against government-elicited incriminating statements.
Strickland established the foundational two-prong test for ineffective assistance of counsel claims.
Strickland articulated the substantive doctrine that the Sixth Amendment guarantees effective assistance of counsel as a fundamental right.
Chimel established the scope limitation for searches incident to arrest.
United States v. Leon established the good-faith exception to the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule.
United States v. Leon established the good-faith reliance on warrant test for determining admissibility of evidence obtained pursuant to an invalid warrant.

United States v. Leon narrowed the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule by creating an exception for evidence obtained by officers acting in reasonable reliance on a facially valid warrant issued by a detached and neutral magistrate.
Carroll v. United States established the automobile exception to the Fourth Amendment warrant requirement.
Carroll v. United States established the two-prong test for warrantless automobile searches: probable cause and impracticability of obtaining a warrant.

Carroll v. United States applied and synthesized the probable cause standard from prior cases including Stacey v. Emery and Commonwealth v. Carey, defining it as facts and circumstances warranting a man of prudence and caution in believing an offense has been committed.
Kyllo established the sense-enhancing technology test as a framework for determining when use of technology constitutes a Fourth Amendment search.
Kyllo established the doctrine that the Fourth Amendment protects the home against warrantless searches using sense-enhancing technology not in general public use.
Carpenter established that digital location information from cell-site records receives Fourth Amendment protection despite the third-party doctrine.

Whren established the principle that probable cause to believe a traffic violation has occurred is sufficient to justify a traffic stop under the Fourth Amendment, regardless of the officer's subjective intent.
Riley v. California establishes the doctrine that warrantless searches of digital information on cell phones are generally unreasonable under the Fourth Amendment.
Riley v. California establishes the cell phone search warrant requirement test as the governing standard for warrantless searches of digital information on cell phones seized incident to arrest.
Riley narrows the search incident to arrest exception by holding it does not extend to digital information on cell phones seized from arrestees.

Riley applies and reinforces the Fourth Amendment warrant requirement, holding that warrants are generally required before searching digital information on cell phones and emphasizing that the warrant requirement is an important component of Fourth Amendment jurisprudence.

Brewer expanded the right to counsel doctrine by holding that deliberately eliciting incriminating statements outside formal interrogation, after the Sixth Amendment right has attached, also violates the right to counsel.
Wade establishes the doctrine that the Sixth Amendment guarantees the right to counsel at post-indictment lineups.
Wade establishes the independent source test for determining whether courtroom identifications may be admitted despite a Sixth Amendment violation at a lineup.

Gant narrowed the scope of New York v. Belton by requiring that the arrestee be unsecured and able to access the vehicle interior, or that evidence of the specific offense be reasonably believed to be in the vehicle.
Arizona v. Gant established the two-prong disjunctive test for vehicle searches incident to arrest.
Gant applied and clarified the Chimel immediate control doctrine in the vehicle context.
The Court applied the consent search doctrine to police encounters on buses.
The Court applied the reasonable person refusal standard to determine whether police encounters on buses constitute Fourth Amendment seizures.
Maryland v. Buie established the protective sweep doctrine as a Fourth Amendment principle.
Maryland v. Buie established the standard for evaluating the justification required for warrantless protective sweeps incident to arrest.
Florida v. J.L. establishes the doctrine that anonymous tips require specific indicia of reliability to justify investigatory stops and frisks.
Berkemer applies Miranda doctrine by clarifying its scope: Miranda does not apply to routine traffic stops.
Berkemer clarifies that routine traffic stops do not constitute custodial interrogation, even though the motorist is detained and questioned.
Apprendi v. New Jersey establishes the constitutional requirement that sentence-enhancing facts must be proven to a jury beyond a reasonable doubt.
Salinas established the doctrine that express invocation of the Fifth Amendment privilege is required in precustodial interrogation.
The case applies and extends the search incident to arrest doctrine to automobile passenger compartments.
Blakely v. Washington established the foundational rule that judicial fact-finding at sentencing must comply with Sixth Amendment jury trial protections.
The case applies and clarifies the scope of the Sixth Amendment jury trial right in the sentencing context.
Blakely applied the Apprendi rule to state sentencing guidelines.
Booker established that the Federal Sentencing Guidelines are advisory, not mandatory.
Booker applied the Blakely rule to federal sentencing guidelines, finding the mandatory Guidelines violated the Sixth Amendment.
Batson v. Kentucky established the three-step test for evaluating peremptory challenges based on race.
Batson v. Kentucky established the doctrine that peremptory challenges cannot be exercised on the basis of race under the Equal Protection Clause.
Powers expanded Batson by holding that defendants have standing to raise Batson challenges regardless of whether they share the same race as the excluded jurors.
J.E.B. established that gender-based peremptory challenges violate the Equal Protection Clause.
J.E.B. expanded the Batson framework from race to gender-based peremptory challenges.
Brady v. Maryland established the foundational doctrine requiring prosecutors to disclose material exculpatory evidence to the defense.
Giglio expanded Brady by holding that the duty to disclose extends to impeachment evidence, including promises of leniency made to government witnesses.
Manson v. Brathwaite established the five-factor totality of circumstances test for eyewitness identification reliability.
Neil v. Biggers applied the totality of circumstances approach to eyewitness identification — Manson later formalized this into the five-factor test.
Barker v. Wingo established the first comprehensive standard for evaluating speedy trial claims, filling a doctrinal gap left by Klopfer v. North Carolina.
Barker applies and operationalizes the speedy trial right established as fundamental in Klopfer v. North Carolina (1967).
McCollum expanded Batson by holding that the Equal Protection Clause prohibits criminal defendants, not just prosecutors, from using race-based peremptory challenges.
McCollum applied the equal protection prohibition on race-based peremptory challenges to criminal defendants.
Chavez addressed whether coercive interrogation violates substantive due process liberty interests under the Fourteenth Amendment.
The Court applied and clarified the Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination, holding that the right is implicated only when statements are used in a criminal prosecution.
Gall established the abuse-of-discretion standard for appellate review of all sentences.
Gall applies and clarifies the doctrine of appellate review of sentencing decisions.
Gall applied the advisory Guidelines framework from Booker, establishing the abuse-of-discretion standard for appellate review of all sentences.
Arizona v. Hicks clarified the plain view doctrine by establishing that probable cause is required for seizure, rejecting the application of the doctrine based on mere suspicion.
Minnesota v. Dickerson established the plain feel doctrine as a Fourth Amendment limitation on seizure of contraband during patdown searches.
Minnesota v. Dickerson established the plain feel test as the operative standard for determining whether contraband may be seized during a protective patdown search.
Dickerson applied the Terry stop and frisk framework, extending it to tactile discovery of contraband while limiting seizure to cases where incriminating character is immediately apparent.
Goldberg v. Kelly established the doctrine requiring procedural due process protections prior to welfare termination.

The Court establishes the foundational principle that a constitutionally protected property interest in employment requires a legitimate claim of entitlement under state law or contract, not merely the fact of employment.
The Court identifies liberty interests as a separate category of Fourteenth Amendment protection, distinct from property interests, though the full scope is not resolved in this excerpt.
Mathews v. Eldridge establishes the foundational balancing test for procedural due process analysis.
Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill established the pretermination due process test for public employees with property interests in continued employment.
The case establishes that a property interest in continued employment arises when state law limits termination to for-cause dismissals.
The case establishes the doctrine that pretermination due process is required before termination of public employees with property interests in continued employment.
Hamdi established the doctrine that enemy combatants who are U.S. citizens detained domestically have due process rights.
Hamdi established the specific test for evaluating enemy combatant detention of U.S. citizens.
Mathews refined the procedural due process framework established in Goldberg by introducing the balancing test rather than requiring pre-termination hearings in all cases.
Hamdi applied the Mathews balancing test to determine the process due to a citizen detained as an enemy combatant.
McCulloch v. Maryland established the doctrine of implied powers as a central principle of federal constitutional law.
McCulloch v. Maryland established that states cannot tax federal instrumentalities.
Gibbons v. Ogden establishes the doctrine that state laws granting exclusive navigation rights conflict with and are superseded by federal commerce power.
Wickard established the aggregation principle for Commerce Clause analysis, permitting federal regulation of intrastate activity with substantial aggregate effects on interstate commerce.
The Court upheld the constitutionality of Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 as applied to restaurants.
Katzenbach applied the Wickard aggregation principle to uphold Title II of the Civil Rights Act as applied to restaurants serving interstate commerce.
Lopez established that the Commerce Clause has judicially enforceable limits by striking down a federal statute as exceeding Congress's commerce power.
Lopez narrowed the Wickard aggregation doctrine by holding that non-economic activity cannot be aggregated to satisfy the Commerce Clause.
Morrison applies Commerce Clause doctrine to determine that Congress lacked authority to regulate gender-motivated violence as non-economic activity.
Morrison applies Section 5 doctrine to determine that Congress lacked authority to enact the civil remedy under the Fourteenth Amendment.
Morrison applied Lopez's Commerce Clause limits to strike down VAWA's civil remedy.
New York v. United States established the anti-commandeering doctrine as a constitutional limit on federal power.
The Court applied Tenth Amendment principles to strike down provisions compelling States to take title to radioactive waste.
Printz applied and extended the anti-commandeering doctrine to executive officials.
Printz established the anti-commandeering doctrine as a constitutional limit on federal power to compel state and local officials to administer federal programs.
The case addresses the constitutionality of the preclearance requirement under Section 5.
The syllabus notes that Section 2 is not at issue in this case but remains valid.
South Dakota v. Dole established the test for analyzing whether Congress may condition the receipt of federal funds on a state's adoption of specified policies.
South Dakota v. Dole applies the spending power doctrine to uphold Congress's authority to condition federal highway funds on state adoption of a minimum drinking age.

South Dakota v. Dole clarifies that the Twenty-first Amendment does not limit Congress's spending power and does not confer on states the power to permit sales that Congress seeks to prohibit through conditional spending.
Herring narrowed the exclusionary rule by holding suppression is not automatic for Fourth Amendment violations caused by isolated police negligence.
Herring established the framework for applying the exclusionary rule based on police culpability and deterrent effect.
Herring applies Fourth Amendment doctrine in the context of arrests based on mistaken warrant information.
Hudson held that knock-and-announce violations do not automatically trigger exclusionary rule suppression.
Hudson applies the knock-and-announce rule established in Wilson v. Arkansas to determine the remedy for violation.

Court applies the attenuation doctrine to determine that discovery of a valid arrest warrant sufficiently attenuates the connection between an unconstitutional investigatory stop and evidence seized incident to arrest.
Court applies the exclusionary rule framework and its exceptions to determine admissibility of evidence obtained following an unconstitutional stop.
Court assumes without deciding that the officer's investigatory stop violated the Fourth Amendment by lacking reasonable suspicion.

Court applies and clarifies the Brown v. Illinois three-factor attenuation test, establishing that the test applies to intervening circumstances beyond defendant's independent acts, including discovery of valid arrest warrants.
Jones established that physical intrusion on an effect constitutes a search under the Fourth Amendment, reviving the trespass-based approach to Fourth Amendment analysis.
Jones applied the Katz reasonable expectation of privacy framework alongside the trespass doctrine.
McNeely applied the Fourth Amendment warrant requirement to blood testing in DWI investigations.
McNeely clarifies that natural dissipation of blood alcohol does not automatically satisfy the exigency exception; case-by-case analysis required.
Glover applied the Terry reasonable suspicion standard to investigatory traffic stops based on vehicle registration information.
Strieff references the independent source doctrine as one of the three exceptions to the exclusionary rule.
Strieff references the inevitable discovery doctrine as one of the three exceptions to the exclusionary rule.
Penn Central applies the Takings Clause doctrine to historic preservation restrictions.
Penn Central established the constitutional framework permitting historic preservation restrictions without automatic compensation requirements.
Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council established the total economic deprivation test for takings clause analysis.
Lucas v. South Carolina Coastal Council established the doctrine that total economic deprivation constitutes a taking requiring just compensation.
Lucas applied and extended Penn Central by establishing a categorical rule for total economic deprivation.
Kelo applies and interprets the takings clause doctrine by establishing that economic development qualifies as public use.
Kelo expanded the public use requirement by holding that economic development constitutes a public purpose.
Loretto established the doctrine that permanent physical occupations authorized by government constitute takings requiring just compensation.
The Court established the doctrine that presidential power is constrained by separation of powers and cannot encompass legislative functions like property seizure without congressional authorization.
Jackson's concurrence established the three-category framework for analyzing presidential power.
Morrison applied the Appointments Clause doctrine to validate the appointment mechanism for independent counsel.
Morrison applied Article III doctrine to uphold the special court's powers.
Chadha established that legislative vetoes violate bicameralism and presentment requirements.
Clinton applied Chadha's presentment analysis to strike down the Line Item Veto Act.
Pike v. Bruce Church, Inc. established the balancing test for evaluating state laws that burden interstate commerce.
Pike applies the dormant Commerce Clause doctrine to evaluate the constitutionality of the Arizona Fruit and Vegetable Standardization Act.
Philadelphia v. New Jersey established the principle that state laws facially discriminating against interstate commerce violate the Commerce Clause.
Philadelphia applied dormant Commerce Clause doctrine to invalidate discriminatory waste import ban.
Granholm v. Heald established the principle that the 21st Amendment does not authorize state economic discrimination in violation of the dormant Commerce Clause.
The Court applied and reaffirmed the doctrine of state sovereign immunity under the Eleventh Amendment.
The Court established that the Indian Commerce Clause does not grant Congress power to abrogate state sovereign immunity.
Alden extended state sovereign immunity to state courts, building on Seminole Tribe's federal court holding.
The Court established the principle that discriminatory purpose, not merely discriminatory effect, is required for an equal protection violation.
The Court established a multi-factor test for determining discriminatory purpose in equal protection cases.
Frontiero established that sex-based classifications violate equal protection doctrine.
Craig v. Boren establishes the intermediate scrutiny standard for gender-based statutory classifications under equal protection.
Craig v. Boren applies and develops the doctrine governing gender-based classifications under equal protection.
Tennessee v. Lane established the doctrine that Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity under the Fourteenth Amendment when legislation is a reasonable prophylactic measure.
Tennessee v. Lane recognized access to courts as a fundamental right protected by the Due Process Clause.
Tennessee v. Lane articulated the congruence and proportionality test for evaluating whether Congress has validly abrogated state sovereign immunity.
Tennessee v. Lane applied Section 5 authority to uphold ADA Title II as applied to court access.
The case applied and reinforced the doctrine that Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity through Section 5 legislation addressing documented constitutional violations.
The case applied the doctrine that Congress may abrogate Eleventh Amendment immunity when it clearly states its intent and acts within constitutional authority.
The case applied equal protection principles regarding gender-based discrimination in the administration of leave benefits to justify Section 5 prophylactic legislation.
Saenz v. Roe established the right to travel as a fundamental right protected by the Fourteenth Amendment with three distinct components.
Zivotofsky v. Kerry established the doctrine of exclusive presidential recognition power derived from Article II.
The Court applied separation of powers principles to allocate foreign affairs powers, distinguishing between Executive passport regulation and Congressional naturalization powers.
The Court applied rational basis review to uphold Oklahoma's licensing and practice restrictions for optometrists and optical services.
Troxel v. Granville established the parental right interference test to evaluate whether state action violates a parent's fundamental right to direct the care, custody, and control of children.
Snyder v. Phelps established the doctrine that First Amendment protections extend to funeral protests.
Arlington Heights built on Washington v. Davis by elaborating the multi-factor test for proving discriminatory purpose.
Craig built on Frontiero's fractured plurality to establish intermediate scrutiny as the majority standard for sex classifications.
Manson v. Brathwaite refined the Neil totality test into the explicit five-factor reliability framework.
Manson formalized Neil's totality approach into five specific reliability factors.
First English established the doctrine that compensation for regulatory takings must be available for the interim period before final determination.
Nollan established the exaction test as the governing standard for permit conditions.
Nollan established the doctrine that government exactions on development permits are subject to takings scrutiny.
Dolan v. City of Tigard establishes the two-part exactions test requiring essential nexus and rough proportionality.

Dolan v. City of Tigard refines and establishes the takings exactions doctrine by resolving the question left open in Nollan regarding the degree of connection required between exactions and development impacts.
Dolan added rough proportionality as a second requirement to Nollan's nexus test.
Cedar Point distinguished physical takings from regulatory takings under Penn Central.
Cedar Point Nursery clarified and reinforced the per se physical takings doctrine by holding that appropriation of the right to exclude constitutes a physical taking.

Cedar Point Nursery established that the right to exclude third parties from private property is a fundamental attribute of property ownership, and appropriating this right constitutes a physical taking.
Murr v. Wisconsin established the parcel definition test for regulatory takings analysis.
Murr applies and clarifies the regulatory takings doctrine by establishing the proper method for defining the relevant property parcel.

Seila Law clarifies and narrows the presidential removal power doctrine by establishing that Congress cannot restrict removal of single-director agencies with substantial executive power, even if such restrictions might serve independence interests.
Seila Law establishes the principle that the Constitution's structure scrupulously avoids concentrating substantial executive power in single individuals other than the President.
Seila Law establishes the test identifying the two narrow exceptions to the presidential removal power restriction.

edges.condition_note: An agency structure must first be evaluated under the general rule that Congress cannot restrict presidential removal power. Only if the structure falls within one of the two exceptions identified by the Seila test can Congress restrict removal; otherwise, the restriction violates separation of powers.

edges.notes: Seila Law applied Youngstown Category 3 analysis — CFPB structure impermissibly restricted presidential removal power.
The Court applied the Appointments Clause doctrine to uphold the appointment of PCAOB members by the SEC as head of a department.
Collins applied removal power doctrine to strike down FHFA single-director removal restriction.
Hunt establishes the test for evaluating facially neutral state regulations with discriminatory effects on interstate commerce.
The Court established that flow control ordinances violating the dormant Commerce Clause by excluding out-of-state competitors from local waste processing markets.
The case clarifies and reinforces the nondiscrimination principle of the dormant Commerce Clause in the context of Twenty-First Amendment alcohol regulation.
The case articulates the test for evaluating state laws that discriminate against nonresident economic actors under the dormant Commerce Clause.
The case considers whether the market participant doctrine applies to state bond issuance and taxation, ultimately holding that bond proceeds are a quintessentially public function.
Connick v. Myers applied the public employee speech doctrine established in Pickering v. Board of Education.
Connick v. Myers refined and clarified the Pickering balancing test by establishing that speech on internal office matters does not constitute speech on matters of public concern.
The Court established that special taxes on press materials that disproportionately burden a small number of newspapers violate the First Amendment freedom of the press.
Boy Scouts of America v. Dale established the modern framework for expressive association doctrine in the context of state public accommodations laws.
The Court established a two-prong test for determining when state public accommodations laws violate the First Amendment right of expressive association.
Freedom of expressive association is grounded in First Amendment protections for freedom of association and freedom from compelled speech.
Feeney applied discriminatory intent doctrine to facially neutral veterans preference.
VMI tightened Craig's intermediate scrutiny formulation to 'exceedingly persuasive justification'.
Nguyen applies gender-based classification equal protection doctrine to evaluate citizenship statute.

The Court applied rational basis review to the city's zoning ordinance and found it failed because it was based on irrational prejudice rather than a rational relationship to legitimate governmental interests.
Case involved challenge to local zoning decision under RFRA, implicating free exercise of religion.
Case established the proportionality and congruence requirement for Section 5 enforcement legislation.
Church of the Lukumi Babalu Aye established the two-prong test for Free Exercise Clause validity: permissible object and general applicability.
Reaffirmed the fundamental nonpersecution principle of the First Amendment as a core Free Exercise protection.
Established the principle that secular ends must be pursued uniformly with respect to all similarly situated conduct, not selectively with respect to religiously motivated conduct.
Fulton applied the general applicability doctrine from Lukumi to strike down Philadelphia's conditional contract policy.
Town of Greece established the legislative prayer doctrine permitting sectarian prayer in legislative settings.
City of Boerne v. Flores held RFRA unconstitutional as applied to states.
Pickering established the foundational doctrine protecting public employee speech on matters of public concern.
Wooley v. Maynard established the constitutional right to refuse to display or endorse ideological messages on private property.
Hurley establishes that the compelled speech doctrine applies to private parade organizers and protects their right to exclude messages they do not wish to convey.
Timmons established the balancing test for evaluating anti-fusion ballot laws.
The case applies and clarifies the doctrine of freedom of association in the context of ballot access and anti-fusion laws.
The Court established a test for evaluating whether primary election schemes violate political party associational rights.
The Court recognized and articulated the political party's First Amendment right to associate and select its own nominees without forced association with nonmembers.

Clingman v. Beaver established the principle that not all electoral laws burdening associational rights require strict scrutiny, and that minimal burdens justified by legitimate state interests are permissible.
The Court applied limited public forum doctrine to evaluate the university's all-comers policy for student organizations, holding that less restrictive scrutiny applies in such forums.

The case addressed the tension between the Christian Legal Society's freedom of association and the university's nondiscrimination policy, ultimately holding that the all-comers policy does not violate the First Amendment.
Nebraska Press Association v. Stuart established the framework for evaluating prior restraints on press publication in criminal proceedings.
Engel v. Vitale established the doctrine that government-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause.
Abington established the doctrine that public schools cannot sponsor mandatory religious exercises including Bible readings and prayer recitations.
Santa Fe established the principle that student-led prayer policies at school-sponsored events violate the Establishment Clause when they involve government authorization and endorsement.
