Connick clarified Pickering by adding public concern threshold
Garcetti narrowed Pickering/Connick: no protection for speech made pursuant to official duties
Lemon v. Kurtzman established the excessive entanglement doctrine as a key component of Establishment Clause jurisprudence.
Madsen established the doctrine governing content-neutral injunctions affecting First Amendment activity.
Zauderer established the doctrine protecting truthful attorney advertising from state discipline.
Zauderer established the two-part test for evaluating state regulation of attorney advertising.
The Court applied prior restraint doctrine to invalidate an injunction against distribution of handbills.
Schenck applies the content-neutral injunction standard to evaluate the constitutionality of buffer zones around abortion clinics.
Schenck applies content-neutral time, place, and manner doctrine to distinguish between fixed and floating buffer zones.
The Court applied the retaliatory litigation doctrine established in Power Systems to determine whether the employer's state-court civil suit violated the NLRA.
BE&K addresses scope of Petition Clause protection for retaliatory litigation
Abington established the doctrine that public schools cannot sponsor mandatory religious exercises including Bible readings and prayer recitations.
Alden established that states retain sovereign immunity in their own courts, extending the doctrine beyond federal court.
Benton applied and clarified the concurrent sentence doctrine, holding that it does not create a jurisdictional bar to appellate review but may survive as a rule of judicial convenience.
Betts established the right to counsel as a circumstance-dependent right, later superseded by Gideon's absolute right rule.
The Court established the doctrine that Petition Clause protection requires a matter of public concern.
The Court established a threshold test requiring that a public employee's petition relate to a matter of public concern.
Bowsher applies the doctrine of executive power to determine that the Comptroller General's deficit reduction functions are inherently executive in nature.
Branzburg applied and rejected the claimed reporter's privilege doctrine, holding no absolute privilege exists under the First Amendment.
Brewer v. Williams establishes that statements obtained in violation of the right to counsel are inadmissible, even absent interrogation.
Cruzan established the constitutional liberty interest in refusing unwanted medical treatment as a matter of substantive due process.
The Dolan exactions test is the operative mechanism for applying the takings exactions doctrine.
Garcetti v. Ceballos established the principle that public employee speech made pursuant to official duties is not protected by the First Amendment.
Georgia v. McCollum established that the Equal Protection Clause prohibits criminal defendants from engaging in purposeful racial discrimination in the exercise of peremptory challenges.
Harris v. McRae establishes the doctrine that restrictions on Medicaid funding for abortion do not violate the Due Process Clause or Religion Clauses.
Harris v. McRae establishes that there is no constitutional right to government funding of abortion services.
Hazelwood established the test for evaluating school editorial control over school-sponsored student speech.
Hazelwood established the foundational doctrine distinguishing school-sponsored student speech from other student speech contexts.
The Court upheld the constitutionality of Title II as applied to restaurants and public accommodations.
Horne established that Takings Clause applies equally to personal and real property.
Horne established that market participation conditions requiring property surrender constitute per se takings.
Horne established that returning net proceeds does not satisfy just compensation requirement for a taking.
Hudson narrows the exclusionary rule by holding that knock-and-announce violations do not automatically require suppression of evidence.

Humphrey's Executor clarified and limited the presidential removal power established in Myers v. United States by recognizing that statutory for-cause restrictions are constitutional when applied to officers exercising quasi-legislative or quasi-judicial functions.

The Court upheld the Federal Trade Commission Act's provision restricting removal to cases of inefficiency, neglect of duty, or malfeasance in office as a valid constitutional limitation on presidential removal power.
Hurley establishes that the compelled speech doctrine applies to private parade organizers and protects their right to exclude messages they do not wish to convey.
The Court established that the exclusionary rule does not apply in civil deportation proceedings based on a cost-benefit analysis specific to that context.
Johanns established the government speech doctrine as a framework distinguishing compelled funding of government speech from compelled funding of private speech.

Katzenbach establishes the doctrine that Congress has independent enforcement power under Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment to prohibit state laws without awaiting judicial determination of unconstitutionality.
The Court applies the principle that the Equal Protection Clause protects voting rights and limits state power to impose voting qualifications.
Kennedy explicitly abandons the Lemon test for Establishment Clause analysis, replacing it with a historical practices and understandings framework.
Kennedy establishes that the Free Exercise Clause protects personal religious observance from government reprisal outside the scope of official duties.
Kennedy establishes the historical practices and understandings framework as the new standard for Establishment Clause analysis.
Kennedy establishes that the Free Speech Clause protects personal expression by government employees outside the scope of official duties.

Lee v. Weisman established the principle that the Establishment Clause is violated by government-sponsored prayer at public school graduations based on both direct state involvement and subtle coercive effects on student participation.
The Court applied viewpoint discrimination doctrine to strike down the Disparagement Clause, finding that it discriminated based on viewpoint by targeting messages deemed offensive.
The Court considered and rejected the government speech doctrine as a basis for upholding the Disparagement Clause, holding that PTO approval of trademarks does not constitute government speech.
The Court applied commercial speech regulation doctrine and found the Disparagement Clause was not narrowly drawn to serve a substantial interest.
Meyer articulated and applied the doctrine of substantive due process liberty, defining the scope of liberty protected by the Fourteenth Amendment to include occupational freedom and parental rights.
Morse v. Frederick established the doctrine that student free speech rights are more limited than adult speech rights and do not extend to pro-drug messages.
Morse v. Frederick recognized schools' significant interest in preventing drug use as a basis for restricting student speech.
Nguyen articulates the intermediate scrutiny standard for gender-based classifications in the citizenship context.
The Court established that discriminatory intent, not disparate impact alone, is required to trigger heightened scrutiny under equal protection analysis.
Pickering established the foundational doctrine protecting public employee speech on matters of public concern.
Pierce v. Society of Sisters established the doctrine of parental liberty to direct children's education as a fundamental liberty interest protected by the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause.
The Court established the government speech doctrine as a framework for analyzing monument placement in public parks.
R.A.V. establishes the principle that content-based discrimination in speech regulation is unconstitutional even when applied to traditionally unprotected categories.
R.A.V. applies the categorical exceptions doctrine but clarifies that content-based discrimination within these categories is unconstitutional.
Bakke established the constitutional framework for affirmative action in higher education, permitting race as a factor in admissions while prohibiting rigid quotas.
Rosenberger established viewpoint discrimination as a violation of the Free Speech Clause in the context of public university funding decisions.
Rosenberger established the principle that government funding programs must operate on a viewpoint-neutral basis.
Shaw v. Reno established the doctrine that race-conscious redistricting is subject to equal protection scrutiny.
Shaw v. Reno established the test for identifying race-conscious redistricting that violates equal protection.
Skinner establishes that strict scrutiny applies to equal protection challenges involving fundamental rights, requiring the state to justify arbitrary classifications affecting such rights.
The nondiscrimination principle is a core component of dormant Commerce Clause doctrine.
Torres established the doctrine that Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity when exercising the power to raise and support the Armed Forces.
Torres applied and clarified the structural waiver test from PennEast in the context of federal war powers.
The doctrine of sovereign immunity abrogation via federal war powers is governed by the structural waiver test.
The Court established a balancing test for evaluating nondiscriminatory local ordinances under the dormant Commerce Clause.

The Court applied rational basis review to uphold the Filled Milk Act as a valid exercise of the commerce power, establishing that regulatory legislation affecting ordinary commercial transactions is presumed constitutional and will be upheld if it rests upon some rational basis.
Bond applies the Tenth Amendment doctrine by recognizing standing to challenge federal legislation on reserved powers grounds.
Bond establishes the doctrine that an individual defendant has standing to challenge the constitutionality of a federal statute.
Virginia v. Maryland established the doctrine that states retain sovereign authority under interstate compacts absent explicit divestment language.
Washington v. Glucksberg established the two-part test for identifying fundamental liberty interests protected by the Due Process Clause.
The case articulated the governing framework for substantive due process analysis of fundamental rights, emphasizing the requirement that rights be deeply rooted in history and tradition.
Wilkinson establishes the doctrine that procedural due process protections for prisoners in supermaximum placement decisions must be balanced against legitimate state penological interests.
Wilkinson establishes the specific test for evaluating procedural due process compliance in prisoner supermaximum security placement decisions.
Williamson applies rational basis review to uphold state economic regulation of optometry.
Wolf applies the selective incorporation framework established in Palko v. Connecticut, reaffirming that the Fourteenth Amendment incorporates only those rights implicit in ordered liberty.
Zelman established the private choice doctrine as the governing framework for Establishment Clause analysis of aid programs benefiting religious institutions.
Dun & Bradstreet established the doctrine that private concern speech receives less First Amendment protection than public concern speech.
Dun & Bradstreet established the doctrine that non-media defendants may be treated differently under First Amendment defamation law.
Texas v. Johnson established that flag burning constitutes expressive conduct protected by the First Amendment.
Texas v. Johnson established that the government cannot restrict the use of symbols to communicate only limited messages.
The Court applied viewpoint discrimination doctrine to strike down the LSC funding restriction.
The Court established the principle that funding programs designed to facilitate private speech cannot restrict that speech through viewpoint-based conditions.
The Court applied the material support doctrine to uphold the constitutionality of the AEDPA material support provision as amended.
The Court applied the vagueness doctrine to evaluate whether the material support statute provided fair notice and was sufficiently clear as applied to the plaintiffs' conduct.
Elonis clarified the true threat doctrine by establishing that subjective intent to threaten is required, rejecting the objective reasonable person standard previously applied.
Packingham applies the narrow tailoring doctrine to strike down North Carolina's sex offender social media access restriction.
The Court applied the free exercise doctrine to strike down the state's denial of unemployment benefits.
The Court applied the RFRA compelling interest test to evaluate whether the Government's burden on the religious practice satisfied the statutory requirements.

The Court applied the Religious Freedom Restoration Act to determine whether the Federal Government's enforcement of the Controlled Substances Act substantially burdened the sect's religious practice.
The Court established that state civil rights commissions must apply anti-discrimination laws neutrally with respect to religion.
The Court recognized that artistic creation can constitute sincere religious expression entitled to First Amendment and Free Exercise Clause protection.

The Court established that compelled speech doctrine applies to public accommodations law enforcement, prohibiting states from compelling expressive services that convey messages contrary to the service provider's beliefs.
The Court established that custom website design for weddings constitutes protected First Amendment speech.
Tanzin establishes that RFRA permits money damages against individual federal officials.
The Court applied the endorsement doctrine to evaluate whether the creche and menorah displays violated the Establishment Clause, examining the effect of each display in its specific context.
Agostini explicitly overruled Aguilar's presumption that public school teachers in parochial schools inevitably lead to religious indoctrination.

Agostini clarified that only excessive entanglement violates the Establishment Clause, rejecting the prior presumption that all church-state contact has a distinctly positive or negative impact on religion.
The plurality opinion articulated the neutrality principle as a framework for analyzing Establishment Clause challenges to neutral aid programs.
American Legion established the doctrine that longstanding religious monuments receive different Establishment Clause treatment than newly erected ones.
American Legion established the doctrine that historical practice and tradition guide Establishment Clause analysis, moving away from the Lemon test framework.
Shurtleff establishes the three-factor holistic inquiry test for determining government speech.
Shurtleff applies the government speech doctrine to determine that Boston's flag-raising program is not government speech.
Shurtleff applies the viewpoint discrimination doctrine to hold that Boston violated the Free Speech Clause by excluding the religious flag based on religious viewpoint.
Stenberg applied the undue burden standard from Casey to evaluate Nebraska's partial birth abortion statute and found it unconstitutional for lacking a health exception.

The Court applied the undue burden standard from Planned Parenthood v. Casey and held that the Partial-Birth Abortion Ban Act does not impose an undue burden because it applies only to a specific procedure.

The Court narrowed the health exception requirement by holding that Congress may omit a health exception when it determines the banned procedure is never medically necessary, rejecting the requirement that medical uncertainty alone necessitates a health exception.
Zablocki applies and reaffirms the fundamental right to marry as a constitutional protection under the Fourteenth Amendment.
M.L.B. establishes that the parental relationship is a fundamental right protected by the Fourteenth Amendment.
M.L.B. establishes that states cannot condition appellate access on ability to pay when fundamental rights are at stake.
The Court applied Establishment Clause doctrine to evaluate whether the Proclamation violated the constitutional prohibition on laws respecting an establishment of religion.
The Court applied and affirmed the president's broad discretionary authority under Section 1182(f) of the Immigration and Nationality Act.
Trump v. United States (2024) established the doctrine of Presidential immunity from criminal prosecution for official acts.
Trump v. United States (2024) established the doctrine that the President's exclusive constitutional authority is beyond criminal prosecution.
Trump v. United States (2024) established the doctrine distinguishing official from unofficial Presidential conduct for immunity purposes.
Trump v. United States (2024) established the Presidential immunity test for determining whether a former President is entitled to immunity from criminal prosecution.
Palazzolo applies and clarifies the Takings Clause doctrine by establishing that post-regulation acquisition of title does not bar a takings claim.
Palazzolo establishes the principle that a total deprivation takings claim requires the property to retain no significant economic worth.
The Court established a multi-factor balancing test for analyzing whether temporary moratoria constitute takings.
The Court applied and clarified the Takings Clause doctrine by rejecting categorical rules for temporary moratoria.
The Court established the multifactor balancing test for Fifth Amendment takings claims, rejecting categorical rules based on permanence.
The Court applied and clarified the Fifth Amendment Takings Clause by rejecting the categorical exception for temporary takings.
Knick established that property owners may bring Fifth Amendment takings claims in federal court under § 1983 without exhausting state remedies, overruling the Williamson County requirement.
The Court applied dormant Commerce Clause doctrine to invalidate New Jersey's waste importation ban as discriminatory against interstate commerce.
The Court applied the principle that state measures discriminating against interstate commerce and functioning as tariffs violate the Commerce Clause.
The Court articulated and applied the internal consistency test as the governing standard for dormant Commerce Clause analysis of state tax schemes.
Court applies the unconstitutional conditions doctrine to public employment, holding that government cannot deny re-employment based on protected speech.
Establishes that public employees have First Amendment protection against retaliation for speech even without tenure.
Establishes that procedural due process may apply to non-tenured public employees if they have a legitimate claim of entitlement to re-employment.
Goss established that education is a property interest protected by the Due Process Clause when a state extends it to its citizens.
Goss established minimum due process requirements for student suspensions.
Goss established the procedural test requiring notice and hearing before student suspension.
Bishop v. Wood establishes the doctrine that property interests in public employment are determined by state law and require an enforceable expectation of continued employment.

Zinermon narrows the Parratt rule by holding that it does not apply when the state has delegated authority to officials to make determinations affecting liberty interests and predeprivation safeguards were feasible.
Zinermon applies and clarifies the procedural due process doctrine in the context of mental hospital admissions.
Windsor established that federal law cannot deny same-sex couples federal marriage benefits available to opposite-sex couples under the Fifth Amendment's equal protection guarantee.
Windsor established that states have authority to define marital relationships and that federal law cannot undermine this authority.

The plurality applied equal protection doctrine to gender-based statutory rape laws, holding that differential treatment of males and females does not violate equal protection when the sexes are not similarly situated with respect to the risks addressed by the law.
Plyler established that undocumented children have a constitutional right to free public education under Equal Protection Clause.
The Court established a refined strict scrutiny test for race-based admissions that requires measurable compelling interests, avoidance of stereotyping, and a logical endpoint.
The Court established the doctrine that race-based admissions programs must satisfy strict scrutiny and cannot rely on race as a primary factor without meeting the three-prong test.
Darby established the doctrine that Congress may regulate interstate commerce to address labor conditions affecting such commerce.
Garcia explicitly overrules National League of Cities, holding that the 'traditional governmental function' test is unworkable and inconsistent with federalism principles.
Garcia repudiates the 'traditional governmental function' standard as a basis for determining state immunity under the Commerce Clause.
Atwater established the doctrine permitting warrantless arrests for minor criminal offenses when probable cause exists.
Georgia v. Randolph established the co-occupant consent search test as a new Fourth Amendment protection.
Georgia v. Randolph applies and refines the consent search doctrine by establishing a co-occupant veto rule.
Georgia v. Randolph applies the Fourth Amendment reasonableness requirement to evaluate consent searches in the presence of objecting co-occupants.
Navarette applies and clarifies the reasonable suspicion doctrine by establishing that anonymous tips with sufficient indicia of reliability may support a traffic stop.
Knick explicitly overruled Williamson County state-exhaustion requirement
Furman established that the Eighth Amendment, as applied to the states, prohibits arbitrary and capricious application of the death penalty.
Gregg established the modern constitutional framework for capital punishment.
Gregg established the bifurcated trial requirement as a constitutional safeguard.
Gregg established the multi-part test for constitutional capital sentencing.
Coker established the proportionality principle as a constraint on capital punishment under the Eighth Amendment.
Solem v. Helm established the proportionality doctrine as a constitutional constraint on sentencing under the Eighth Amendment.
Solem v. Helm established the three-part proportionality test for Eighth Amendment sentencing challenges.
Atkins established the categorical prohibition on executing mentally retarded offenders under the Eighth Amendment.
Roper explicitly overrules Stanford v. Kentucky's holding that executing minors does not violate the Eighth Amendment, based on changed evolving standards of decency.
Roper applies and reinforces the evolving standards of decency doctrine in the context of juvenile capital punishment.
Roper establishes the constitutional prohibition on executing juveniles under the Eighth Amendment.
Kennedy v. Louisiana established the doctrine that the Eighth Amendment limits capital punishment based on national consensus and excessiveness analysis.
Kennedy v. Louisiana established the two-part test for Eighth Amendment capital punishment analysis, building on prior precedent.
Graham v. Florida established the categorical Eighth Amendment test for evaluating sentences imposed on classes of offenders.
Graham v. Florida established the doctrine that the Eighth Amendment prohibits life sentences without parole for juveniles convicted of non-homicidal crimes.
Graham v. Florida applied and refined the categorical analysis framework for Eighth Amendment challenges to sentences imposed on entire classes of offenders.
Miller v. Alabama established the doctrine that mandatory life without parole sentences for juveniles violate the Eighth Amendment's prohibition on cruel and unusual punishment.
Moran applies the Miranda rights doctrine to determine the validity of waivers when a suspect is unaware of attorney contact efforts.
Padilla applies and clarifies the scope of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel by requiring counsel to advise on deportation consequences.
Missouri v. Frye establishes the framework for evaluating ineffective assistance claims in the plea negotiation context.
Missouri v. Frye applies and extends the established right to effective assistance of counsel to the plea negotiation context.
Lafler v. Cooper established the specific test for evaluating ineffective assistance of counsel claims in the plea bargain context.
Lafler applies the doctrine of right to effective assistance of counsel to the context of plea bargain advice.
Barron v. Baltimore established the foundational principle that the Bill of Rights does not apply to the states.
The case applied the Fifth Amendment's just compensation clause to determine its scope, holding it applicable only to federal government action.
Duncan established the fundamental right to jury trial in criminal cases and its incorporation against the states.
McDonald established the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms as an individual right applicable to the states.
McDonald applied the Fourteenth Amendment incorporation doctrine to the Second Amendment, holding that the Due Process Clause is the appropriate mechanism for incorporation.
McDonald articulated the test for incorporation: a right must be fundamental to the Nation's scheme of ordered liberty or deeply rooted in the Nation's history and tradition.
The Court applied the Tenth Amendment reserved powers doctrine to invalidate the Keating-Owen Child Labor Act.
The Court applied the production-commerce distinction to hold that Congress lacked power to regulate the production of goods, even when those goods would be shipped in interstate commerce.
Schechter Poultry Corp. v. United States established the non-delegation doctrine as a constitutional limit on Congress's power to delegate legislative authority.
The Court established the intelligible principle test as the standard for evaluating whether a delegation of legislative authority is constitutional.
The Court established the principle that Congress may regulate intrastate economic activity as part of a class of activities substantially affecting interstate commerce.

Missouri v. Holland established the treaty power doctrine by holding that the treaty power, combined with congressional implementation, may regulate matters within the national interest even if those matters would otherwise be reserved to the states.
The Court applied the Supremacy Clause to establish that treaty provisions are the supreme law of the land and are not subject to state-level constitutional challenge.

Murphy clarifies that the anticommandeering doctrine applies equally to prohibitions on state legislative action as to affirmative commands, establishing that the distinction between compelling a state to enact legislation and prohibiting a state from enacting new laws is empty.

schema_issues_found.description: ESTABLISHES edge type implies positive doctrine creation. Valentine established a rule by denying protection, not by articulating a framework. Need holding_type attribute on Case nodes (affirmative | by_negation) or a separate ESTABLISHES_BY_NEGATION edge type.
Virginia Pharmacy did not explicitly overrule Valentine but is directly incompatible with it. OVERRULES edge needs an overrule_type attribute: explicit | implicit | effective.
schema_issues_found.resolution: OVERRULES edge now has overrule_type attribute (implicit/explicit/effective). Virginia Pharmacy → Valentine correctly modeled with overrule_type='implicit'.

schema_issues_found.description: MODIFIES is doing too much work. Posadas expanded Central Hudson deference, 44 Liquormart narrowed it, Sorrell complicated it without resolving it. Need a direction attribute: narrows | expands | complicates | repudiates | clarifies. Without this, MODIFIES edges carry no directionality and the research tool cannot tell users whether doctrine tightened or loosened.

A case can have both APPLIES and MODIFIES edges to the same target. Ambiguous whether MODIFIES should subsume APPLIES or coexist with it. Recommend: MODIFIES implies application. Remove redundant APPLIES edge when MODIFIES is present.
schema_issues_found.resolution: Schema validator (E011) now catches APPLIES+MODIFIES collision. MODIFIES subsumes APPLIES is enforced programmatically.

schema_issues_found.description: Prong 4 has been interpreted differently over time — some justices push for least restrictive means, the majority applies reasonable fit. The prong text itself is contested. Static prong sub-schema cannot represent this evolution. Consider adding a valid_from / valid_until window to individual prongs, or a contested: true flag with a notes field.

INCORPORATES is not in the original edge type list — added here ad hoc for the 14th Amendment incorporation relationship. Needs to be added to the formal schema as a ConstitutionalProvision-to-ConstitutionalProvision edge type.
schema_issues_found.resolution: INCORPORATES is now a formal edge type in the schema with validation rules. Used for 14th Amendment incorporation relationships.

schema_issues_found.description: Dissents and concurrences as doctrinal sources. The Brandenburg test was established as majority law in 1969, but its intellectual substance originated in Holmes's Abrams dissent (1919) and Brandeis's Whitney concurrence (1927). The schema has no mechanism to represent a non-majority opinion as a doctrinal source. ESTABLISHES edges run from Case nodes, and source_case_id on DoctrinalTest points to the case that made the test majority law — but this conflates 'legal establishment' with 'intellectual origin.' Proposed fix: add an optional intellectual_source field to DoctrinalTest (array of {case_id, opinion_type: majority|concurrence|dissent, author, note}). Alternatively, add an INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES edge type between Case nodes with an opinion_type attribute.

schema_issues_found.location: majority_author: null on Brandenburg and Hess (Case nodes)
schema_issues_found.description: Per curiam opinions have no majority author, but the schema's majority_author field is a plain string with no formal null handling documented. More importantly, the schema has no way to record concurring opinions as doctrinal sources or to note that Black/Douglas's Brandenburg concurrences argued for a broader rule. The majority_author field needs: (1) explicit null semantics for per curiam; (2) an optional concurrences array with author and significance fields, parallel to the dissent pattern. This is related to Issue #7 but distinct — it affects the Case node schema, not just the edge layer.

Cross-slice node dependency gap. Several doctrines modeled in this slice originated in cases outside the slice's scope (public_concern_doctrine originates in Connick v. Myers, 1983; true_threats_doctrine as a category originates in R.A.V. v. St. Paul, 1992 and Virginia v. Black, 2003). The ESTABLISHES edges for these doctrines cannot be modeled without case nodes for Connick, R.A.V., and Virginia v. Black. The schema has no formal mechanism for a node to declare a dependency on a case node in another slice, or to represent a doctrine as 'partially established' pending a future slice addition. Proposed fix: add an established_by_pending field to Doctrine and DoctrinalTest nodes — an array of {case_id, slice_id, note} — documenting out-of-scope establishment cases. Alternatively, require stub Case nodes (id and citation only, status: stub) for any case referenced by an ESTABLISHES edge.

Single valid_from cannot represent layered doctrinal development. The true threats doctrine has two distinct temporal layers: (1) the category itself (unprotected since at least R.A.V., 1992); (2) the subjective mens rea requirement (Counterman, 2023). The node's valid_from can only capture one of these. This is a generalization of Commercial Speech Issue #5 (prong evolution) — here the problem is at the doctrine node level, not the prong level. A doctrine can have elements that became operative at different times. Proposed fix: add an elements_history array to Doctrine nodes, parallel to the prong valid_from/valid_until pattern in DoctrinalTest, allowing individual elements to carry their own validity windows.

APPLIES vs. MODIFIES tension on context-extension cases. Claiborne Hardware applied the Brandenburg test in a civil context for the first time. The schema rules say APPLIES is for cases that apply a doctrine 'without modifying it' — but extending a doctrine's applicability to a new legal context (civil vs. criminal) is a meaningful doctrinal development that APPLIES doesn't capture, while MODIFIES (which requires a direction attribute) seems too strong. 'Expands' on the incitement_doctrine MODIFIES edge captures the context extension, but the test node gets only APPLIES. Consider whether context-extension deserves its own direction value: 'extends' (applies doctrine in a new legal context without changing its prongs).

No RELATED_TO edges exist yet across either slice. The schema defines RELATED_TO as a cross-edge for non-hierarchical doctrinal relationships between Area nodes. Commercial speech and core political speech are sibling Area nodes under free_speech, but their relationship is more than just shared parentage — the public concern doctrine (core political speech) and the commercial speech doctrine share the same axis of analysis (what kind of speech is this?), and Sorrell (commercial speech) imports reasoning from the content-based restriction doctrine (core political speech). A RELATED_TO edge between commercial_speech and core_political_speech would be meaningful — but the schema gives no guidance on when RELATED_TO is appropriate vs. redundant vs. misleading. Needs editorial criteria.

No node type for legal instruments. Prior restraint doctrine applies differently depending on whether the government instrument is a licensing scheme, a judicial injunction, or an administrative censorship order. The schema has no way to represent instrument type as a structured concept — it can only appear in free-text notes. This matters for query accuracy: a walk looking for 'cases where prior restraint test was applied' will return Near (licensing scheme), Pentagon Papers (injunction sought by DOJ), and Madsen (content-neutral injunction) without distinguishing them, even though they trigger different constitutional standards. Proposed fix: add an InstrumentType enum to the schema — licensing_scheme, judicial_injunction, administrative_order, statute, regulation — as an optional attribute on edges between Case nodes and Doctrine/Test nodes.

Inadequate procedural posture representation. The schema's Case node status enum (good_law, overruled, limited, disputed) has no value for mooted, dismissed, cert_denied, or never_reviewed. US v. Progressive is mooted and was never reviewed on appeal — its district court holding has no precedential force — but the schema cannot represent this distinction. A mooted unreviewd district court holding and an affirmed SCOTUS decision are structurally identical in the current schema. Proposed fixes: (1) add status values: mooted, dismissed, cert_denied, per_curiam_affirmed; (2) add a precedential_weight field: binding, persuasive, none; (3) add an appellate_history array to Case nodes. The court field also needs expansion: currently the schema implies scotus as primary, but the graph needs district, circuit (by number), and state court levels.

Fractured concurrences as doctrinal source — extreme case. Pentagon Papers produced nine separate opinions with no majority. The per curiam said only that the government failed to meet its burden; the operational content of what would constitute a sufficient showing is assembled from six non-majority concurrences. This is a more extreme instance of Issue #7 (Holmes Abrams dissent / Brandeis Whitney concurrence as doctrinal sources). In Brandenburg, at least there was a majority opinion that stated the test. In Pentagon Papers there is no majority opinion at all — the test is a synthesis of concurrences. The schema's ESTABLISHES edge from near_v_minnesota to the test is accurate for the test's origin; the MODIFIES(clarifies) edge from Pentagon Papers captures something, but doesn't represent that Pentagon Papers is the primary operational source of the test's second prong. Proposed fix: extends Issue #7 fix — the intellectual_source or INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES mechanism must handle not just dissents/concurrences but cases where the per curiam itself provides no substantive doctrine.

Conditional governance — GOVERNED_BY cannot express instrument-type conditions. The prior restraint Area is governed by two different tests depending on the government instrument: the classic prior restraint test (Near/Pentagon Papers) applies to licensing schemes and government-sought injunctions; the Madsen test applies to content-neutral court injunctions against specific parties. The current schema cannot express this conditionality — both GOVERNED_BY edges exist but the graph cannot tell a query 'use Madsen when the instrument is a content-neutral injunction, use prior_restraint_test otherwise.' Proposed fix: add an optional condition attribute to GOVERNED_BY edges — a structured object with instrument_type, context, and note fields. This is related to Issue #14 (no instrument type node) and would be resolved together.

First use of RELATED_TO edge type — editorial criteria needed. This is the first RELATED_TO edge in the graph. The edge is justified (prior restraint and core political speech protect the same speech through complementary doctrinal mechanisms) but the schema provides no editorial criteria for when RELATED_TO is appropriate. Without criteria, contributors will use it inconsistently — either over-connecting everything related or under-using it. This is Issue #12 from the core political speech slice. The prior restraint / core political speech relationship provides a concrete example to anchor the criteria: RELATED_TO is appropriate when two Area nodes (1) share constitutional grounding, (2) have overlapping case law, and (3) operate as complementary rather than hierarchical doctrinal frameworks. Proposed spec addition: formalize these three criteria as the RELATED_TO editorial standard.

Meta-test problem: a DoctrinalTest that is a threshold precondition to another DoctrinalTest. The public_figure_classification_test is not applied to the merits of a defamation claim — it is applied first to determine which other test governs the claim. The schema has no way to represent a conditional relationship between DoctrinalTests: 'if public_figure_classification_test returns public_official or public_figure, then actual_malice_test applies; if it returns private_figure, then press_defamation_private_figure_doctrine applies.' This is a new pattern. Proposed fix: add a PRECONDITION_TO edge type between DoctrinalTest nodes, or add a conditional_test field to DoctrinalTest that specifies what governs when the test returns each classification. This is related to but distinct from the GOVERNED_BY condition attribute (which handles instrument-type conditionality, not test-sequencing conditionality).

Constitutional constraint on common law cause of action — new structural pattern. All prior slices modeled government restrictions on speech (statutes, court orders, administrative licensing). Sullivan constitutionalized a state common law cause of action (defamation) by imposing a constitutional standard on what private plaintiffs must prove. This is structurally different: the First Amendment is constraining a private lawsuit, not a government restriction. The schema's GOVERNS_BY and ESTABLISHES edges model doctrine that governs areas of law — but they don't capture the distinction between doctrine that restricts government action and doctrine that constrains private litigation. A research tool query for 'what governs this area' returns both types without distinguishing them, which is misleading for practice. Proposed fix: add a constraint_type field to Doctrine nodes: government_restriction | litigation_constraint | structural_rule.

Federal constitutional floor with state law superstructure. The private figure defamation doctrine sets a constitutional minimum (no strict liability, fault required) but explicitly leaves states free to set higher standards. The schema models federal constitutional law but this doctrine is incomplete without reference to the state law it constrains and the state variation it permits. The doctrine node correctly describes the federal floor but the graph has no way to represent the relationship between the federal floor and the state law superstructure. This is a Phase 2 boundary condition. Proposed fix: add a federalism_structure attribute to Doctrine nodes: federal_floor (federal minimum, state law operates above), federal_ceiling (federal maximum, state law operates below), federal_preemption (federal law displaces state law), federal_exclusive (state has no role). This is a relatively common constitutional law pattern and will recur in equal protection, due process, and other 14th Amendment doctrine.

Partially superseded plurality opinion — status enum inadequate. Butts is in an unusual status: Harlan's plurality holding (highly unreasonable conduct standard) was superseded by Gertz, but the case is not overruled because Warren's concurrence position (full actual malice for public figures) was adopted by Gertz. The case is simultaneously: (1) limited as to Harlan's plurality standard, and (2) the source of the rule adopted by Gertz via Warren's concurrence. The status: limited captures the first but not the second. There is no status value for 'partially superseded with surviving doctrinal contribution via concurrence.' Proposed fix: add a doctrinal_survival note field to Case nodes, and consider whether status needs a partially_superseded value distinct from limited.

Multi-factor standard without clean prongs — Doctrine vs. DoctrinalTest boundary. The opinion-fact distinction involves a contextual multi-factor analysis (is the statement objectively verifiable? does context signal opinion? is the statement capable of being proven true or false?) that courts apply as a totality-of-circumstances test rather than a sequential prong-by-prong analysis. It fits neither the Doctrine model (too structured) nor the DoctrinalTest model (lacks clean numbered prongs and sequential burden structure). Currently modeled as Doctrine. The promotion rule criteria suggest it might need its own treatment: it recurs across multiple areas (defamation, IIED, false light), has its own lineage, and is independently queried. Proposed fix: add a test_form field to DoctrinalTest: sequential_prongs (standard multi-prong test like Central Hudson or Brandenburg) | classification_framework (like public figure taxonomy) | totality_of_circumstances (like opinion-fact analysis) | balancing (like Mathews). This would allow DoctrinalTest to represent a wider range of structured legal analysis.

schema_issues_found.location: free_exercise_doctrine (Doctrine node) / employment_division_v_smith_1990 (Case node) / smith_test (DoctrinalTest node)
schema_issues_found.description: Intra-Court challenge to a precedent — dispute_source needed. The Smith rule is formally good law but at least four sitting justices have explicitly called for its reconsideration (Barrett, Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch in Fulton concurrences). The status: disputed flag is correct but conflates four distinct types of doctrinal instability: (1) circuit split on application, (2) academic consensus against the rule, (3) intra-Court challenge by sitting justices, and (4) legislative response (RFRA). These are meaningfully different for a research tool — a circuit split means lower courts are divided; an intra-Court challenge means the Supreme Court may revisit the rule at any time. Proposed fix: add dispute_source enum to status fields: circuit_split | academic | intra_court_challenge | legislative_response | post_overruling_transition. Multiple values should be permitted.

Threshold classification framework — second instance of Issue #19 meta-test problem. The belief/conduct distinction is a precondition to Free Exercise analysis, not a test applied to the merits. It determines whether the conduct is even subject to constitutional protection analysis. Like the public figure taxonomy (Issue #19), it is a gateway classification, not a merits test. The GOVERNED_BY edge is accurate but cannot represent that belief_conduct_distinction is a precondition to both sherbert_test and smith_test. The PRECONDITION_TO edge type proposed in Issue #19 would handle this. This second instance confirms the pattern is recurring and the fix is needed before Equal Protection (where tiered scrutiny is the same kind of threshold classification).

Constitutional test restored by statute — schema cannot represent this. The Sherbert compelling interest test was significantly curtailed by Smith (1990) as a constitutional matter, but Congress responded with RFRA (1993), which restored the Sherbert test as a statutory standard for federal laws. City of Boerne v. Flores (1997) struck down RFRA as applied to state laws. The graph models the constitutional Sherbert test. It cannot represent that: (1) the constitutional test was narrowed, (2) a statute restored the narrowed test for federal laws, and (3) that statute was partially invalidated, leaving the test operative only for federal laws. This is a three-layer constitutional-statutory interaction that requires the Phase 2 statute layer to model completely. Proposed interim fix: add a statutory_restoration note field to DoctrinalTest nodes documenting when a statute has restored a limited constitutional test.

Totality-of-circumstances analysis without clean prongs — second instance of Issue #23. The determination of whether a law is neutral and generally applicable draws on multiple factors (text, history, enforcement patterns, structure of exceptions) that courts apply holistically rather than sequentially. This is the same structural problem as the opinion-fact distinction (Issue #23). The neutrality_general_applicability_doctrine is modeled as a Doctrine rather than a DoctrinalTest because its factors lack the sequential burden structure of a formal test. The test_form field proposed in Issue #23 (totality_of_circumstances) would apply here. This second instance confirms the pattern is sufficiently common to warrant the field addition in v0.5.

Conditional GOVERNED_BY edges with overlapping validity windows — temporal ambiguity. Both the Sherbert test and the Smith test GOVERN the free_exercise Area simultaneously, with overlapping valid_from dates and no valid_until on either. This is accurate — both tests remain operative but in different circumstances — but creates graph ambiguity: a query for 'what test currently governs free exercise?' returns both tests without indicating that Smith governs neutral general laws and Sherbert governs everything else. The condition attribute on GOVERNED_BY edges (added in v0.4 for instrument-type conditionality) is used here to document the conditionality, but the condition attribute is optional and not queryable as a structured field. Proposed fix: promote condition.context to a structured enum rather than a free string, so queries can filter GOVERNED_BY edges by governing condition.

First overruled DoctrinalTest with valid_until — temporal query behavior. The Lemon test is the first DoctrinalTest in the graph with status: overruled and valid_until populated. The temporal model handles this correctly at the data level, but it surfaces a query design requirement: any query for 'what test currently governs this area' must filter by valid_until: null on GOVERNED_BY edges and status: active on DoctrinalTest nodes, or it will return the Lemon test as a governing test. The validator and pipeline must enforce this. In Neo4j, the canonical QT-1 query (what test governs this area) must include WHERE t.status <> 'overruled' AND e.valid_until IS NULL. This is the first instance demonstrating that temporal filtering is required at the query layer, not just the data layer.
schema_issues_found.resolution: Lemon test has valid_until='2022-06-27' set. Kennedy v. Bremerton OVERRULES edge with overrule_type='explicit' correctly models temporal boundary.

schema_issues_found.description: Interpretive methodology modeled as Doctrine — inadequate node type. The historical practices and understandings standard is not a doctrine in the conventional sense — it is an interpretive methodology that tells courts how to analyze Establishment Clause questions rather than what the answer is. It lacks the structural features of a Doctrine (substantive rule that lawyers apply) and lacks the structured prongs of a DoctrinalTest. The schema has no node type for interpretive methodology. Currently modeled as Doctrine as the best available fit, but the test_form field proposed in Issue #23 (adding totality_of_circumstances, classification_framework values) should also add interpretive_methodology as a DoctrinalTest test_form value, or the schema should consider an InterpretiveFramework node type for a future version.

schema_issues_found.location: establishment_clause (Area node) / establishment_clause_doctrine (Doctrine node) / historical_practices_doctrine (Doctrine node)
schema_issues_found.description: Post-overruling transition — status: disputed insufficient. The Establishment Clause doctrine is in a state that the current schema cannot precisely represent: the old governing test (Lemon) is explicitly overruled; the new governing standard (historical practices) is stated but its operational content is actively being developed in lower courts; multiple circuits are applying the new standard with inconsistent results. This is categorically different from status: disputed (contested doctrine with defined content) or status: active (settled doctrine). It is a third state: the governing framework changed, the replacement exists, but the replacement has no settled application yet. The dispute_source enum proposed in Issue #24 (post_overruling_transition) would capture this. The schema needs a way to represent that a doctrine is in active post-overruling development, including: (1) which cases are overruled, (2) what the replacement is, (3) when the transition began, (4) what aspects are still being settled.

RELATED_TO relationship_type attribute needed. The Religion Clauses' relationship is qualitatively different from prior restraint/core political speech. Prior restraint and core political speech are complementary — they protect the same interest through different mechanisms. The Establishment and Free Exercise Clauses are structurally tensioned — they protect the same interest through opposing commands, and maximizing one necessarily limits the other. Laws that accommodate religion risk Establishment Clause challenge; neutral laws risk Free Exercise challenge. This structural tension generates the RFRA problem and the accommodation doctrine. The RELATED_TO edge type needs an optional relationship_type attribute: complementary | structurally_tensioned | historically_linked | parallel_development. This would allow the research tool to explain not just that two areas are related but how.

Companion cases decided simultaneously — DISTINGUISHES edge semantics imperfect. Van Orden and McCreary County were decided on the same day by the same Court with opposite results. The DISTINGUISHES edge type was designed for later cases limiting earlier cases to their facts — a precedent/precedent-limiter temporal relationship. Using DISTINGUISHES for two simultaneously-decided companion cases is technically accurate (the cases do limit each other to their facts) but semantically odd: neither case distinguishes the other in the conventional sense of a later court limiting an earlier holding. Proposed fix: add a companion_case attribute to Case nodes (array of companion case IDs) for cases decided simultaneously on the same question. Alternatively, add COMPANION_TO as a new edge type for simultaneously-decided companion cases.

Dual constitutional grounding — Area GROUNDED_IN cannot distinguish sub-doctrine grounding. The freedom_of_association Area has a GROUNDED_IN edge to amendment_1 (covering expressive association). But intimate_association_doctrine within the same Area is grounded in amendment_14_section_1 (substantive due process), not amendment_1. The current model handles this by adding a Doctrine-level GROUNDED_IN edge from intimate_association_doctrine to amendment_14_section_1 — the first use of this edge pattern in the graph. This is a valid v0.4 schema use (GROUNDED_IN is permitted from Doctrine to ConstitutionalProvision) but it surfaces an ambiguity: a consumer of the graph traversing from amendment_1 through GROUNDED_IN to freedom_of_association would not reach intimate_association_doctrine, and vice versa for amendment_14_section_1. The dual-grounding problem requires documentation in the spec: when a sub-doctrine has different constitutional grounding than its parent Area, use a Doctrine-level GROUNDED_IN edge and note the distinction.

Porous boundary between compelled association and compelled speech — cases in two doctrinal lines simultaneously. Hurley is simultaneously a compelled speech case (core political speech doctrine — government cannot compel a speaker to carry a message they reject) and a compelled association case (freedom of association — parade organizers cannot be forced to include a group with a conflicting message). The case sits in both doctrinal lines. The schema models this correctly through edges to doctrine nodes in both areas, but there is no way to represent that a case is 'primarily' in one doctrinal line while 'secondarily' in another. The RELATED_TO edge between freedom_of_association and core_political_speech captures the relationship at the Area level, but the case-level overlap is not explicitly represented. Proposed fix: add a primary_doctrine field to Case nodes identifying the primary doctrinal area the case is associated with, and allow the edges to reflect secondary doctrinal associations.

Exacting scrutiny as a distinct standard — scrutiny level enum inadequate. The exacting scrutiny standard for disclosure requirements is distinct from both intermediate scrutiny and strict scrutiny — it requires narrow tailoring (like strict scrutiny) but applies to a less demanding category of governmental burden than content-based speech restrictions. The current scrutiny_level enum (strict | intermediate | rational_basis | balancing | per_se) does not have a value for exacting scrutiny. Currently mapped to strict as the closest fit, but this is imprecise — it will cause the research tool to treat exacting scrutiny cases the same as strict scrutiny cases, which is doctrinally incorrect. Proposed fix: add exacting to the scrutiny_level enum. This would also apply to other disclosure and association contexts.

Constitutional doctrine with significant statutory dimension — Phase 2 boundary. The Noerr-Pennington doctrine is rooted in the First Amendment Petition Clause, but its primary application is as an immunity from federal antitrust liability under the Sherman Act. The constitutional grounding is thin — courts have sometimes rested the doctrine on statutory interpretation of the Sherman Act rather than on the First Amendment directly. The schema models it as a constitutional petition doctrine because it has a constitutional dimension, but the fuller picture requires the statutory layer (Phase 2) to represent the Sherman Act relationship. This is the first instance of a doctrine that sits primarily at the intersection of constitutional law and a federal statute, where the constitutional grounding may be secondary to the statutory interpretation question.

Thin doctrinal area — Area node represents a real category but the doctrine is constitutionally underdeveloped. The Petition Clause has generated far less Supreme Court doctrine than the Speech, Press, Assembly, or Religion Clauses. Most petition activity is analyzed under speech doctrine, with the Petition Clause cited as additional support rather than the primary ground. The Area node is appropriate for taxonomic completeness but the graph's representation of petition doctrine is necessarily less rich than other 1A areas. This surfaces a schema question: should the graph represent the relative doctrinal development of different areas? Currently all Area nodes appear structurally equivalent regardless of how developed their doctrine is. A doctrinal_development_level field (nascent | developing | mature | contested) on Area nodes would allow the research tool to signal that some areas have richer doctrine than others.

Dual constitutional grounding through different legal theories — theory attribute needed on GROUNDED_IN edges. The intimate association doctrine is grounded in both amendment_1 (First Amendment implicit association) and amendment_14_section_1 (substantive due process liberty) through two different legal theories. Two GROUNDED_IN edges correctly capture the dual grounding, but the schema has no way to represent which legal theory each grounding reflects. A researcher looking at the graph cannot tell whether the amendment_14 GROUNDED_IN edge reflects incorporation (14th Amendment applying 1st Amendment to states) or substantive due process (14th Amendment's own liberty clause). Proposed fix: add an optional theory attribute to GROUNDED_IN edges: incorporation | substantive_due_process | structural_inference | textual | historical. This would make doctrinal theories navigable rather than implicit in the notes field.

Third instance of threshold classification meta-test problem (Issues #19, #25). The expressive association doctrine requires a threshold determination — does this organization engage in protected expressive activity? — before the merits analysis applies. This is the same structural pattern as the public figure taxonomy (Issue #19) and the belief/conduct distinction (Issue #25): a classification framework that preconditions which analysis follows. Three confirmed instances across three different doctrinal areas (press freedom, free exercise, association) establishes this as a systemic schema gap. The PRECONDITION_TO edge type proposed in Issue #19 is now confirmed as a genuine need. Adding it to the v0.5 spec is high priority before modeling Equal Protection, where the strict/intermediate/rational basis tiers are the most consequential instance of this pattern.

Anonymous speech sub-doctrine not yet modeled — floating connection. The compelled disclosure doctrine (NAACP v. Alabama, Americans for Prosperity) is substantively connected to the anonymous speech doctrine (Talley v. California, 1960; McIntyre v. Ohio Elections Commission, 1995) in the core political speech area. Both protect the ability to engage in expressive activity without government-compelled identification. The RELATED_TO edge from freedom_of_association to core_political_speech captures this relationship at the Area level, but the specific doctrinal connection (compelled_disclosure_doctrine ↔ anonymous_speech_doctrine) cannot be modeled because anonymous_speech is not yet a separate doctrine node in the core_political_speech slice. The core_political_speech slice should be expanded to include the anonymous speech doctrine as a sub-doctrine, at which point a RELATED_TO edge between compelled_disclosure_doctrine and the anonymous_speech_doctrine node would make the connection explicit.

Cross-field doctrinal placement — first doctrine with a primary application outside constitutional law. The Noerr-Pennington doctrine originated as a Sherman Act / antitrust doctrine and is primarily litigated in antitrust cases. Its constitutional grounding in the First Amendment Petition Clause was only made explicit in California Motor Transport (1972). The schema places it in freedom_of_petition (correct for constitutional law purposes) but it is equally a doctrine of antitrust law. When Phase 2 models antitrust doctrine, Noerr-Pennington will need to appear there as well. Proposed fix: add secondary_area_ids (string[]) field to Doctrine and DoctrinalTest nodes, parallel to secondary_parents on Area nodes. This would allow a doctrine to have a primary constitutional law home (area_id) and secondary homes in other fields (secondary_area_ids: ['antitrust']). This is the doctrinal equivalent of the DAG structure at the Area level — doctrines, like areas, can genuinely belong in multiple contexts.
